Ayahs

تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ نَذِيرًا (١)
Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion to His servant that he may be a warner to all the nations. (1)
ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَدًا وَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُۥ شَرِيكٌ فِى ٱلْمُلْكِ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُۥ تَقْدِيرًا (٢)
He, to whom belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth, and who did not take any offspring, nor has He any partner in sovereignty, and He created everything and determined it in a precise measure. (2)

Content of Surah al-Furqan

Since this Surah is among the Meccan Surahs [^1], its primary emphasis is on issues related to the Origin (Monotheism) and the Resurrection, the explanation of the Prophethood of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), the struggle against polytheism and polytheists, and warning against the dire consequences of disbelief, idol worship, and sin.

In reality, this Surah consists of three sections:

The first section, which forms the beginning of this Surah, severely shatters the logic of the polytheists, addresses their excuses and responds to them, and warns them of Allah's punishment, the reckoning of Judgment Day, and the painful penalties of Hell. Following that, it recounts portions of the history of previous nations who were afflicted with the harshest calamities and punishments due to their opposition to the call of the Prophets, serving as a lesson for these stubborn and truth-averting polytheists.

In the second section, to complete this discussion, it mentions a portion of the proofs of Monotheism and the signs of Allah's greatness in the world of creation—ranging from the light of the sun to the gloom and darkness of the night, the blowing of winds, the falling of rain, the revival of dead lands, the creation of the heavens and the earth in six periods, and the creation of the sun and the moon and their orderly movement through the celestial constellations, and the like.

In fact, the first section defines the concept of "La ilaha" (There is no god) and the second section defines "illa Allah" (except Allah).

The third section is a very comprehensive and beautiful summary of the attributes of the true believers ('Ibad al-Rahman) and the sincere servants of Allah. By comparing them with the prejudiced, excuse-making, and defiled disbelievers mentioned in the first section, the position of both groups becomes completely clear. As we shall see, these attributes are a collection of beliefs, righteous deeds, struggle against desires, possessing sufficient awareness, and social commitment and responsibility.

The name of this Surah is taken from its first verse, which refers to the Quran as "al-Furqan" (The Criterion for separating truth from falsehood).

The Virtue of Surah al-Furqan

In a hadith from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Furqan (and reflects upon its content and draws inspiration from it in belief and action) will be resurrected on the Day of Judgment while being among the ranks of those who believe in the Resurrection—those who were certain that the Hour is coming and that Allah will bring the dead back to a new life." [^1]

In another hadith from Ishaq ibn 'Ammar, it is narrated from Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja'far (peace be upon him) that he said to him:

"Do not abandon the recitation of Surah 'Blessed is He who sent down the Criterion (al-Furqan) upon His servant,' for whoever recites it every night, Allah will never punish him, will not call him to account, and his abode shall be in the Highest Paradise (al-Firdaws al-A'la)." [^2]

As we will see in the commentary of this Surah, the attributes of the sincere servants of Allah are described in such a way that whoever truly recites it with soul and heart and aligns their attributes and actions with it, their place is indeed the Highest Paradise.

[^1]: Some commentators insist that three verses of this Surah (68, 69, and 70) were revealed in Medina, perhaps because these few verses discuss rulings such as the prohibition of murder and adultery. However, careful examination of the verses before and after them clearly shows a completely connected and cohesive sequence describing the special servants of Allah ('Ibad al-Rahman) and their qualities. Therefore, it appears that the entire Surah was revealed in Mecca. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal by Saduq, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 2.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً (1) الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ وَ خَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً (2)

Commentary

The Supreme Criterion of Recognition

This Surah begins with the word "Tabaraka," which is derived from the root "Barakah" (blessing). We know that for something to have Barakah means it possesses permanence, goodness, and complete benefit.

It says: "Blessed and imperishable is the One Who sent down the Criterion (al-Furqan) upon His servant, that he may be a warner to the worlds." (تَبارَكَ الَّذِي نَزَّلَ الْفُرْقانَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً) [^1]

It is interesting that the blessed nature of the Lord's Essence is introduced through the revelation of the Furqan—that is, the Quran which separates truth from falsehood. This indicates that the greatest goodness and blessing is for a human to have a means of recognition—distinguishing truth from falsehood—in his hands!

It is also noteworthy that "Furqan" is sometimes used to mean the "Quran," sometimes to mean the miracles that clarify truth from falsehood, and sometimes it has been used regarding the Torah. However, here, given the context in this verse and the subsequent verses, it refers to the Quran.

In some narrations, when Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) was asked if there is a difference between "Quran" and "Furqan," he said: "Quran refers to the entirety of this Heavenly Book, and Furqan refers to the clear, decisive verses (al-Ayat al-Muhkamat)." [^2] This statement does not contradict the fact that all verses of the Quran are Furqan; rather, it means that the decisive verses of the Quran are a clearer and more prominent manifestation of the Furqan and the separation of truth from falsehood.

[^1]: In Volume 6, page 208, under verse 56 of Surah al-A'raf, the original root of "Barakah" is explained. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 3, p. 155.

The blessing of "Criterion and Recognition" is so important that the Holy Quran mentions it as a great reward for the God-fearing: "O you who believe! If you fear Allah, He will grant you a Criterion (Furqan)..." [^1] Indeed, without piety (Taqwa), distinguishing truth from falsehood is impossible, because attachments, animosities, and sins cast a thick veil over the face of truth and blind human perception and vision.

In any case, the Holy Quran is the supreme Furqan.

It is the means of distinguishing truth from falsehood in the entire system of human life.

It is the means of distinguishing truth from falsehood in the path of individual and social life, and it is a standard and touchstone in the field of thoughts, beliefs, laws, rulings, etiquette, and ethics.

It is also noteworthy that it says: "He sent down the Furqan upon His servant." Indeed, it is the station of absolute servitude and submission ('Ubudiyyah) that creates the worthiness for the descent of the Furqan and the acceptance of the standards for recognizing truth from falsehood.

Finally, the last point raised in this verse is that it states the ultimate goal of the Furqan is to warn the worlds—a warning whose result is a sense of responsibility toward the duties and obligations placed upon man. The expression "lil-'Alamin" (for the worlds) clarifies that the religion of Islam has a universal aspect and is not limited to a specific region, race, or people. In fact, some have inferred the finality of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) mission from this, because "Alamin" is not only unlimited geographically but also has no temporal restriction and includes all future generations. (Reflect on this).

In the second verse under discussion, Allah, who is the sender of the Furqan, is described with four attributes, one of which is the foundation and the rest are its results and branches:

First, it says: "He is the One to Whom belongs the sovereignty and dominion of the heavens and the earth." (الَّذِي لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌) [^1] Yes, He is the Ruler over the entire universe and all the heavens and the earth, and nothing is outside the realm of His government. Given the placement of "lahu" (to Him) before "mulk al-samawat" (dominion of the heavens), which according to Arabic grammar indicates exclusivity, it is understood that the real government and sovereignty of the heavens and the earth belong exclusively to Him, because His rule is universal, eternal, and real, unlike the sovereignty of others which is partial, fleeting, and simultaneously dependent on Him.

Then, it proceeds to negate the beliefs of the polytheists one after another, saying:

"The God Who has not taken a son." (وَ لَمْ يَتَّخِذْ وَلَداً) [^2] Fundamentally, the need for a child, as we have said before, is either to utilize his human strength in affairs, or to seek help during weakness, old age, and disability, or for companionship in loneliness. It is certain that none of these needs can find a way into His Pure Essence.

In this way, it negates the belief of the Christians that "Messiah" (peace be upon him) is the son of God, or the Jews who considered "Ezra" to be the son of God, as well as the beliefs of the Arab polytheists.

[^1]: Surah al-Anfal, 29. [^1]: The word "Mulk" (with a damma on the mim), as Raghib says in al-Mufradat, means taking possession of something and having sovereignty over it, whereas Milk (with a kasra) does not always imply sovereignty and owner-like disposal. Thus, every Mulk is Milk, but not every Milk is Mulk. [^2]: Further explanation regarding the negation of a son for Allah and its proofs is found in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 116 of Surah al-Baqarah.

Then it adds: "He has no partner in the ownership and sovereignty over the universe." (وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُ شَرِيكٌ فِي الْمُلْكِ‌).

If the Arab polytheists believed in the existence of a partner or partners and considered them partners with Allah in worship, they would seek their intercession and ask for their help in their needs, to the extent that they would explicitly utter this sentence and similar polytheistic and ugly phrases during the Talbiyah for Hajj: "Labbayk, You have no partner, except a partner who belongs to You; You own him and what he owns!" The Quran negates and condemns all these delusions.

In the final sentence, it says: "He created all things; not only did He create them, but He precisely determined their measure, management, and proportions." (وَ خَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ فَقَدَّرَهُ تَقْدِيراً).

Not like the belief of the Dualists who considered a portion of the beings of this world to be the creation of "Yazdan" (the god of good) and a portion to be the creation of "Ahriman" (the god of evil)! In this way, they divided creation between Yazdan and Ahriman because they perceived the world as a collection of "good" and "evil." However, from the perspective of a true monotheist, there is nothing in the universe but good, and if we see evil, it is either relative, or a matter of privation, or the result of our own actions. (Reflect on this).

Key Point: The Precise Measurement of Beings

Not only is the calculated and solid system of the universe one of the firm proofs of Monotheism and the recognition of God, but its precise measurement is another clear proof. We can never consider the measurement of various beings in this world and its calculated quantity and quality to be the result of chance, which is inconsistent with the "calculus of probabilities."

Scientists have conducted studies in this field and unveiled secrets that plunge man into deep wonder, such that his tongue involuntarily chants in praise of the greatness and power of the Lord.

Here we present a glimpse of it for you.

Scientists say that if the outer crust of the Earth were ten feet thicker than it is, oxygen—the primary substance of life—would not exist. Or if the depth of the seas were a few feet more than the current depth, all the oxygen and carbon of the Earth would be absorbed, and no possibility of plant or animal life would remain on the surface of the soil. Most likely, all existing oxygen would be absorbed by the Earth's crust and the sea water, and man would have to wait for plants to grow so that through their existence, the necessary oxygen could reach him.

With precise calculations, it has been found that oxygen for human respiration can be obtained from various sources, but the important point is that the amount of this oxygen is dispersed in the air exactly in the amount necessary for our breathing.

If the air surrounding the Earth were slightly thinner than it is, celestial bodies and piercing meteors—millions of which strike it every day and explode and are destroyed in that outer space—would constantly reach the Earth's surface and strike every corner of it! These celestial bodies move at a speed of 6 to 40 miles per second and cause explosions and fires wherever they hit. If the speed of these bodies were less than it is—for example, the speed of a bullet—they would all fall to the Earth's surface, and the result of their destruction would be obvious.

If a human were in the path of the smallest piece of these celestial bodies, the intensity of their heat—moving at a speed ninety times that of a bullet—would tear him to pieces and disintegrate him.

The density of the air surrounding the Earth is such that it allows cosmic rays to pass toward the Earth only to the extent necessary for the growth of plants, destroys all harmful microbes in that space, and creates beneficial vitamins.

Despite the various vapors that have emerged from the depths of the Earth over many centuries and dispersed into the air—most of which are toxic gases—the air surrounding the Earth has not become contaminated and has always remained in that balanced state suitable for the continuation of human life.

The massive mechanism that creates this extraordinary balance and maintains equilibrium is the sea and the ocean, from whose source of grace vital materials, food, rain, air moderation, plants, and ultimately the existence of man himself originate. Anyone who perceives meaning must bow in respect before the greatness of the sea and be grateful for its blessings! ...

The extraordinary proportion and very precise balance established between "oxygen" and "carbonic acid" (carbon dioxide) to bring about animal and plant life has attracted the attention of all thinkers and set them to reflection.

However, the vital importance of carbonic acid is still hidden from many people. It should be noted that carbonic acid is the same gas used to make carbonated drinks and is well-known among people.

Carbonic acid is a heavy and dense gas that fortunately stays near the Earth's surface, and its separation from oxygen is done with difficulty. When a fire is lit, wood—which itself is composed of oxygen, carbon, and hydrogen—undergoes chemical decomposition due to heat; carbon mixes with oxygen at maximum speed to form carbonic acid, and its hydrogen also mixes with oxygen with the same haste to form water vapor. Smoke consists of pure, uncombined carbon.

When breathing, a person inhales some oxygen, and the blood distributes it to all parts of the body. This same oxygen burns the food in various cells slowly and quietly with low heat, and carbonic acid and water vapor are expelled. For this reason, when it is said jokingly that someone is sighing like a "furnace," a literal truth has been expressed!

The carbonic acid gas created by the combustion of food in the cells enters the lungs, is expelled from the body with subsequent breaths, and returns to the surrounding air. In this way, all animals inhale oxygen and exhale carbonic acid.

How wonderful is the method of control and balance in this world! As a result of this very balance, nature has prevented animals—no matter how large or predatory and wild—from dominating the world. Only man disrupts this balance of nature, moving plants and animals from one place to another, and incidentally, he immediately pays the penalty for this audacity, as plant pests and animal diseases cause him irreparable damage! The following story is a very good example showing how man must observe this control and balance for his own survival:

Several years ago in Australia, a type of bush known as "cactus" was planted along farm fences. Since the insect enemy of this plant did not exist in Australia at that time, the cactus bush began an extraordinary increase and expansion. Before long, it covered an area the size of the island of Great Britain, drove people out of villages and towns, destroyed their crops, and made farming disrupted and impossible.

The local residents used whatever means they had at their disposal but achieved no result. Australia was in danger of being completely taken over by the silent and stubborn army of the cactus plant! Scientists and specialists sought a remedy for this danger and searched the world for this purpose until they finally found an insect that feeds exclusively on the stem, leaves, and base of the cactus and eats no other food, reproduces easily, and has no enemy in Australia.

In this case, the animal overcame the plant, and today the danger of the cactus invasion has been completely removed. With the destruction of the cactus, those insects also perished, and only a small number remained to constantly control the amount of cactus growth and expansion! Creation has established this control and balance in nature, and it has proven very beneficial.

Why is it that the malaria mosquito has not covered the Earth's surface and caused the destruction of the human race, while the common mosquito is abundant even in polar regions?

Or why is it that the yellow fever mosquito, which at one time had come near New York, did not devastate the world? Or why is it that the sleeping sickness fly—created such that it can only live in tropical regions—has not wiped out the human race from the Earth?! (All of these have been prevented through a calculated system of control).

It is enough to remember what pests and diseases we have grappled with throughout time, and how until yesterday we had no means of defense against them and knew nothing of the principles of hygiene; then we realize in what a marvelous way our existence has been preserved and protected. [^1]

[^1]: Summarized from the book The Secret of Human Creation, pages 33 to 36, 39 to 41, and 49 to 152.

Ayahs

وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً لَّا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَوٰةً وَلَا نُشُورًا (٣)
Yet they have taken gods besides Him who create nothing and have themselves been created, and who have no control over their own harm or benefit and have no control over [their own] death, life, or resurrection. (3)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ إِفْكٌ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ وَأَعَانَهُۥ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ ءَاخَرُونَ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءُو ظُلْمًا وَزُورًا (٤)
The faithless say, ‘This is nothing but a lie that he has fabricated, and other people have abetted him in it.’ Thus they have certainly come out with wrongdoing and falsehood. (4)
وَقَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ ٱكْتَتَبَهَا فَهِىَ تُمْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا (٥)
They say, ‘He has taken down myths of the ancients, and they are dictated to him morning and evening.’ (5)
قُلْ أَنزَلَهُ ٱلَّذِى يَعْلَمُ ٱلسِّرَّ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٦)
Say, ‘It has been sent down by Him who knows the hidden in the heavens and the earth. Indeed He is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (6)

Commentary

Various Accusations

These verses are, in fact, a continuation of the discussion in the preceding verses regarding the struggle against polytheism and idol worship, followed by the baseless claims of the polytheists concerning idols, and their accusations against the Quran and the person of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

The first verse essentially brings the polytheists to trial and speaks with a logic that is clear and simple, yet decisive and crushing, to awaken their consciences. It states: "They have taken gods besides the Lord of the worlds—whose attributes were previously mentioned—gods that create absolutely nothing, but are themselves created" (وَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئاً وَ هُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ‌).

The true Deity is the Creator of the universe, whereas they made no such claim regarding the idols; rather, they considered them "created" by Allah.

Furthermore, what could be their motivation for worshipping idols? "Idols that do not even possess the power to benefit or harm themselves, nor do they possess power over death, life, or resurrection" (وَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَ لا نفعاً وَ لا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتاً وَ لا حَياةً وَ لا نُشُوراً), let alone possessing such power for others.

The principles of primary importance to a human being are these five matters: the issue of benefit and harm, and death, life, and resurrection.

Truly, if someone were the original master of these affairs concerning us, He would be worthy of worship. But are these idols ever capable of these matters regarding themselves, let alone wanting to take their worshippers under their protection in these regards?

What scandalous logic is it that a human follows a being and prostrates at its threshold when it has no authority over itself, let alone others?! These idols not only fail to solve any problem for their servants in this world, but they can also achieve nothing in the Hereafter.

This expression indicates that this group of polytheists, who are the addressees of these verses, accepted the Resurrection in some form (even if it was a spiritual rather than physical resurrection), or that the Quran, even despite their lack of belief in the Resurrection, took the matter as a given and spoke to them decisively in this regard. It is common that sometimes a person faces someone who denies a truth, yet expresses his words decisively according to his own thoughts without regard for the other's ideas—especially since an implicit proof for the Resurrection is hidden within the verse itself. For when a Creator creates a creature and is the master of its death, life, benefit, and harm, He must have a purpose for its creation, and this purpose is impossible for human beings without accepting the Resurrection. If everything were to end with human death, life would be futile, which would imply that the Creator was not Wise.

Incidentally, if we see that the word "harm" (ضرر) is placed before "benefit" (نفع) in the verse, it is because a human being primarily fears harm, and the maxim "averting harm is more important than seeking benefit" has become a rational law.

Also, the fact that "harm," "benefit," "death," "life," and "resurrection" are mentioned in the indefinite form (nakirah) is to express the reality that these idols do not possess power over even a single instance of benefit, harm, death, life, or resurrection, let alone for everyone.

And if "they do not possess" (لا يملكون) and "they do not create" (لا يخلقون) are mentioned in the form of the "masculine plural for rational beings" (while stone and wooden idols have not the slightest intellect or consciousness), it is because the target of this speech is not only stone and wooden idols, but also groups who worshipped angels or Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him). Since rational and non-rational beings are combined in the meaning of this sentence, all are mentioned in the rational form, which in literary terms is called taghlib (predominance).

Alternatively, this expression is used according to the belief of the interlocutor to highlight their helplessness and inability; meaning: "Since you consider these to possess intellect and consciousness, why then can they not avert a harm from themselves or attract a benefit?!"

The next verse proceeds to analyze the interpretations of the disbelievers, or more accurately, their excuses in the face of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) call, saying: "The disbelievers said: 'This is nothing but a lie which he has forged, and another folk have helped him at it!'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِنْ هَذا إِلَّا إِفْكٌ افْتَراهُ وَ أَعانَهُ عَلَيْهِ قَوْمٌ آخَرُونَ‌).

In reality, in order to shirk the burden of truth—like all those throughout history who decided to oppose divine leaders—they first accused him of fabrication and lying. Specifically, to belittle the Quran, they used the word "this" (هذا).

Then, to prove that he alone was not capable of producing such words—since producing such profound content, whatever it may be, requires great scientific power, and they were unwilling to admit this—and also to suggest that this was a deep-rooted and calculated plan, they said: "He was not alone in this task; rather, a group helped him, and surely there is a conspiracy afoot that must be resisted!" Some commentators have said that by "another folk" (قَوْمٌ آخَرُونَ‌), they meant a group of Jews, and some said they meant three people from the People of the Book named "Addas," "Yasar," and "Habr" (or Jabr).

In any case, since such subjects did not exist among the polytheists of Mecca, and parts of it, like the stories of previous prophets, were with the Jews and the People of the Book, they were forced to involve the People of the Book in this accusation to dampen the wave of people's wonder upon hearing these verses.

But the Quran gives them only one sentence in response: "They have indeed produced an injustice and a lie" (فَقَدْ جاؤُ ظُلْماً وَ زُوراً) [1]. It is "injustice" (ظلم) because they accused a man as trustworthy, pure, and truthful as the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) of lying and forging against Allah with the cooperation of a group of the People of the Book, thereby oppressing the people and themselves. And it is a "lie" (زور) and falsehood because their claim was completely baseless; for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had repeatedly challenged them to bring surahs and verses like the Quran, and they had been helpless and unable in the face of this challenge.

This itself showed that these verses were not the product of a human brain, for if they were, they too could have brought the like of it by seeking help from the Jews and the People of the Book. Therefore, their inability is proof of their lie, and their lie is proof of their injustice.

Thus, the short phrase "فَقَدْ جاؤُ ظُلْماً وَ زُوراً" is an eloquent and expressive response to their baseless claims.

The word "Zur" (زور - with the vowel 'u') is originally derived from "Zawr" (زور - with the vowel 'a') meaning the upper part of the chest; then it was applied to anything that deviates from the middle path. Since a "lie" deviates from the truth and inclines toward falsehood, it is called Zur.

The next verse addresses another of their deviant analyses and vain excuses regarding the Quran, saying: "And they say: 'Fables of the ancients, which he has had written down!'" (وَ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَها).

They claimed he actually has nothing of his own—neither knowledge nor innovation, let alone revelation and prophethood; he has sought help from a group and gathered a handful of ancient legends and named them revelation and a Heavenly Book.

To achieve this goal, he benefits from others every day, "and they are dictated to him morning and evening!" (فَهِيَ تُمْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَ أَصِيلًا).

He seeks help for his purpose at times when people are less present on the scene, namely in the morning and in the evening.

This statement is, in fact, an interpretation and explanation of the accusations quoted from them in the previous verse.

In these few short sentences, they wanted to impose several points of weakness upon the Quran:

First, that the Quran has absolutely nothing new and is just a handful of ancient legends! Second, that the Prophet of Islam cannot continue his work for even a single day without the help of others; they must dictate the material to him morning and evening, and he writes it down.

And third, that he knows how to read and write, and if he says "I have not studied," that too is a false statement! In reality, with these clear lies and accusations, they wanted to scatter the people from around the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), while all those who possessed intellect and had lived in that society for a time knew well that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had not studied under anyone. Furthermore, he had no regular dealings with the Jews and the People of the Book, and if he were receiving inspiration from others every day, morning and evening, how could it remain hidden from anyone?

Beyond this, the verses of the Quran were revealed to him during travel and at home, in public and in solitude, and in all circumstances.

In addition to all this, the Quran was a collection of creedal teachings, practical laws, regulations, and parts of the biographies of the prophets. It was not the case that the entire Quran consisted of the stories of the prophets. Moreover, what appeared in the Quran regarding the history of previous nations bore no resemblance to what existed in the Two Testaments (the distorted Torah and Gospel) and the superstitious legends of the Arabs; for those were full of superstitions, while these are purified of superstitions. If one were to place the two side-by-side and compare them, the truth of the matter would become clearly evident [1].

Therefore, the last verse under discussion, as a response to these baseless accusations, says:

"Say: 'He has sent it down Who knows the secret in the heavens and the earth'" (قُلْ أَنْزَلَهُ الَّذِي يَعْلَمُ السِّرَّ فِي السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

This is an indication that the content of this Book and the various secrets it contains—regarding sciences, the history of previous nations, laws, human needs, and even secrets of the natural world and news of the future—show that it is not the product of a human brain and was not organized with the help of this person or that. Rather, it is the product of the knowledge of One Who is aware of the secrets of the heavens and the earth, and whose knowledge encompasses all things.

Yet, despite all this, He leaves the path of return open to these biased, ill-intentioned people and scandalous liars, saying at the end of the verse: The doors of repentance and returning to Allah are open to all of you, "for He is and has always been Oft-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

By His mercy, He sent the prophets and revealed the Heavenly Books, and by His forgiveness, He forgives your countless sins in the light of faith and repentance.

[1] "Jā'ū" (جاؤُ) from the root "majī'" usually means "to come," but here it means "to bring" or "to produce," just as we read in Surah Yunus, verse 81, where Moses said to the sorcerers: "What you have brought is sorcery" (ما جِئْتُمْ بِهِ السِّحْرُ).

[1] A group of commentators believe that the meaning of the phrase "iktatabahā" (اکتتبها) is that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) asked others to write these verses for him, and likewise, the phrase "tumlā 'alayhi" (تُمْلى‌ عَلَيْهِ‌) means they would recite them and he would memorize them. However, given that we have no reason to take these two phrases contrary to their apparent meaning, the interpretation given in the text seems more correct; in reality, they wanted to accuse the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) of being literate and intentionally presenting himself as unlettered.

Ayahs

وَقَالُوا۟ مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشِى فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۙ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُۥ نَذِيرًا (٧)
And they say, ‘What sort of apostle is this who eats food and walks in the marketplaces? Why has not an angel been sent down to him so as to be a warner along with him?’ (7)
أَوْ يُلْقَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِ كَنزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُۥ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْهَا ۚ وَقَالَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ إِن تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَّسْحُورًا (٨)
Or, ‘[Why is not] a treasure thrown to him, or [why does] he [not] have a garden from which he may eat?’ And the wrongdoers say, ‘You are just following a bewitched man.’ (8)
ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا۟ لَكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ فَضَلُّوا۟ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا (٩)
Look, how they coin epithets for you; so they go astray, and cannot find the way. (9)
تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِن شَآءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْرًا مِّن ذَٰلِكَ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ وَيَجْعَل لَّكَ قُصُورًۢا (١٠)
Blessed is He who will grant you better than that if He wishes—gardens with streams running in them, and He will make for you palaces. (10)

Occasion of Revelation

In a narration from Imam Hasan al-Askari عليه السلام, we read that he said: I asked my father, the tenth Imam: Did the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله engage in discussion, debate, and reasoning with the Jews and polytheists in response to their reproaches and excuses?

My father replied: Yes, this happened many times. Among them was a day when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was sitting by the House of Allah (the Kaaba), and "Abdullah bin Abi Umayyah al-Makhzumi" stood before him and said: "O Muhammad, you have made a great claim and you speak terrifying words! You imagine that you are the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds, yet it is not befitting for the Lord of the worlds and the Creator of all creatures to have a messenger like you—a human being just like us. You eat food as we do, and you walk in the markets just as we do!" The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله supplicated: "O my Lord, You hear all speech and are All-Knowing of everything; You know what Your servants say (so provide the answer to them Yourself)." At this moment, the above verses were revealed and responded to their excuses [1].

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 6.

Commentary

Why Does This Prophet Not Have Treasures and Gardens?

Since the previous verses addressed and answered some of the disbelievers' objections regarding the Holy Quran, the verses under discussion raise and respond to another part of their objections concerning the person and mission of the Prophet himself.

It says: "And they say, 'What is this messenger that he eats food and walks in the markets?'" (وَ قالُوا ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ يَأْكُلُ الطَّعامَ وَ يَمْشِي فِي الْأَسْواقِ).

What kind of prophet is this who, like ordinary people, needs nourishment and walks in the markets for earning and trade, or to purchase his necessities? This is neither the tradition of messengers nor the way of kings and monarchs! Yet he wishes to both convey a Divine call and rule over us all!

Fundamentally, they believed that people of status should not personally step into markets to fulfill their needs; they should send agents and servants for such tasks.

Then they added: "Why was there not at least an angel sent down to him to be a warner with him," as a witness to the truth of his call? (لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً).

Very well, assuming we accept that the Messenger of Allah can be a human, why then is he a destitute man lacking wealth and property? "Why was a treasure not cast down to him from heaven, or at least why does he not have a garden from which he may eat and make a living?" (أَوْ يُلْقى‌ إِلَيْهِ كَنْزٌ أَوْ تَكُونُ لَهُ جَنَّةٌ يَأْكُلُ مِنْها).

And they were not satisfied with even this, and finally, with a false conclusion, they accused him of madness, as we read at the end of this same verse: "And the wrongdoers say, 'O people who have believed in him, you follow none but a man bewitched (mad and under the influence of sorcerers)!'" (وَ قالَ الظَّالِمُونَ إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَسْحُوراً).

This was because they believed that sorcerers could interfere with a person's thoughts and intelligence and strip them of their sanity!

From the sum of the above verses, it is understood that they actually had several hollow objections against the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, retreating from their claims step by step.

First, they believed he should be an angel; the fact that he eats food and walks in the markets proves he is certainly not an angel! Then they said: Very well, if he is not an angel, at least let an angel be sent from God as an assistant alongside him.

Again, they descended further and said: Assuming the Messenger of God is a human, a treasure should be cast down to him from heaven to prove he is favored by God! And in the final stage, they said: Assuming he has none of these, at least he should not be a poor man; like a prosperous farmer, he should have a garden at his disposal to provide for his life! But unfortunately, he lacks all of these and still says, "I am a prophet!" Finally, they concluded that his making such a great claim under these conditions is proof that he does not possess a sound mind!

The next verse expresses the answer to all of these in one short sentence: "See how they strike parables for you, and so they have gone astray, such that they are unable to find a way" (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ ضَرَبُوا لَكَ الْأَمْثالَ فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا).

This sentence is an eloquent expression of the reality that, in the face of the call to truth and the Quran—whose content is a clear witness of its connection to God—they have resorted to a bunch of hollow and baseless words, wishing to cover the face of truth with these groundless statements.

This is exactly like a person who, in the face of our logical arguments, resorts to a series of excuses whose weakness is completely apparent; without wanting to answer his words one by one, we say: "See what hollow words he uses to stand against logical proof!" Truly, their words in all parts were like this, because:

Firstly: Why should a prophet be of the genus of angels? On the contrary, reason and knowledge dictate that a human must be the leader of humans, so that he may fully understand all their pains, desires, needs, problems, and life issues, and be a practical role model for them in all fields, so that people can take inspiration from him in all programs. If an angel had descended, these goals would certainly not have been met; people would have said: "If he speaks of asceticism and indifference to the world, he is an angel and has no need; if he calls to purity and chastity, he knows nothing of the storm of sexual instinct," and dozens of similar "ifs."

Secondly: What necessity is there for an angel to come with a human to confirm him? Are not miracles, especially a great miracle like the Quran, sufficient for understanding this reality?

Thirdly: Eating food like other humans and walking in the markets causes him to mingle more with the people, delve into the depths of their lives, and perform his mission better; this is not a detriment but rather an aid to him.

Fourthly: The greatness and personality of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not in treasures and hoards, nor in lush, green fruit gardens. This is the deviant way of thinking of the disbelievers who consider personality and even proximity to God to lie in being wealthy, whereas the prophets came to say: "O human, the value of your existence is not in these things; it is in knowledge, faith, and piety."

Fifthly: By what criteria did they consider him "bewitched" and "mad"? How can someone whose intellect—by the testimony of the history of his life, his great revolution, and his founding of Islamic civilization—was extraordinary, be accused with these ridiculous labels? Unless we say that breaking idols and not blindly following ancestors is proof of madness! Based on what we have said, it becomes clear that "parables" (amthal) here, especially with the existing context in the verse, means weak and baseless words; the expression "parables" is perhaps because they presented them in the garb of truth and as if they were logical proofs, while in reality, they are not [1].

[1]: Many commentators here have taken "amthal" to mean "similes," but they have not clarified what simile the polytheists made here. Others have interpreted "amthal" here as "descriptions," because one of the meanings of "mathal"—according to what Raghib said in Al-Mufradat—is "description." However, even if description was intended, it certainly means a baseless and groundless description here, as there are clues in the beginning and end of the verse that indicate this meaning; on one hand, it says with wonder, "See what parables they strike?" and on the other hand, it says: descriptions that cause their irreversible misguidance.

It should also be noted that the enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sometimes accused him of being a "sorcerer" (sahir) and sometimes "bewitched" (mashur). Although some commentators have suggested that "mashur" might mean "sorcerer" (as sometimes the passive participle is used in the sense of the active), the apparent meaning is that these two are different.

If they called him a sorcerer, it was because of the extraordinary influence of his words on hearts; since they did not want to accept this truth, they resorted to the accusation of being a sorcerer.

But "mashur" means someone whose intellect has been interfered with by sorcerers, causing a disturbance of his senses. This accusation arose because he was a breaker of traditions and acted contrary to superstitious customs and individual expediency.

However, the answer to all these accusations became clear from the above discussion.

Here the question arises: why does He say: "so they have gone astray, such that they are unable to find a way" (فَضَلُّوا فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَبِيلًا)?

The answer is that a person can find the way to truth only if he is a seeker of truth and desires it. But he who has already made his decision based on false and misleading prejudices stemming from ignorance, stubbornness, and malice will not only fail to find the truth but will always take a stand against it.

The last verse under discussion, like the previous one, addresses the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—as a way of belittling their words and indicating they are not worth answering—saying: "Blessed and Great is He who, if He willed, could have made for you better than that—gardens under which rivers flow, and He could make for you palaces" (تَبارَكَ الَّذِي إِنْ شاءَ جَعَلَ لَكَ خَيْراً مِنْ ذلِكَ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ وَ يَجْعَلْ لَكَ قُصُوراً).

Who gave the gardens and palaces of others to them except Allah? Fundamentally, who created all this blessing and beauty in this world except the Lord? Is it not possible for such a Powerful and Gracious God to place better than these at your disposal?! But He never wants people to perceive your personality through wealth, property, palaces, and gardens, and thus become heedless of real values.

He wants your life to be like that of ordinary people, the downtrodden, and the deprived, so that you can be a refuge for all of them and for the general public.

But why does He say gardens and palaces are better than what they asked for? Because a "treasure" alone does not solve problems; rather, after much effort, it must be converted into gardens and palaces. Furthermore, they said you should have a garden to provide for your life, but the Quran says: God can provide gardens and palaces for you, but the goal of your mission and messengership is something else.

In the "Qasi'ah Sermon" of Nahj al-Balagha, there is an eloquent and expressive statement in this regard, where the Imam عليه السلام says:

"Musa bin Imran and his brother (Harun) entered upon Pharaoh while they were wearing woolen clothes and each held a staff in his hand. They made a condition with him—that if you submit to the command of the Lord—your rule and kingdom will remain, and your honor and power will endure. But he said:

'Do you not wonder at these two? They make a condition with me that the survival of my kingdom and the endurance of my honor depend on their wish, while they themselves manifest poverty and misery in their appearance. (If they are telling the truth) why have bracelets of gold not been given to them?'

Pharaoh said this because of his glorification of gold and its accumulation, and his belittling of wool and the wearing of it.

But if Allah had willed, when He sent His prophets, He would have opened for them the doors of treasures, gold mines, and lush green gardens, and if He had willed, He would have sent the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth with them. If He had done this, the trial would have vanished, reward and punishment would have become ineffective, Divine promises and warnings would have been useless, and for those who accepted, the reward of the tested ones would not have been mandatory, and the believers would not have deserved the reward of the doers of good, and names would not have been accompanied by their meanings...

But Allah made His prophets strong in terms of determination and will, and poor and weak in terms of appearance, but coupled with a contentment that filled hearts and eyes with self-sufficiency, even though their outward poverty filled eyes and ears with discomfort.

If the prophets possessed such power that no one thought of opposing them, and had strength and honor that were never defeated, and possessed a sovereignty and majesty that all eyes were fixed upon them and people traveled from far distances to reach them, their credibility and value for the people would have been less, and the arrogant would have bowed their heads before them and declared faith.

But this faith would not have been because of love for their goal, but rather because of the fear that overcame them or because of the desire they had for their material possessions. In that case, sincerity would not be found in their intentions, and other than Allah would have a share in their deeds" [1]. It is also necessary to note that some have said the garden and palace refer to the garden and palace of the Hereafter, but this interpretation is by no means consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse [2].

[1]: Sermon 192 of Nahj al-Balagha (The Qasi'ah Sermon).

[2]: Similarly, for those who have said it refers to the gardens of this world and the palaces of the Hereafter, the past and present verbs (ja'ala and yaj'alu) in the verse should not cause such a misconception, because we know that according to the rules of Arabic literature, verbs lose their temporal concept when they are placed in a conditional sentence.

Ayahs

بَلْ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِمَن كَذَّبَ بِٱلسَّاعَةِ سَعِيرًا (١١)
Indeed, they deny the Hour, and We have prepared a Blaze for those who deny the Hour. (11)
إِذَا رَأَتْهُم مِّن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا۟ لَهَا تَغَيُّظًا وَزَفِيرًا (١٢)
When it sights them from a distant place, they will hear it raging and roaring. (12)
وَإِذَآ أُلْقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مَكَانًا ضَيِّقًا مُّقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا۟ هُنَالِكَ ثُبُورًا (١٣)
And when they are cast into a narrow place in it, bound together [in chains], they will pray for [their own] annihilation. (13)
لَّا تَدْعُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ثُبُورًا وَٰحِدًا وَٱدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا كَثِيرًا (١٤)
[They will be told:] ‘Do not pray for a single annihilation today, but pray for many annihilations!’ (14)
قُلْ أَذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ ٱلْخُلْدِ ٱلَّتِى وُعِدَ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ ۚ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزَآءً وَمَصِيرًا (١٥)
Say, ‘Is that better, or the everlasting paradise promised to the Godwary, which will be their reward and destination?’ (15)
لَّهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ خَـٰلِدِينَ ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ وَعْدًا مَّسْـُٔولًا (١٦)
There they will have whatever they wish, abiding [forever]—a promise [much] besought, [binding] on your Lord. (16)

Commentary

A Comparison of Heaven and Hell

In these verses—following the discussion in previous verses regarding the deviation of the disbelievers concerning Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Prophethood of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله)—the discourse turns to another aspect of their deviations regarding the Resurrection. In truth, by explaining this section, it becomes clear that they were plagued by instability and deviation in all the principles of religion: in Monotheism, in Prophethood, and in the Resurrection. The first two parts appeared in the preceding verses, and now we read the third part.

First, He says: "Nay, they deny the Hour" (بَلْ كَذَّبُوا بِالسَّاعَةِ).

The use of the word "Nay" (bal), which in literary terminology is for "digression" (idrab), means that whatever they say regarding the denial of Monotheism and Prophethood are merely excuses that actually stem from the denial of the Resurrection. For if someone truly believed in such a great tribunal and Divine reward and punishment, they would not so recklessly mock the truths, nor would they turn away from the call of a Prophet whose proofs of Prophethood are manifest by citing hollow excuses, nor would they bow their heads in reverence before idols they fashioned with their own hands.

However, the Quran does not engage in an argumentative response here, because this group was not composed of people of logic and reasoning. Instead, it confronts them with shaking and crushing threats and visualizes their grim and painful future before their eyes, as this logic is sometimes more effective for such individuals.

First, He says: "And We have prepared for him who denies the Hour a blazing Fire" (وَ أَعْتَدْنا لِمَنْ كَذَّبَ بِالسَّاعَةِ سَعِيراً) [^1].

Then, He provides a striking description of this blazing Fire, saying: "When it sees them from a far-off place, they will hear its raging and roaring!" (إِذا رَأَتْهُمْ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ سَمِعُوا لَها تَغَيُّظاً وَ زَفِيراً).

In this verse, there are several expressive descriptions that report the intensity of this Divine punishment:

  1. It does not say they see the Fire of Hell from afar; rather, it says the Fire sees them, as if it has eyes and ears, fixing its gaze on the path and awaiting these sinners!
  2. It does not need them to draw near for it to become agitated; rather, from a far distance—which according to some narrations is a year's journey away—it cries out in rage.
  3. This blazing Fire is described with "taghayyuz" (raging), which refers to a state where a person manifests their anger through shouting and roaring.
  4. The Fire of Hell is attributed with "zafir" (roaring/hissing), which is similar to the state when a person draws a deep breath into the chest such that the ribs are pushed upward, and this usually occurs when a person becomes extremely angry.

The sum of these states indicates that the blazing Fire of Hell, like a hungry predatory beast waiting for its prey, awaits this group (we seek refuge in Allah).

This is the state of Hell when it sees them from afar. But He describes their condition inside the Fire of Hell as follows: "And when they are cast into a narrow place thereof, bound together, they will plead there for destruction" (وَ إِذا أُلْقُوا مِنْها مَكاناً ضَيِّقاً مُقَرَّنِينَ دَعَوْا هُنالِكَ ثُبُوراً) [^1]. This is not due to the smallness of Hell, for according to verse 30 of Surah Qaf: "On the Day We will say to Hell, 'Are you full?' and it will say, 'Are there any more?'" (يَوْمَ نَقُولُ لِجَهَنَّمَ هَلِ امْتَلَأْتِ وَ تَقُولُ هَلْ مِنْ مَزِيدٍ).

Therefore, it is a vast place, but they are so restricted within this vast place that, according to some narrations, their entry into Hell is like a nail being driven into a wall! [^2] Additionally, the word "thubur" originally means "destruction and ruin," and when a person faces something terrifying and lethal, they sometimes cry out "Wa thubura!", the meaning of which is "O, woe/death unto me."

[^1]: "Sa'ira" is from the root "sa'r" (rhyming with qa'r), meaning the flaring up of fire. Thus, sa'ir refers to a flared, extensive, and scorching fire.

[^1]: "Muqarranin" is from the root "qarn", meaning the proximity and joining of two or more things together. A rope used to bind objects is called qarn, and similarly, someone whose hands and feet are bound together with shackles is called muqarran (for more explanation on this word, refer to Volume 10, page 386, under verse 49 of Surah Ibrahim).

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

But soon they will be told: "Pray not this day for one destruction, but pray for many destructions" (لا تَدْعُوا الْيَوْمَ ثُبُوراً واحِداً وَ ادْعُوا ثُبُوراً كَثِيراً).

In any case, this wailing of yours will lead nowhere, and there will be no death or annihilation; rather, you must remain alive and taste the painful punishments.

This verse is, in fact, similar to verse 16 of Surah At-Tur, which says: "اصْلَوْها فَاصْبِرُوا أَوْ لا تَصْبِرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ إِنَّما تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ" ("Burn therein; then be patient or impatient, it is all the same for you. You are only being recompensed for what you used to do").

As for who the speaker of these words to the disbelievers is, indications suggest they are the angels of punishment, as they are the ones dealing with them.

As for why they are told not to cry for destruction once but many times, it may be because their painful punishment is not temporary such that it would end with one cry of "Wa thubura"; rather, they must constantly repeat this phrase throughout this duration. Furthermore, the Divine punishment for these criminal oppressors is so varied that with every punishment, they see their death with their own eyes and their cry of "Wa thubura" rises, as if they are repeatedly dying and being brought back to life.

Then, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He commands him to invite them to a judgment, saying: "Say: Is that better, or the Garden of Eternity which is promised to the righteous? It shall be their reward and their final destination" (قُلْ أَ ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ أَمْ جَنَّةُ الْخُلْدِ الَّتِي وُعِدَ الْمُتَّقُونَ كانَتْ لَهُمْ جَزاءً وَ مَصِيراً).

The same Paradise where "they shall have therein whatever they desire" (لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ).

The same Paradise where "they will abide forever" (خالِدِينَ).

"It is a promise binding upon your Lord, to be petitioned for" (كانَ عَلى‌ رَبِّكَ وَعْداً مَسْؤُلًا).

This question and seeking of judgment is not because anyone has doubt in this matter, nor because those terrifying, painful punishments can be balanced or compared with these unparalleled blessings. Rather, such questions and requests for judgment are to awaken dormant consciences, placing them before an obvious matter and at a crossroads.

If they answer that those blessings are better and superior (which they certainly must), they have condemned themselves because their actions are contrary to that. And if they say that punishment is superior to this blessing, they have signed the document of their own insanity. This is exactly like warning a truant youth who has left school and university, saying: "Look, those who fled from knowledge and fell into the embrace of corruption are in prison; is prison better or reaching high positions?!"

Key Points

  1. First, it should be noted that in the above verses, in one place "eternity" (khuld) is mentioned as an attribute for Paradise, and in another place "being eternal" (khalidin) is the state of the inhabitants of Paradise, to emphasize the fact that both Paradise is eternal and its residents are as well.

  2. The phrase "لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ" (they shall have therein whatever they desire) is the opposite of what is mentioned regarding the people of Hell in verse 54 of Surah Saba: "وَ حِيلَ بَيْنَهُمْ وَ بَيْنَ ما يَشْتَهُونَ" (And a barrier is placed between them and what they desire).

  1. The use of the term "masir" (destination/place of return) after the term "jaza" (reward) regarding Paradise serves to emphasize what is included in the concept of reward, and it is a direct contrast to the place of the people of Hell mentioned in the preceding verses—that they are cast into a narrow and restricted place with hands and feet in shackles.
  1. The phrase "كانَ عَلى‌ رَبِّكَ وَعْداً مَسْؤُلًا" (It is a promise binding upon your Lord, to be petitioned for) indicates that the believers, in their supplications, have requested Paradise with all its blessings from Allah. They are the petitioners (sa'il) and Allah is the One petitioned (mas'ul 'anhu), just as we read in verse 194 of Surah Al-Imran from the tongue of the believers: "رَبَّنا وَ آتِنا ما وَعَدْتَنا عَلى‌ رُسُلِكَ" ("Our Lord, grant us what You promised us through Your messengers").

All believers also make such a request of Allah through their state of being (zaban-e hal), for whoever obeys His command is making such a request through their actions.

The angels also make such a request of Allah for the believers, as we read (in verse 8 of Surah Al-Mu'min): "رَبَّنا وَ أَدْخِلْهُمْ جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدْتَهُمْ" ("Our Lord! And admit them to the Gardens of Eternity which You have promised them").

There is another interpretation here as well: that the word "mas'ula" is an emphasis on this certain promise of Allah, meaning this promise is so definitive that the believers can demand it from Allah. This is exactly like when we give someone a promise and simultaneously give them the right to claim it from us.

Of course, there is no obstacle to all these meanings being combined within the broad concept of "mas'ula".

  1. Regarding the phrase "لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ" (they shall have therein whatever they desire), a question has arisen for some: if we consider the broad meaning of this phrase, does it mean that if the people of Paradise desire the status of the Prophets and Saints, it will be given to them? Or if they seek the salvation of their sinful friends and relatives who deserve Hell, will they attain it? And similar desires.

However, the answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: the veils are removed from before the eyes of the people of Paradise; they perceive truths well, and proportions become completely clear to them. It would never cross their minds to make such requests of Allah. This is exactly like us in this world requesting that a primary school child become a university professor, or a criminal thief become a court judge; would such things occur to any rational person in this world? It is the same there; furthermore, all their desires are overshadowed by the Will of Allah, and they desire only what Allah desires.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ فَيَقُولُ ءَأَنتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبَادِى هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا۟ ٱلسَّبِيلَ (١٧)
On the day that He will muster them and those whom they worship besides Allah, He will say, ‘Was it you who led astray these servants of Mine, or did they themselves stray from the way?’ (17)
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ مَا كَانَ يَنۢبَغِى لَنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ مِن دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِن مَّتَّعْتَهُمْ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ نَسُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بُورًا (١٨)
They will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove us to take any wali in Your stead! But You provided for them and their fathers until they forgot the Reminder, and they were a ruined lot.’ (18)
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُم بِمَا تَقُولُونَ فَمَا تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفًا وَلَا نَصْرًا ۚ وَمَن يَظْلِم مِّنكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذَابًا كَبِيرًا (١٩)
So they will certainly impugn you in what you say, and you will neither be able to circumvent [punishment] nor find help, and whoever of you does wrong, We shall make him taste a terrible punishment. (19)

Commentary

The Trial of the False Deities and the Misguided Worshipers

Since the preceding verses discussed the fate of the believers and the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection, along with the rewards and punishments for these two groups, the verses under discussion continue this theme in a different form. They present a warning by describing the question Allah will pose to the "deities" of the polytheists on that Day, along with their response:

First, He says: "And [mention] the Day He will gather them and that which they worship besides Allah" (وَ يَوْمَ يَحْشُرُهُمْ وَ ما يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

And He will ask them: "Was it you who misled these, My servants, or did they [themselves] stray from the way?" (فَيَقُولُ أَ أَنْتُمْ أَضْلَلْتُمْ عِبادِي هؤُلاءِ أَمْ هُمْ ضَلُّوا السَّبِيلَ).

In response, they will say: "Exalted are You! It was not proper for us to take any protectors besides You" (قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ ما كانَ يَنْبَغِي لَنا أَنْ نَتَّخِذَ مِنْ دُونِكَ مِنْ أَوْلِياءَ). Not only did we not invite them to ourselves, but we were confessors of Your guardianship and servitude, and we did not choose any deity for ourselves or others besides You! The cause of their deviation was that "You provided them and their fathers with the comforts and blessings of the world (instead of being grateful for Your blessings, they became submerged in lusts and self-indulgence) until they forgot the Remembrance" (وَ لكِنْ مَتَّعْتَهُمْ وَ آباءَهُمْ حَتَّى نَسُوا الذِّكْرَ).

"And for this reason, they became a ruined and perished people" (وَ كانُوا قَوْماً بُوراً).

At this point, Allah turns His speech toward the polytheists, saying: "Your deities have denied you in what you say" (You used to say: 'These [deities] diverted us from the path and invited us to worship them,' while they now declare your statement a lie) (فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُمْ بِما تَقُولُونَ). And since this is the case and you yourselves were responsible for your own deviation, "you are unable to avert [the punishment] or [find] help" for yourselves, nor can you seek help from others (فَما تَسْتَطِيعُونَ صَرْفاً وَ لا نَصْراً).

"And whoever among you does wrong, We will make him taste a great punishment" (وَ مَنْ يَظْلِمْ مِنْكُمْ نُذِقْهُ عَذاباً كَبِيراً).

Undoubtedly, "wrongdoing" (dhulm) has a broad meaning. Although the subject of discussion in the verse is "polytheism" (shirk)—which is one of the clearest manifestations of wrongdoing—this does not negate the general nature of the verse's concept.

It is interesting that "مَنْ يَظْلِمْ" (whoever does wrong) appears in the present/future tense (mudari'). This indicates that while the first part of the discussion relates to the dialogues of the Resurrection, the final sentence is an address to them in this world. It is as if, once the hearts of the polytheists are prepared to be moved by hearing the "dialogues between the worshipers and the deities on the Day of Judgment," the discourse is brought from the Hereafter back to this world, telling them: "Whoever commits wrongdoing and injustice, We shall punish him severely." [1]

[1] There is also the possibility that the final sentence is a continuation of Allah's dialogue with the polytheists on the Day of Resurrection. The present tense of the verb does no harm to this view, as the phrase "مَنْ يَظْلِمْ..." is stated as a general law (a conditional sentence), and we know that verbs in conditional sentences lose their specific temporal meaning, focusing only on the connection between the condition and the consequence.

Key Points

1. Who is meant by the "deities" here?

In response to this question, two famous interpretations exist among commentators:

First, it refers to human deities (such as Christ), or demonic ones (such as the Jinn), or the "Angels," each of whom a group of polytheists had chosen for worship. Since these beings possess intellect, consciousness, and perception, they can be subjected to this interrogation. To establish the proof and demonstrate the falsehood of the polytheists—who claim these beings invited them to worship them—they are asked: "Is such a statement true?" And they explicitly deny the polytheists' claim!

The second interpretation, mentioned by a group of commentators, is that on that Day, Allah will grant a form of life, perception, and consciousness to the "idols" so that they can be interrogated and provide the necessary response, saying: "O Lord, we did not mislead them; rather, they themselves went astray due to being drowned in lusts and pride."

There is also the possibility that it includes all deities, whether those possessing intellect and consciousness who speak the truth with their own tongues, or those lacking intellect who reflect the truth through their state (lisan al-hal).

However, the existing clues in the verse are more consistent with the first interpretation, as the verbs and pronouns all indicate that the interlocutors are beings of intellect and consciousness, which fits deities like Christ, the Angels, and their like.

Furthermore, from the phrase "فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوكُمْ" (they have denied you), it appears that the polytheists had previously claimed these deities misled them and invited them to themselves. It is unlikely that the polytheists would make such a claim regarding stone and wooden idols, for they themselves—as mentioned in the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him)—were certain that idols do not speak: "لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ ما هؤُلاءِ يَنْطِقُونَ" (Surah Al-Anbiya, 63).

In contrast, regarding Christ, for example, we read in Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 116: "أَ أَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَ أُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ" ("Did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as two deities besides Allah'?!"). In any case, it is certain that the claim of the polytheists and idolaters was hollow and baseless, and those deities never invited them to their worship.

It is noteworthy that the deities do not say in response: "O Lord, we did not invite them to worship us." Instead, they say: "We did not take any protector for ourselves besides You." This means: "When we ourselves worship only You, we certainly would not introduce another to them." This is especially so as this statement is accompanied by the word "سُبْحانَكَ" (Exalted are You) and the phrase "ما كانَ يَنْبَغِي لَنا" (It was not proper for us), which clarifies their utmost etiquette and emphasis on Monotheism (Tawhid).

2. The Motive for Deviating from the Principle of Monotheism

It is significant that the deities identify the primary factor for the deviation of this polytheistic group as their "affluent life." They say: "O Lord, You provided them and their fathers with the blessings of this life, and this caused their forgetfulness." Instead of recognizing the Giver of these blessings and engaging in gratitude and obedience, they sank into the whirlpool of negligence and pride, forgetting You and the Day of Resurrection.

Truly, an affluent life for a population that lacks sufficient spiritual capacity and whose foundations of faith are weak is, on one hand, "pride-inducing." When they reach abundant blessings, they lose themselves and forget God; sometimes, like Pharaoh, they even beat the drum of "I am God!" On the other hand, such individuals desire to be as free as possible, with no restrictions on their indulgence in pleasures. They do not want bonds such as "lawful" (halal) and "prohibited" (haram), or "legitimate" and "illegitimate," to prevent them from reaching their goals. For this reason, they do not wish to bow before religious laws and regulations or accept the Day of Account and Retribution.

Even today, the followers of God's religion and the teachings of the Prophets are not many among the affluent; it is the "oppressed" (mustad'afin) who are the staunch supporters and loyal, self-sacrificing friends of faith and religion. Of course, there are exceptions on both sides, but the majority of both groups are as we have described.

Furthermore, the above verse does not only emphasize their own affluent life but also the affluent life of their ancestors. When a person is raised in luxury and comfort from childhood, it is natural for them to often see a separation and distinction between themselves and others, and they are not easily willing to lose their material interests and comfortable life.

In contrast, being committed to God's command and religious programs requires sacrifice, and sometimes migration (hijrah), or even struggle (jihad) and martyrdom. Sometimes it requires enduring various deprivations and not surrendering to the enemy—matters that are less compatible with the temperament of the affluent, except for those whose personality transcends their material life. If they have it one day, they thank God; if they do not, they do not become unstable or distressed. In other words, they are the masters of their material life, not its subjects; they are commanders, not captives!

Additionally, from this explanation, it is understood that the phrase "نَسُوا الذِّكْرَ" (they forgot the Remembrance) refers to forgetting the remembrance of Allah—which is replaced in Surah Al-Hashr, verse 19, by "وَ لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ" (And be not like those who forgot Allah)—or forgetting the Day of Resurrection and the court of Divine Justice, as stated in Surah Sad, verse 26: "لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ" ("For them is a severe punishment because they forgot the Day of Account"). Or it could mean forgetting both, and all Divine programs.

3. The Word "Bur"

"بُور" (Bur) is derived from the root "bawar," which originally means the extreme stagnation of something. Since extreme stagnation leads to corruption—as the Arabic proverb says, "It stagnated until it corrupted"—this word is applied to corruption and subsequently to destruction. And since land devoid of trees, flowers, and plants is essentially corrupt and dead, it is called "ba'ir."

Therefore, the phrase "كانُوا قَوْماً بُوراً" indicates that this group, due to being drowned in a comfortable material life and forgetting God and the Resurrection, was led to corruption and destruction. The land of their hearts became like a dry and barren desert, devoid of the flowers of valuable human traits and the fruits of virtue and spiritual life.

Studying the state of nations today that are drowned in luxury and comfort, oblivious to God and His creation, clarifies the depth of this verse's meaning—how they have drowned in a sea of moral corruption and how human virtues have been uprooted from the barren land of their existence. [1]

[1] Some consider the word "بُور" to be an infinitive (masdar) that is sometimes used as an active participle (ism fa'il), remaining the same for singular, dual, plural, masculine, and feminine. Others consider it the plural of "ba'ir."

Ayah

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَيَمْشُونَ فِى ٱلْأَسْوَاقِ ۗ وَجَعَلْنَا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً أَتَصْبِرُونَ ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيرًا (٢٠)
We did not send any apostles before you but that they indeed ate food and walked in marketplaces. We have made you a [means of] test for one another, [to see] if you will be patient and steadfast, and your Lord is all-seeing. (20)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for the first of the aforementioned verses that a group of the leaders of the polytheists came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Muhammad, what do you want from us? If you seek leadership, we will make you our leader; and if you desire wealth, we will collect our riches for you." However, when they saw that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not submit to these proposals, they began making excuses and said: "What kind of messenger of God are you, while you eat food and walk in the marketplaces?!" They reproached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله for eating food because they wanted him to be an angel, and they blamed him for walking in the marketplaces because they had seen the Chosroes, Caesars, and tyrannical kings who never set foot in the marketplaces. Meanwhile, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله associated and socialized with the people in the markets, was intimate with them, and conveyed the divine commands and prohibitions to them. The excuse-seekers objected and said: "He wants to become our ruler, yet his method is contrary to the conduct of kings!" The above verse was revealed and clarified this reality: that the method of the Prophet of Islam was the method of all previous prophets. [^1]

Commentary

All Prophets Were Thus

In a few verses prior, one of the excuses of the polytheists was mentioned—namely, why the Prophet of Islam eats food and walks in the marketplaces—and a brief, concise answer was given. The verse under discussion returns to the same subject and provides a more explicit and detailed response, stating:

"And We did not send any messengers before you except that they all [being of the human race] ate food and walked in the marketplaces" (and socialized with the people) (وَ ما أَرْسَلْنا قَبْلَكَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا إِنَّهُمْ لَيَأْكُلُونَ الطَّعامَ وَ يَمْشُونَ فِي الْأَسْواقِ).

And at the same time, "We have made some of you a trial for others" (وَ جَعَلْنا بَعْضَكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ فِتْنَةً).

This trial may be through the fact that choosing prophets from the human race—and specifically from among humans who rise from the deprived masses—is itself a great test. This is because a group of people refuse to submit to one of their own kind, especially someone who is at a lower level in terms of material resources, while they are at a higher level, or are older and more prominent in society.

[^1]: Although the content of the above narration appears in many commentaries, what we have mentioned above is according to the narration that Al-Qurtubi cited in Volume 7 of his Tafsir, page 4721.

There is also the possibility that the meaning is the general testing of people by one another. For the disabled, the weak, the sick, the orphan, and the suffering are a test for the strong and the healthy; conversely, the healthy and strong are a test for the weak and disabled. Will the second group be content with Allah's decree? And will the first group fulfill their human duty and commitment toward the second group or not?

Since these two interpretations are not contradictory, both may be included in the broad concept of the verse, which is the testing of people by one another.

Following this statement, He addresses everyone and asks:

"Will you be patient?" (أَ تَصْبِرُونَ).

For the most important pillar of victory in all these trials is patience, steadfastness, and perseverance—patience against the rebellious whims that prevent the acceptance of truth, patience against the difficulties inherent in duties and the fulfillment of missions, and also patience in the face of calamities and painful events from which human life will never be free.

In short, it is only through the power of endurance and patience that one can succeed in this great divine trial. [^1] At the end of the verse, as a warning, He says: "And your Lord has always been All-Seeing" (وَ كانَ رَبُّكَ بَصِيراً).

Lest they imagine that anything of your behavior in the face of divine trials remains hidden or concealed from the All-Seeing and scrutinizing eye of Allah's knowledge; He knows and sees everything precisely.

[^1]: For further explanation regarding divine trials, the purpose of these trials, and their other characteristics, we have discussed in detail in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 155 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Question

A question arises here: Does the Quran's response to the polytheists in the above verses—stating that all prophets were of the human race—not only fail to solve the problem but, so to speak, multiply the objection? For they might extend their criticism to all prophets.

Answer

Various verses of the Quran show that they had this objection specifically toward the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and believed he had adopted a peculiar status, hence they said: "ما لِهذَا الرَّسُولِ..." (What is it with this messenger...).

The Quran says in response to them: This is not unique to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he eats food and walks in the marketplaces; all of them possessed such attributes. And assuming they were to extend this objection to all prophets, the Quran has already provided their answer where it says: وَ لَوْ جَعَلْناهُ مَلَكاً لَجَعَلْناهُ رَجُلًا: "And if We had made him an angel, We would have made him [in the form of] a man" (so that he could be a role model and example in all fields for humans). This points to the fact that only a human can be a leader for humans, as he is aware of all their needs, desires, problems, and issues.

Ayahs

۞ وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَرْجُونَ لِقَآءَنَا لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ نَرَىٰ رَبَّنَا ۗ لَقَدِ ٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَعَتَوْ عُتُوًّا كَبِيرًا (٢١)
Those who do not expect to encounter Us say, ‘Why have angels not been sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?’ Certainly, they are full of arrogance within their souls and have become terribly defiant. (21)
يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ لَا بُشْرَىٰ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُجْرِمِينَ وَيَقُولُونَ حِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا (٢٢)
The day they will see the angels, there will be no good news for the guilty on that day, and they will say, ‘Keep off [from paradise]!’ (22)
وَقَدِمْنَآ إِلَىٰ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ هَبَآءً مَّنثُورًا (٢٣)
Then We shall attend to the works they have done and then turn them into scattered dust. (23)
أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُّسْتَقَرًّا وَأَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا (٢٤)
On that day the inhabitants of paradise will be in the best abode and an excellent resting place. (24)

Commentary

Grandiose Claims

We have stated that the stubborn polytheists, in order to escape the burden of commitments and responsibilities that faith in God and the Resurrection places upon them, raised excuses. One of these was: why does the Prophet need food like us and walk in the marketplaces? The answer to this was provided in the previous verses.

The verses under discussion raise and answer two other parts of their excuses. First, He says:

"And those who do not expect the meeting with Us (and deny the Resurrection) say, 'Why are the angels not sent down to us, or why do we not see our Lord?'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ لا يَرْجُونَ لِقاءَنا لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ عَلَيْنَا الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ نَرى‌ رَبَّنا).

Supposing we accept that a Prophet can have an ordinary life like ours, it is not acceptable that the messenger of revelation should descend only upon him while we do not see him! What prevents an angel from appearing to endorse his prophethood, or to recount part of the revelation in our presence?! Or why can we not see God with our own eyes so that no doubt or ambiguity remains for us?

These are the things that raise questions for us and prevent us from accepting the call of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)! Importantly, the Quran describes these excuse-seekers as "those who do not expect the meeting with Us," which indicates that the source of these baseless sayings is a lack of faith in the Hereafter and a refusal to accept responsibility before God.

In verse 7 of Surah Al-Hijr, we read a similar statement where they said:

لَوْ ما تَأْتِينا بِالْمَلائِكَةِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ‌: "Why do you not bring the angels to us if you are of the truthful?"
