At the beginning of this very Surah Al-Furqan, we also read that the polytheists said: لَوْ لا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَلَكٌ فَيَكُونَ مَعَهُ نَذِيراً: "Why has an angel not been sent down to him to be a warner with him?"

While a truth-seeking person only demands evidence to prove a matter, and the type of evidence surely makes no difference, once the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) has clearly proven the truth of his call by showing miracles—including the Quran itself—what is the meaning of these excuses?

The best proof that they did not say these things for the sake of researching the Prophet's prophethood is that they demanded to see the Lord, reducing Him to the level of a visible physical body. This is the same baseless demand that the criminals of the Children of Israel had, and they received a decisive answer, the details of which are mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 143.

Therefore, in response to these demands, the Quran says at the end of the verse under discussion: "They have certainly become arrogant within themselves and have become caught up in pride, conceit, and self-centeredness" (لَقَدِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌).

"And they have transgressed with a great transgression" (وَ عَتَوْا عُتُوًّا كَبِيراً).

"Utu" (on the weight of ghuluw) means refraining from obedience and rebelling against a command, coupled with enmity and stubbornness.

The expression "within themselves" (فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌) might mean that they became afflicted with pride and conceit regarding themselves, or it might mean that they hid their pride and arrogance in their hearts while making these excuses manifest. In our age and time, there are also those who repeat the logic of the former polytheists and say: until we see God in our laboratory environment and until we observe the soul under the surgeon's knife, we will not believe! The source of both is the same: arrogance and rebellion.

Fundamentally, all those who consider the only tools of knowledge to be the senses and experience are implicitly repeating this same statement, and all materialists are included in this group, whereas our senses are only capable of perceiving a tiny portion of the matter of this world.

Then, as a threat, He says that those who demand to see the angels will eventually see them: "But the day they see the angels, there will be no glad tidings that day for the criminals" (because it is the day of punishment and painful retribution for their deeds) (يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلائِكَةُ لا بُشْرى‌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْمُجْرِمِينَ‌) [1]. Yes, on that day they will not be happy to see the angels; rather, because they see the signs of punishment along with them, they will be so plunged into terror that they will utter the same phrase they used in the world when feeling danger from others: "And they will say, 'Grant us refuge, grant us immunity'" (وَ يَقُولُونَ حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً).

But undoubtedly, neither this phrase nor any other will have an effect on their inevitable fate, for the fire they themselves have kindled will inevitably engulf them, and the evil deeds they have performed will be manifested before them; there is no remedy for what one has done to oneself!

[1]: The word "la" (no) here may be for negation, as many commentators have said, and it is also possible it is in the position of an imprecation (curse), in which case the meaning of the sentence would be: "On that day, may there be no glad tidings for the criminals."

The word "hijr" (on the weight of qishr) originally refers to an area that has been walled off with stones (tahjir) and made forbidden to enter. If we see that the "Hijr of Ismail" is called "Hijr," it is because of the wall drawn around it, separating it. Intellect is also called "hijr" because it prevents (yamna') a person from wrong actions; hence, in verse 5 of Surah Al-Fajr, we read: هَلْ فِي ذلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِذِي حِجْرٍ (Is there in that an oath for one of intellect?).

Also, the "Companions of al-Hijr," whose name appears in the Quran (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 80), refers to the people of Salih (عليه السلام) who carved strong stone houses in the heart of the mountains for themselves and placed themselves under their protection.

This concerns the meaning of the word "hijr."

However, the phrase "hijran mahjura" was an idiom among the Arabs; when they encountered someone they feared, they would say this phrase to them to seek safety/asylum.

Specifically, it was the custom of the Arabs that during the sacred months when war was forbidden, when someone encountered another and suspected that this tradition might be broken and harm might come to him, he would repeat this phrase. The other party, upon hearing it, would grant him safety and relieve him of anxiety, distress, and terror. Therefore, the meaning of the said phrase is: "I seek a protection, a protection that cannot be revoked or changed" [1]. Incidentally, from what we said above, it becomes clear that here the speakers of this phrase are the criminals, and the context of the verbs in the verse, the historical course, and the background of this phrase among the Arabs also necessitate this.

[1]: From a grammatical perspective, "hijran" is the object of an omitted verb, and "mahjuran" comes as an emphasis; originally it was: "I seek from you a prevention to which there is no way of removal or repulsion."

This is the case, even though some have suggested that the speakers of this phrase will be the angels, and their goal is to forbid the polytheists from the mercy of Allah.

Some have also said that the speakers of this phrase are the criminals saying it to one another, but the apparent meaning is the first one, which many commentators have chosen or mentioned as the primary interpretation [1]. But as for which day it is that the criminals meet the angels, commentators have given two possibilities: some say it is the day of death when a person sees the Angel of Death, as we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 97: وَ لَوْ تَرى‌ إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي غَمَراتِ الْمَوْتِ وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ باسِطُوا أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ‌: "And if you could but see when the wrongdoers are in the floods of death and the angels are stretching out their hands, saying, 'Discharge your souls!'"

Others have chosen the interpretation that it refers to the Day of Judgment and Resurrection when the criminals face the angels of punishment and observe them.

The second interpretation seems closer, considering the subsequent verses that speak of the Resurrection, especially since the phrase "that day" (yawma'idhin) points to it.

The next verse visualizes the state of the deeds of these criminals in the Hereafter, saying: "And We shall turn to whatever deeds they did and make them as scattered dust" (وَ قَدِمْنا إِلى‌ ما عَمِلُوا مِنْ عَمَلٍ فَجَعَلْناهُ هَباءً مَنْثُوراً).

[1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Tafsir Fi Zilal, and Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi under the verse discussed.

The word "amal" (deed), as Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat, refers to any action performed with intention, whereas "fi'l" (action) is more general, meaning it applies to actions performed both with and without intention [1]. The phrase "qadimna" is from the root "qudum," meaning "to arrive" or "to go toward something," and here it serves as evidence for the emphasis and seriousness of the matter; that is, certainly and definitely, all their deeds which they performed with attention and will—even if they were outwardly good deeds—We shall erase and destroy like particles of dust in the air because of their polytheism and disbelief.

The Blights of Righteous Deeds

The word "haba'" refers to very fine particles of dust that are normally not visible at all, except when sunlight falls through a hole or window into a dark room, making these particles illuminated and observable.

This expression shows that their deeds will become so worthless and ineffective that it will be as if no deed exists at all, even if they had struggled and strived for many years.

This verse is similar to verse 18 of Surah Ibrahim, which says: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ أَعْمالُهُمْ كَرَمادٍ اشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي يَوْمٍ عاصِفٍ‌: "The example of those who disbelieve in their Lord is that their deeds are like ashes on which the wind blows forcefully on a stormy day."

[1]: Raghib mentions this distinction under the root "amal," although he states the opposite under the root "fi'l"; however, considering the usage of these two words, this distinction seems correct. Of course, there may be exceptions, such as calling working oxen "awamil."

The logical reason for this is also clear, because what gives shape and content to a human's deed is the intention, motive, and ultimate goal of the action. People of faith, with divine and monotheistic motives, holy and pure goals, and a correct and healthy program, go about performing tasks, whereas people without faith are often caught up in showing off, hypocrisy, deception, pride, and conceit, and this is what causes their deeds to become worthless.

As an example, we know of mosques from hundreds of years ago that the passage of centuries has had the least effect upon, and conversely, we see houses where, after a month or a year, numerous cracks and defects appear. The first, because it had a divine motive, was built strong in every respect with the best materials and with foresight for all future events; but the second, because the goal was to acquire wealth through pretense and deception, only its outward appearance was attended to [1]. Fundamentally, from the perspective of Islamic logic, righteous deeds have blights that must be carefully guarded against: sometimes they are ruined and corrupt from the start, such as a deed performed for "riya" (showing off).

Sometimes, during the deed, a person becomes caught up in pride, vanity, and conceit, and the value of the deed is lost because of it.

And sometimes, after the deed, because of performing contradictory and incompatible actions, its effect is erased and destroyed, like charity followed by "mann" (reminding of the favor), or righteous deeds followed by disbelief and apostasy.

And even according to some Islamic narrations, sometimes committing sins before performing a deed affects it.

[1]: We have discussed this in more detail in Volume 10 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 308 onwards (under verse 18 of Surah Ibrahim).

As we read regarding the wine-drinker, his deeds will not be accepted by God for forty days [1]. In any case, Islam has an exceptionally precise, subtle, and calculated program regarding the characteristics of a righteous deed.

In a tradition from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah will resurrect a group while a light is before them like white and shining garments (this light is their deeds). Then He will command those deeds: 'All of you become scattered particles of dust' (and following that, they all vanish)." Then he said:

"They used to fast and pray, but when something forbidden was presented to them, they would seize it, and when something of the virtues of the Commander of the Faithful (Ali عليه السلام) was mentioned to them, they would deny it!" [2]

And since the Quran usually places good and evil against each other so that by comparison, the state of both becomes clear, the next verse speaks of the people of Paradise and says: "The companions of Paradise on that day will be in a better settlement and a finer resting place" (أَصْحابُ الْجَنَّةِ يَوْمَئِذٍ خَيْرٌ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَ أَحْسَنُ مَقِيلًا).

The meaning of this statement is not that the state of the people of Hell is good and the people of Paradise are better than them, for the "elative" form (comparative/superlative) is sometimes used in cases where one side completely lacks that concept and the other side possesses it. For example, in Surah Fussilat, verse 40, we read: أَ فَمَنْ يُلْقى‌ فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَمْ مَنْ يَأْتِي آمِناً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ: "Is he who is cast into the Fire better or he who comes secure on the Day of Resurrection?" "Mustaqarr" means a settlement or dwelling place, and "maqil" means "a place of rest at midday" (derived from the root "qaylulah," meaning a midday nap).

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 427, under the root "khamr." [2]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 9.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِٱلْغَمَـٰمِ وَنُزِّلَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ تَنزِيلًا (٢٥)
The day when the sky with its clouds will be split open, and the angels will be sent down [in a majestic] descent, (25)
ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۚ وَكَانَ يَوْمًا عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَسِيرًا (٢٦)
on that day true sovereignty will belong to the All-beneficent, and it will be a hard day for the faithless. (26)

Commentary

The Sky is Rent Asunder with Clouds!

In these verses, the discussion regarding the Resurrection and the fate of the criminals on that day continues. First, it says: "The day of distress and sorrow for the criminals is the day when the sky shall be rent asunder with clouds, and the angels shall be sent down in successive ranks!" (وَ يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ بِالْغَمامِ وَ نُزِّلَ الْمَلائِكَةُ تَنْزِيلًا) [^1]. The word ghamām (cloud) is derived from ghamm, meaning to cover something. Since clouds cover the sun, they are called ghamām; similarly, sorrow that covers the heart is called ghamm.

This verse is, in fact, a response to a request by the polytheists and another one of their excuses. They expected that Allah and the angels—according to their myths and legends—would come to them amidst the clouds and invite them toward the truth. In Jewish myths as well, it is mentioned that sometimes God would appear within the clouds! [^1] In response to them, the Quran says: Yes, the angels (and not God) will come to them one day, but which day? The day when the punishment and retribution for these evildoers arrives and puts an end to their vain talk.

Now let us see what is meant by the sky being rent asunder with clouds, considering we know that there is nothing around us called "the sky" that is capable of being physically torn apart.

Some commentators, such as "Allamah Tabataba'i" (may Allah be pleased with him) in Tafsir al-Mizan, have stated: The meaning is the rending of the sky of the world of perception (alam al-shuhud), the removal of the veil of ignorance, and the manifestation of the Unseen world (alam al-ghayb). That is, on that day, man will acquire a perception and vision vastly different from today; the veils will be lifted, and he will see the angels descending from the higher realm.

Another interpretation is that "sky" (sama') refers to the celestial bodies which will disintegrate due to successive explosions. The clouds resulting from these explosions and the crumbling of mountains will cover the expanse of the heavens. Thus, the celestial bodies are rent asunder while accompanied by the clouds resulting from them. [^2]

[^1]: The phrase "يَوْمَ تَشَقَّقُ السَّماءُ" is actually conjoined to the phrase "يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ الْمَلائِكَةَ" which appeared previously. Therefore, "Day" here relates to the same thing as in the previous verse, namely the phrase "لا بشرى" (no glad tidings). Some believe it relates to an omitted verb, such as "Remember." Additionally, the "ba" in "بالغمام" may denote accompaniment, or mean "from," or denote causality, as reflected in the commentary above. [^1]: Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 154 (under the verse in question). [^2]: From a linguistic perspective, the "ba" in this case denotes accompaniment (mulabasah).

Many verses of the Holy Quran, especially those in the short Surahs at the end of the Quran, describe this reality: that on the threshold of the Resurrection, massive upheavals and a strange revolution and transformation will encompass the entire universe. Mountains will disintegrate and scatter like dust in the air, the sun will lose its light, the stars will lose their brilliance, even the distance between the moon and the sun will vanish, and a tremendous tremor and earthquake will seize the entire earth.

Indeed, on such a day, the disintegration of the sky—meaning the celestial bodies—and the covering of the sky's surface by a thick cloud will be a natural occurrence.

This same interpretation can be expressed in another way: The intensity of the transformations and the explosions of the stars and planets will cause the sky to be covered by a thick cloud, yet within these clouds, there will sometimes be rifts. Therefore, the sky that is normally seen with the eye will be separated by these massive explosive clouds. [^1] Other interpretations have also been mentioned for this verse which do not align with scientific and logical principles. At the same time, the three aforementioned interpretations are not mutually exclusive. It is possible that, on one hand, the veils of the material world are removed from before man's eyes so he may witness the metaphysical world, and on the other hand, the celestial bodies disintegrate and explosive clouds appear with rifts visible among them. That day is the day of the end of this world and the beginning of the Resurrection—an extremely painful day for faithless criminals and stubborn oppressors.

Then, it addresses one of the clearest characteristics of that day, saying:

[^1]: In this case, the "ba" in "بالغمام" denotes causality.

"The sovereignty on that day belongs to Allah, the Most Compassionate" (الْمُلْكُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ لِلرَّحْمنِ).

Even those who held a form of metaphorical, limited, mortal, and fleeting sovereignty in this world will depart from the stage of rule, and sovereignty in every sense and in all aspects will belong exclusively to His Pure Essence. For this reason, "that day will be a hard day for the disbelievers" (وَ كانَ يَوْماً عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ عَسِيراً).

Indeed, on that day when imaginary powers completely vanish and sovereignty becomes exclusive to Allah, the refuges of the disbelievers will collapse, and the tyrannical and rebellious powers will be obliterated. Although in this world too, everyone was insignificant before His will, here they seemingly possessed some glitter and glamour. But in the Resurrection, which is the stage for the manifestation of realities and the folding away of metaphors, fancies, and imaginations, what support can faithless individuals have against Divine punishments? For this reason, that day will pass with extreme difficulty for them.

In contrast, for the believers, it will be very easy and simple.

In a Hadith narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, it is mentioned that when the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) recited the verse: "فِي يَوْمٍ كانَ مِقْدارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ" (In a day the measure whereof is fifty thousand years), which indicates that the Day of Resurrection is equivalent to fifty thousand years, I said: "What a long and strange day!" The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said:

"By Him in Whose hand is my soul, it will be made light for the believer until it becomes lighter for him than a single obligatory prayer he performs in the world!" [^1]

Reflecting on other verses of the Quran clarifies the reasons why that day is hard for the disbelievers. For on one hand, we read: "On that day all ties and means of the world of causes shall be severed" (وَ تَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ) (Baqarah: 166).

On the other hand: "Neither his wealth nor what he earned shall profit him" (ما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُ مالُهُ وَ ما كَسَبَ) (Surah Tabbat: 2).

And on a third hand: "The day when no friend shall avail a friend in aught" (يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً) (Dukhan: 41).

Even intercession, which is the only way of salvation, is reserved for those sinners who had a connection with Allah and the friends of Allah (مَنْ ذَا الَّذِي يَشْفَعُ عِنْدَهُ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِ) (Baqarah: 255).

Furthermore: "On that day they shall not be permitted to offer excuses," let alone have unjustified excuses accepted (وَ لا يُؤْذَنُ لَهُمْ فَيَعْتَذِرُونَ) (Mursalat: 36).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 7, p. 4739.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ يَعَضُّ ٱلظَّالِمُ عَلَىٰ يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى ٱتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ ٱلرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا (٢٧)
It will be a day when the wrongdoer will bite his hands, saying, ‘I wish I had followed the Apostle’s way! (27)
يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰ لَيْتَنِى لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلَانًا خَلِيلًا (٢٨)
Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so and so as a friend! (28)
لَّقَدْ أَضَلَّنِى عَنِ ٱلذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جَآءَنِى ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ خَذُولًا (٢٩)
Certainly he led me astray from the Reminder after it had come to me, and Satan is a deserter of man.’ (29)

Occasion of Revelation

A specific occasion of revelation has been narrated for these verses, the summary of which is as follows:

"During the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, there were two friends among the polytheists named 'Uqbah and Ubayy. Whenever 'Uqbah returned from a journey, he would prepare a meal and invite the nobles of his tribe; at the same time, he liked to visit the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, even though he had not accepted Islam.

One day, he returned from a journey, prepared food as usual, and invited his friends. He also invited the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله.

When the spread was laid and the food was ready, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'I will not eat from your food until you bear witness to the Oneness of Allah and my messengership.' 'Uqbah recited the Shahadatayn (the dual testimony of faith).

This news reached his friend Ubayy, who said: 'O 'Uqbah! Have you deviated from your religion?' He replied: 'No, by Allah, I have not deviated, but a man came to me who refused to eat my food unless I recited the Shahadatayn. I was ashamed that he should rise from my table without having eaten, so I testified!' Ubayy said: 'I will never be pleased with you unless you stand before him and severely insult him!' 'Uqbah did so and apostatized. Ultimately, he was killed in the Battle of Badr among the ranks of the disbelievers, and his friend Ubayy was also killed on the day of the Battle of Uhud. [^1] The above verses were revealed, describing the fate of a man who, in this world, falls victim to a misguided friend who leads him into deviation."

As we have repeatedly stated, although the occasions of revelation are specific, they never limit the meaning of the verses; rather, their generality encompasses all similar individuals.

Commentary

An Evil Friend Led Me Astray!

The Day of Resurrection has wondrous scenes, part of which appeared in the previous verses, and another part is highlighted in the verses under discussion—namely, the extreme regret of the wrongdoers over their past. First, He says:

"And remember the Day when the wrongdoer will bite his hands in [extreme] regret, saying: 'Oh, I wish I had taken a path with the Messenger!'" (وَ يَوْمَ يَعَضُّ الظَّالِمُ عَلى‌ يَدَيْهِ يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي اتَّخَذْتُ مَعَ الرَّسُولِ سَبِيلًا) [^1]

"Ya'addu" is derived from the root "add" (rhyming with sadd), meaning to bite with the teeth. This expression is usually used for those who are distressed by the intensity of regret and sorrow. Just as in Persian there is a proverb that someone "bit the finger of regret" (though in Arabic, "hands" is used instead of "finger," which is perhaps more expressive because a person in such states does not always bite a finger, but sometimes the back of the hand; especially in Arabic, as in the verse under discussion, "yadayhi" meaning "both hands" is often used, which expresses the intensity of sorrow and regret in a more eloquent manner).

Beware of what the enemy says; do that, Lest you bite the hand of loss with your teeth. [^2]

This action is perhaps because such individuals, when they look at their past, see themselves as the culprits and decide to take revenge upon themselves; this is a form of self-vengeance through which they might find a measure of relief.

Truly, that day must be called "Yawm al-Hasrah" (the Day of Regret), just as the Day of Resurrection is referred to by this title in the Quran (Surah Maryam, Verse 39). This is because the wrongdoers find themselves facing an eternal life in the worst conditions, whereas they could have transformed it into a proud and blissful life with a few days of patience, endurance, struggling against the self, striving, and sacrifice.

Even for the righteous, it is a day of regret—regret over why they did not perform even more good deeds!

Then it adds that this tyrannical wrongdoer, immersed in a world of sorrow, says: "Woe to me! I wish I had not taken so-and-so as a friend!" (يا وَيْلَتى‌ لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أَتَّخِذْ فُلاناً خَلِيلًا) [^1] It is clear that "so-and-so" refers to the person who led him astray: Satan, an evil friend, or a misguided relative—someone like Ubayy for 'Uqbah, as mentioned in the occasion of revelation.

In fact, this verse and the preceding one place two states—negation and affirmation—opposite each other. In one place he says: "I wish I had found a path to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله," and here he says: "I wish I had not chosen so-and-so as my friend." All the misery lay in abandoning the connection with the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and accepting a connection with this misguided friend.

He continues further and says: "Awakening and awareness had come to me (and prosperity knocked at my door), but this faithless friend led me astray" (لَقَدْ أَضَلَّنِي عَنِ الذِّكْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ جاءَنِي‌).

If I had been far from faith and eternal bliss, it would not be so regrettable; but I had advanced to the very border, only one step remained to be happy forever, yet this blind-hearted, prejudiced, and stubborn person turned me back thirsty from the brink of the fountain of the water of life and plunged me into the whirlpool of misery.

"Dhikr" (Remembrance) in the above sentence has a broad meaning and includes all the divine verses revealed in the Heavenly Books; rather, it encompasses everything that causes the awakening and awareness of man.

And at the end of the verse, it says: "And Satan has always been a deserter of man" (وَ كانَ الشَّيْطانُ لِلْإِنْسانِ خَذُولًا).

For he leads man into bypasses and dangerous areas and then leaves him wandering and goes about his own business.

It should be noted that "khadhul" is an intensive form (Sighah al-Mubalaghah) meaning "one who deserts greatly."

The reality of khidhlan (desertion) is that someone pins their hopes on the help of another, but that person withdraws their help and support exactly at the critical moments.

Regarding whether the final sentence (كانَ الشَّيْطانُ لِلْإِنْسانِ خَذُولًا) is the word of Allah as a warning to all wrongdoers and the misguided, or a continuation of the speech of these regretful individuals on the Day of Resurrection, commentators have mentioned two interpretations. Both are consistent with the meaning of the verse, but it being the word of Allah is more fitting.

Key Point: The Role of a Friend in Human Destiny

Undoubtedly, the factor that shapes human personality—after one's own will, desire, and decision—consists of various matters, the most important of which is one's companion, friend, and associate. This is because man is inevitably influenced and acquires a significant portion of his thoughts and moral traits through his friends. This reality has been proven both scientifically and through empirical experience and observation.

This susceptibility to influence, from the perspective of Islamic logic, is to such an extent that it is narrated in Islamic traditions from the Prophet of Allah, Prophet Solomon عليه السلام: "Do not judge a man by anything until you look at who he associates with, for a man is known by his likes and peers, and is attributed to his companions and friends." [^1] Imam Amir al-Mu'minin Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام says in his eloquent speech:

"Whenever the condition of someone is ambiguous to you and you do not know his religion, look at his associates. If they are people of the religion of Allah, then he is on the religion of Allah; and if they are not on the religion of Allah, then he has no share of the religion of Allah." [^2]

Truly, sometimes the role of a friend in the prosperity or misery of a human being is more important than any other factor; sometimes he leads one to the brink of annihilation and non-existence, and sometimes he raises one to the peak of honor.

The verses under discussion and their occasion of revelation clearly show how a person might advance to the border of bliss, yet a satanic temptation from an evil friend pulls him back into regression and prepares a deadly fate for him, such that out of regret on the Day of Resurrection, he bites both hands and cries out "Woe to me!"

In the Kitab al-'Ishrah (The Book of Social Etiquette), many narrations have been recorded in this regard, showing to what extent Islam is strict, precise, and meticulous in the matter of choosing a friend.

We conclude this brief discussion by quoting two hadiths in this regard; those who want more information on this subject may refer to Kitab al-'Ishrah in Bihar al-Anwar, Volume 74.

In a hadith from the ninth great leader of Islam, Imam Muhammad al-Taqi al-Jawad عليه السلام, we read: "Beware of associating with the wicked, for he is like a drawn sword; his appearance is beautiful and his effect is very ugly!" [^1]

The Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Four things deaden the heart: sin upon sin... and sitting with the dead." Someone asked: "O Messenger of Allah, who are the dead?" He replied: "Every wealthy person who is intoxicated by wealth." [^2]

Commentary

O Lord, the People Have Abandoned the Quran!

Since the preceding verses addressed various excuses made by the stubborn polytheists and faithless individuals, the first verse under discussion recounts the distress and complaint of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) before Allah regarding how this group treated the Quran. It says:

"And the Messenger said: 'O my Lord! Indeed, my people have taken this Quran as something abandoned'" (وَ قالَ الرَّسُولُ يا رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي اتَّخَذُوا هذَا الْقُرْآنَ مَهْجُوراً) [^1]. This statement and this complaint of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) continue even today, as he complains to Allah's presence regarding a vast group of Muslims who have consigned this Quran to oblivion—the Quran which is the secret of life and the means of salvation; the Quran which is the factor of victory, movement, and progress; the Quran which is filled with programs for living. They have abandoned this Quran and have even stretched out a begging hand toward others for their civil and penal laws!

[^1]: The appearance of the word "قال" (said) is a past-tense verb, indicating that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) voiced this complaint in this world, and most commentators hold this view. However, others, such as Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, believe this statement pertains to the Day of Resurrection, and the past tense here functions as the future. The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan also mentions it as a possibility, but the subsequent verse, which offers comfort to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), serves as evidence that the popular interpretation is more correct.

If we look at the current state of many Islamic countries, especially those living under the cultural hegemony of the East and West, we see that the Quran has become a ceremonial book among them. Only its phonetics are broadcast with attractive voices from transmitters, and its place is in the tilework of mosques as architectural art. It is used for inaugurating a new house, protecting a traveler, healing the sick, and at most, for recitation to gain spiritual reward.

Even when they occasionally argue using the Quran, their goal is to prove their own preconceived notions with the help of verses, utilizing the deviant method of "interpretation by personal opinion" (Tafsir bi al-Ra'y).

In some Islamic countries, expansive schools are seen under the title of "Quran Memorization Schools," where a vast group of boys and girls are busy memorizing the Quran, while their thoughts are sometimes taken from the West and sometimes from the East, and their laws and regulations are derived from those alien to Islam. The Quran is merely a cover for their wrongdoings.

Yes, today too, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) cries out: "O Allah! My people have held the Quran abandoned!" Abandoned in terms of its essence and content, forsaken in terms of reflection and thought, and forsaken in terms of its constructive programs!

The next verse, to comfort the noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) who was faced with this hostile stance of the enemies, says: "And thus have We made for every prophet an enemy from among the criminals" (وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا مِنَ الْمُجْرِمِينَ‌). You are not the only one faced with the stubborn enmity of this group; all prophets were in such circumstances where a group of "criminals" rose in opposition to them and girded themselves for enmity.

But know that you are not alone or without a helper: "And sufficient is your Lord as a Guide and a Helper" (وَ كَفى‌ بِرَبِّكَ هادِياً وَ نَصِيراً). Neither can their whisperings lead you astray, for your Guide is Allah, nor can their conspiracies break you, for your Helper is your Lord, whose knowledge is the highest of all knowledge and whose power is above all powers.

In short, you must say: If a thousand enemies intend my destruction, Since I have You as a friend, I have no fear of enemies.

In the following verse, it points to another excuse of these pretext-seeking criminals, saying: "And those who disbelieve say: 'Why was the Quran not revealed to him all at once?'" (وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ عَلَيْهِ الْقُرْآنُ جُمْلَةً واحِدَةً). Is it not all from Allah? Would it not be better if the beginning, the end, and the entire content of this book were revealed at once so that people would be more aware of its greatness? In short, why are these verses revealed gradually and at different intervals of time?

Of course, for superficial individuals, especially if they are looking for excuses, this objection regarding the manner of the Quran's revelation will arise: why was this great heavenly book—which is the foundation and substance of everything for Muslims and the axis of all their political, social, legal, and devotional laws—not revealed completely at once to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), so that people could constantly read it from beginning to end and become aware of its content?

Fundamentally, it might seem better if the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) himself were aware of the entire Quran at once so that he could immediately answer whatever people asked of him.

However, in the continuation of this very verse, He answers them as such:

"Thus [it is revealed] that We may strengthen thereby your heart, and We have spaced it out in a slow, well-ordered stages" (كَذلِكَ لِنُثَبِّتَ بِهِ فُؤادَكَ وَ رَتَّلْناهُ تَرْتِيلًا). They are unaware of these realities, which is why they raise such objections.

Indeed, the connection between the gradual revelation of the Quran and the strengthening of the heart of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers has an interesting and detailed discussion that will come in the points at the end of these verses.

Then, to emphasize the above answer further, He says: "And they do not bring to you an example [or objection] but We bring to you the truth [which breaks their weak arguments] in the most decisive way and the best explanation" (وَ لا يَأْتُونَكَ بِمَثَلٍ إِلَّا جِئْناكَ بِالْحَقِّ وَ أَحْسَنَ تَفْسِيراً).

And since these spiteful enemies and prejudiced, stubborn polytheists had concluded after their set of objections that Muhammad and his companions—with these attributes, this book, and these programs—were the worst of Allah's creation (God forbid), and because mentioning such a statement was not fitting for eloquent and sublime speech like the Quran, in the final verse under discussion, Allah addresses the answer to this claim without quoting their original words, saying:

"Those who will be gathered on their faces to Hell—those are the worst in position and the most astray in way" (الَّذِينَ يُحْشَرُونَ عَلى‌ وُجُوهِهِمْ إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ أُوْلئِكَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا).

Yes, the result of the life programs of humans becomes clear there; one group has statures like cypress trees and faces shining like the moon, moving swiftly toward Paradise with long strides. In contrast, a group falls on their faces on the earth, and the angels of punishment drag them toward Hell. This differing fate shows who was astray and evil, and who was fortunate and guided!

Key Points

1- Commentary on "We made for every prophet an enemy"

Sometimes it might seem from this phrase that Allah is saying to comfort the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "You are not the only one who has an enemy; rather, for every prophet, an enemy has been placed by Us." The implication of this statement is attributing the existence of the prophets' enemies to Allah, which is consistent neither with Allah's wisdom nor with the principle of human free will.

Commentators have given several answers to this question.

However, as we have repeatedly stated, all human actions are attributable to Allah from one perspective, because everything we have—our power, our strength, our intellect, and even our freedom and choice—is from Him. Therefore, the existence of these enemies for the prophets can be attributed to Allah from this perspective without necessitating predestination or the negation of choice, and without any flaw entering their responsibility for their actions (reflect carefully).

Furthermore, the existence of these stubborn enemies and their opposition to the prophets caused the believers to become more skilled and steadfast in their work, and for the divine trial to be realized for everyone.

This verse is, in fact, similar to verse 112 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: "And thus We have made for every prophet an enemy—devils from mankind and jinn, inspiring to one another decorative speech in delusion" (وَ كَذلِكَ جَعَلْنا لِكُلِّ نَبِيٍّ عَدُوًّا شَياطِينَ الْإِنْسِ وَ الْجِنِّ يُوحِي بَعْضُهُمْ إِلى‌ بَعْضٍ زُخْرُفَ الْقَوْلِ غُرُوراً).

Opposite flowers, thorns grow; and opposite the good, the evil stand, without the responsibility of either group being removed.

Some have also said that "We made" (ja'alna) refers to the commands, prohibitions, and constructive programs of the prophets, which inevitably draw some people into enmity; it is attributed to Allah because these commands and prohibitions are from Him.

Another interpretation is that there are those who, due to persistence in sin and excess in prejudice and stubbornness, have their hearts sealed by Allah, and their eyes and ears made blind and deaf. This group, due to their blindness of heart, eventually becomes enemies of the prophets, but they themselves provided the causes for it.

These three interpretations do not contradict each other, and all of them may be combined within the concept of the verse.

2- The Deep Effects of the Gradual Revelation of the Quran

It is true that according to narrations (and indeed the appearance of some verses), the Quran had two revelations: one "instantaneous revelation" (nuzul-e daf'i) all at once on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr) upon the heart of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the other "gradual revelation" (nuzul-e tadriji) over twenty-three years. However, undoubtedly, what held the official aspect and what the Prophet and the people faced was the gradual revelation of the Quran.

And it was this very gradual revelation that provided the grounds for the objections of the pretext-seeking enemies: "Why is the Quran not revealed all at once? And why is it not placed at the disposal of the people at one time so they may become more aware and enlightened, leaving no room for doubt?"

But as we saw, the Quran gave them a short, comprehensive, and meaningful answer with the phrase "Thus [it is revealed] that We may strengthen thereby your heart." The more we reflect upon it, the clearer the effects of the gradual revelation of the Quran become:

  1. Undoubtedly, both in terms of "receiving the revelation" and "conveying it to the people," if the material is implemented gradually and according to needs, and if there is a witness and an objective instance for every matter, it will be much more effective. Educational principles require that the person or persons being educated traverse this path step by step, and that a program be organized for each of their days so they start from the lower stage and reach the higher stages. Programs implemented in this way are more appealing and profound for both the speaker and the listener.

  2. Fundamentally, those who raised such objections to the Quran did not pay attention to the fact that the Quran is not a "classic book" that discusses a specific subject or science; rather, it is a program of life for a nation that has undergone a revolution and draws inspiration from it in all dimensions of life. Many verses of the Quran were revealed on historical occasions such as the battles of Badr, Uhud, Ahzab, and Hunayn, and were instructions or conclusions drawn from these events. Does it make sense for these to be written all at once and presented to the people? In other words, the Quran is a collection of commands and prohibitions, rulings and laws, history and exhortation, and a set of various strategies and tactics in dealing with events that arose in the path of the Islamic Ummah as it moved forward. Such a book, which explains and implements all its programs—even its general laws—through presence in the scenes of people's lives, cannot possibly be compiled and organized all at once beforehand. This would be like a great leader writing and publishing all his proclamations, statements, and commands and prohibitions—which are delivered on various occasions—all at once to implement a revolution. Could anyone consider such a thing rational?!

  3. The gradual revelation of the Quran resulted in a constant and continuous connection between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Source of Revelation. This permanent connection made his heart stronger and his will more powerful, and its impact on his educational programs was undeniable.

  4. On the other hand, the continuation of revelation signifies the continuation of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission and embassy, leaving no room for the enemies' whisperings that "he was commissioned by God one day, then his God abandoned him!" As we read in Islamic history, when revelation was delayed at the beginning of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) prophethood, this murmur arose, and Surah Ad-Duha was revealed to negate it.

  5. Undoubtedly, if all the programs of Islam were to be revealed at once, it would have been necessary to implement them all at once as well, for revelation without implementation would nullify its value. We know that implementing all programs—including acts of worship, Zakat, Jihad, observing all obligations, and abstaining from all prohibitions—all at once would have been a very heavy task that would have caused a vast group to flee from Islam. So, it was better to be revealed gradually and acted upon gradually. In other words, each of these programs could be well absorbed, and if there were questions or discussions surrounding them, they could be raised and answered.

  6. Another effect of this gradual revelation is the clarification of the greatness and miraculous nature of the Quran. For in every event, verses are revealed that are themselves proof of greatness and miracle, and the more this is repeated, the clearer this greatness and miracle become, penetrating the depths of people's hearts.

3- The Meaning of Tarteel in the Quran

The word "Tarteel" is derived from the root "ratl," meaning to be organized and orderly. Therefore, the Arabs say of someone whose teeth are well-aligned and orderly, "ratl al-asnan." For this reason, Tarteel is said to mean bringing words or verses one after another according to a system and calculation.

Therefore, the phrase "and We have spaced it out in a slow, well-ordered stages" (wa rattalnahu tartila) refers to the fact that although the verses of the Quran were revealed gradually over a period of 23 years, this gradual revelation was based on order, calculation, and a program, in such a way that it would take root in thoughts and attract hearts to itself.

Interesting narrations have been quoted regarding the interpretation of the word "Tarteel," some of which we mention below:

In the commentary Majma' al-Bayan, it is narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said to Ibn Abbas: "When you read the Quran, recite it with Tarteel."

Ibn Abbas says: I asked, "What is Tarteel?"

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Articulate its letters and words clearly; do not scatter it like dried dates (or grains of sand), and do not recite it rapidly like poetry. Stop at its wonders, move the hearts with it, and let not the concern of any of you be [merely] to reach the end of the Surah." (Rather, the important thing is reflection, contemplation, and benefiting from the Quran.) [^1] A similar meaning is narrated in Usul al-Kafi from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام). [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 449 (Chapter on Reciting the Quran with a Beautiful Voice).

It is also narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Tarteel is that you pause during it and beautify your voice with it. When you pass by a verse mentioning the Fire, seek refuge in Allah from the Fire, and when you pass by a verse mentioning Paradise, ask Allah for Paradise." (Characterize yourself with the attributes of the people of Paradise and avoid the traits of the people of the Fire.) [^1]

4- Commentary on "Gathered on their faces to Hell"

There is much discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by this group of criminals being gathered on their faces:

A group has interpreted it in its literal sense and said that the angels of punishment will drag them to Hell while they are fallen on their faces on the ground. This is, on one hand, a sign of their humiliation and abasement, for in the world they possessed the ultimate pride, arrogance, and self-superiority toward Allah's creation. On the other hand, it is an embodiment of their being astray in this world, for someone being taken in this manner cannot see what is in front of him at all and is unaware of what is happening around him.

However, others have taken it in a metaphorical sense: some have said this phrase is a metaphor for their heart's attachment to the world; that is, because the "face" of their hearts is still connected to the world, they are dragged toward Hell. [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, root "ratl." [^2]: According to this interpretation, the expression "on their faces" ('ala wujuhihim) actually takes the place of the cause, and the meaning of the sentence becomes: "They are gathered to Hell because the faces of their hearts are attached to the world."

And some have said: This metaphor is like a specific expression in Arabic literature where they say: "So-and-so went on his face," meaning he did not know where he was going.

But it is clear that as long as we do not have evidence for a metaphorical meaning, it must be interpreted according to the first meaning, which is the literal one.

وَكُلًّا ضَرَبْنَا لَهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ ۖ وَكُلًّا تَبَّرْنَا تَتْبِيرًا (٣٩)
For each of them We drew examples, and each We destroyed utterly. (39)
وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا۟ عَلَى ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ ٱلسَّوْءِ ۚ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا۟ يَرَوْنَهَا ۚ بَلْ كَانُوا۟ لَا يَرْجُونَ نُشُورًا (٤٠)
Certainly they must have passed the town on which an evil shower was rained. Have they not seen it? Rather, they did not expect resurrection [to happen]. (40)

Commentary

So Many Lessons, Yet Such Heedlessness!

In these verses, the Holy Quran—on one hand to comfort the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the believers, and on the other to threaten the excuse-seeking polytheists whose words were mentioned in previous verses—refers to the history of past nations and their ill-fated destinies. It specifically emphasizes six groups (the people of Pharaoh, the people of Noah, the people of ‘Ad, Thamud, the People of al-Rass, and the people of Lot) and reflects the lessons to be learned from their stories in a highly condensed yet eloquent manner.

First, He says: "And indeed, We gave Moses the Scripture and appointed his brother Aaron with him as a helper" (وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْكِتَابَ وَجَعَلْنَا مَعَهُ أَخَاهُ هَارُونَ وَزِيراً).

This was because they bore a heavy burden in the struggle against the people of Pharaoh and had to bring this important revolutionary task to fruition through mutual support.

We addressed the two of them "and said: Go both of you to the people who have denied Our signs" (فَقُلْنَا اذْهَبَا إِلَى الْقَوْمِ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا).

On one hand, they practically denied the signs and tokens of Allah that exist in the horizons and within souls and throughout the entire universe, choosing the path of polytheism and idol worship. On the other hand, they ignored the teachings of previous prophets and denied them as well.

However, despite all the efforts and strivings of Moses and his brother, and despite seeing all those great, diverse, and clear miracles, they still pursued the path of disbelief and denial. Therefore, "We destroyed them with utter destruction" (فَدَمَّرْنَاهُمْ تَدْمِيراً).

The word "Tadmir" is derived from the root "Damar," meaning destruction in an astonishing way. Truly, the destruction of Pharaoh's people in the surging waves of the Nile, in the manner we know, is one of the wonders of human history.

Likewise, "And the people of Noah, when they denied the messengers, We drowned them and made them a clear sign for mankind, and We have prepared for the wrongdoers a painful punishment" (وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ لَمَّا كَذَّبُوا الرُّسُلَ أَغْرَقْنَاهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ لِلنَّاسِ آيَةً وَأَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ عَذَاباً أَلِيماً). It is noteworthy that He says: they denied "the messengers" (not just one messenger), because there is no difference among the messengers and prophets of Allah in the principles of their call; thus, denying one of them is denying all of them. Furthermore, they were fundamentally opposed to the call of all divine prophets and were deniers of all religions.

Also, "And ‘Ad and Thamud and the People of al-Rass and many generations between them [We destroyed]" (وَعَاداً وَثَمُودَ وَأَصْحَابَ الرَّسِّ وَقُرُوناً بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ كَثِيراً) [1]. The people of ‘Ad are the people of Hud, the great prophet of Allah, who was sent from the land of Ahqaf (or Yemen). The people of Thamud are the people of Allah's prophet Salih, who was sent from the land of Wadi al-Qura (between Medina and Levant). As for the "People of al-Rass," we will discuss them at the end of this discourse.

"Qurun" is the plural of "Qarn," which originally means a group of people living together at one time; subsequently, it has also been applied to a long period of time (forty years or one hundred years).

But We never punished them by surprise; rather, "For each of them We set forth parables" (وَكُلّاً ضَرَبْنَا لَهُ الْأَمْثَالَ). We answered their objections, just like the answers to the objections they raise against you. We clarified the divine laws for them and explained the truths of religion.

We warned them, cautioned them, and recounted the destinies and stories of those who came before them.

But when none of this proved beneficial, "each of them We broke into pieces and destroyed" (وَكُلّاً تَبَّرْنَا تَتْبِيراً) [2].

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, it refers to the ruins of the cities of the people of Lot, which were located on the route of the people of Hijaz to Levant, serving as a living and eloquent display of the painful fate of these defiled people and polytheists. He says: "And they have surely passed by the city upon which was rained the rain of evil (a rain of destructive stones). Have they not seen it (during their journeys to Levant) and reflected upon their lives?" (وَلَقَدْ أَتَوْا عَلَى الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي أُمْطِرَتْ مَطَرَ السَّوْءِ أَفَلَمْ يَكُونُوا يَرَوْنَهَا).

Yes, they had seen this scene, but they never took a lesson from it because "they do not expect a resurrection!" (بَلْ كَانُوا لَا يَرْجُونَ نُشُوراً).

They consider death to be the end of this life, and if they do believe in life after death, it is a very weak and baseless belief, such that it does not affect their souls and, by extension, their life programs. For this reason, they take everything as a joke and think of nothing but their fleeting whims.

[1]: The words "‘Ad" and "Thamud" are conjoined to the pronoun "them" in the phrase "We destroyed them." Some have also suggested they are conjoined to "them" in "We made them" or to the position of "the wrongdoers," but the first possibility is more appropriate.

[2]: "Tabbir" is from the root "Tabr" (rhyming with darar and sabr), meaning to perish and to be broken into pieces.

Key Points

1- Who are the "People of al-Rass"?

The word "Rass" originally means a slight trace. For example, it is said: Rassa al-hadithu fi nafsi (I remember a little of his speech) or Wajada rassan min humma (He found a slight trace of fever in himself) [1]. A group of commentators believe that "Rass" means a well.

In any case, naming this people by this name is either because very little trace of them remains, or because they had many water wells, or because they were destroyed and perished due to the sinking of their wells.

[1]: Mufradat by Raghib.

Regarding who these people were, there is much discussion among historians and commentators:

  1. Many believe that the "People of al-Rass" were a tribe living in "Yamama" to whom a prophet named "Hanzalah" was sent. They denied him and threw him into a well. Some have even written that they filled the well with spears and closed the mouth of the well with a stone after throwing him in until that prophet was martyred [1].

  2. Others consider the "People of al-Rass" to refer to the people of the time of "Shu'ayb" who were idolaters, possessing many sheep and water wells. "Rass" was the name of a large well that collapsed and swallowed the people there, destroying them.

  3. Others believe that "Rass" was a village in the land of "Yamama" where some remnants of the people of Thamud lived and were destroyed due to rebellion and defiance.

  4. Some say they were a group of ancient Arabs who lived between "Levant" and "Hijaz" [2].

  5. Some commentaries identify the People of al-Rass as remnants of ‘Ad and Thamud, and also relate the verse "And [many] an abandoned well and a lofty palace" (Surah Al-Hajj, Verse 45) to them, mentioning their location in "Hadramaut." Tha'labi in Ara'is al-Tijan considered this view more reliable.

Some other commentators who became familiar with the name "Aras" have identified "Rass" with the "Aras" river (in northern Azerbaijan)!

  1. The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Fakhr Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, among the possibilities they cited, mentioned that they were people living in Antioch, Levant, and their prophet was "Habib al-Najjar."

  2. In ‘Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, a long hadith is narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) regarding the People of al-Rass, the summary of which is as follows:

"They were a people who worshipped a pine tree and called it the 'King Tree.' It was a tree that 'Japheth' son of 'Noah' had planted after the Flood on a bank called 'Rowshan Ab.' They had twelve flourishing cities situated along a river called 'Rass.' These cities were named: Aban, Adhar, Day, Bahman, Isfandar, Farvardin, Ordibehesht, Khordad, Tir, Mordad, Shahrivar, and Mehr, from which the Persians took the names of their months.

Due to the respect they held for that pine tree, they planted its seeds in other areas and dedicated a river for its irrigation, such that they forbade themselves and their cattle from drinking the water of that river. If anyone drank from it, they would kill him, saying: 'This is the source of life for our gods, and it is not fitting for anyone to diminish it!' Every month of the year, they would celebrate a festival for one day in one of these flourishing cities, going to the pine tree outside the city, offering sacrifices to it, slaughtering animals, and then casting them into fire. When the smoke rose to the sky, they would fall in prostration before the tree, weeping and wailing! This was their custom in all these cities until it was the turn of the great city which was the capital of their king, named Isfandar. All the inhabitants of the settlements would gather there and celebrate for twelve consecutive days, sacrificing whatever they could and prostrating before the tree.

When they became deeply immersed in disbelief and idol worship, Allah sent a prophet from the Children of Israel to them to call them to the worship of the One God and the abandonment of polytheism. But they did not believe. To uproot the source of corruption, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) prayed to God, and that great tree withered. When they saw this, they became deeply distressed and said: 'This man has bewitched our gods!' Others said: 'The gods are angry with us because of this man who calls us to disbelief!'

Following these discussions, they all decided to kill that prophet. They dug a deep well, threw him into it, closed its top, and sat above it, continually hearing his moans until he passed away. Because of these evil deeds and these injustices, Allah afflicted them with a severe punishment and destroyed them" [1].

Several pieces of evidence support the content of this hadith, for despite the mention of the "People of al-Rass" alongside ‘Ad and Thamud, the possibility that they were a group from these two nations seems very unlikely.

Also, the existence of this people in the Arabian Peninsula or Levant and those regions, which many have suggested, is also unlikely, as it should normally have a reflection in Arab history, whereas we see very little reflection of the People of al-Rass in Arab history.

Furthermore, it is compatible with many other interpretations, including that "Rass" was the name of a well (the well they threw their prophet into) or that they were owners of agriculture and livestock, and the like.

And the fact that we read in a narration from Imam Sadiq (عليه السلام) that their women were involved in homosexual deviations does not contradict this hadith [2].

However, it appears from the wording of Nahj al-Balagha (Sermon 180) that they did not have only one prophet, as it says: "Where are the people of the cities of al-Rass who killed the prophets, extinguished the traditions of the messengers, and revived the traditions of the tyrants?" But this expression also does not contradict the above narration, as the narration might only refer to a part of their history when a prophet was sent among them.

[1]: ‘Uyun Akhbar al-Rida (عليه السلام) as cited and summarized in Tafsir al-Mizan, vol. 15, p. 237. [2]: Al-Kafi (as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 19).

2- A Collection of Shaking Lessons

The six groups named in the above verses—the people of Pharaoh, the fanatical people of Noah, the powerful ‘Ad and Thamud, the defiled People of al-Rass, and the people of Lot—were each afflicted with a type of intellectual and moral deviation that led them to misery.

The people of Pharaoh were oppressive, tyrannical, colonizing, exploiting, and egotistical. The people of Noah, as we know, were extremely stubborn, arrogant, and self-superior. The people of ‘Ad and Thamud relied on their own power. The People of al-Rass were immersed in a whirlpool of corruption, especially female homosexuality, and the people of Lot were wallowing in a quagmire of vice, especially male homosexuality. All of them deviated from the path of monotheism and wandered in lost ways.

The Quran intends to warn the polytheists of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) era and all humans throughout history that no matter how much power, ability, and status you have, and no matter how much wealth and comfortable living, being defiled by polytheism, injustice, and corruption will fold up the scroll of your life. The very factors of your victory will become the factors of your death!

The people of Pharaoh and the people of Noah were destroyed by water, which was the source of life. The people of ‘Ad were destroyed by storms and winds, which under specific conditions are also sources of life. The people of Thamud were destroyed by a lightning-bearing cloud, and the people of Lot by a rain of stones caused by lightning or, as some say, volcanic activity. The People of al-Rass, according to the end of the aforementioned narration, were destroyed by a fire that rose from the earth and a flame that fell from a deadly cloud—so that this arrogant human might come to his senses and choose the path of God, justice, and piety.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا رَأَوْكَ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى بَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ رَسُولًا (٤١)
When they see you they just take you in derision: ‘Is this the one whom Allah has sent as an apostle!? (41)
إِن كَادَ لَيُضِلُّنَا عَنْ ءَالِهَتِنَا لَوْلَآ أَن صَبَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَسَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ ٱلْعَذَابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٤٢)
Indeed he was about to lead us astray from our gods, had we not stood firm by them.’ Soon they will know, when they sight the punishment, who is further astray from the [right] way. (42)
أَرَءَيْتَ مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَـٰهَهُۥ هَوَىٰهُ أَفَأَنتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلًا (٤٣)
Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god? Is it your duty to watch over him? (43)
أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ ۚ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ۖ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا (٤٤)
Do you suppose that most of them listen or exercise their reason? They are just like cattle; indeed, they are further astray from the way. (44)

Commentary

More Astray Than Cattle

It is noteworthy that in the verses of this Surah, the Holy Quran does not relay the words of the polytheists all at once; rather, it quotes a portion, provides a response, advice, and warning, and then continues with another portion in the same manner.

The verses under discussion recount another example of the polytheists' logic and the nature of their encounter with the Prophet of Islam and his truthful call.

First, it says: "And when they see you, they treat you only with mockery, [saying], 'Is this the one whom Allah has sent as a messenger?'" (وَ إِذا رَأَوْكَ إِنْ يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُواً أَ هذَا الَّذِي بَعَثَ اللَّهُ رَسُولًا) [1]. What a great claim he makes! What strange words he utters! Truly, it is laughable! But it must not be forgotten that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was the same person who lived among them for forty years before his mission, known for his trustworthiness, honesty, intelligence, and wisdom. However, when the leaders of disbelief saw their interests endangered, they cast all these matters into oblivion. With ridicule and mockery, they dismissed the Prophet's call despite all its clear evidence and eloquent proofs, and even accused him of madness!

[^1]: "Huzuwan" (هزوا) is a verbal noun (masdar) used here in the sense of the object (maf'ul). It is also possible that a possessive noun is implied (the object of mockery). Additionally, the use of "hadha" (this) is intended to belittle and disparage the Prophet.

Then, the Quran continues quoting the words of the polytheists:

"He would almost have misled us from our gods had we not been steadfast in [worshipping] them" and severed our connection with them (إِنْ كادَ لَيُضِلُّنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا لَوْ لا أَنْ صَبَرْنا عَلَيْها) [1]. But the Quran responds to them in several ways. First, with a crushing sentence directed at this group who lacked logic, it replies: "But they will know, when they see the punishment, who is farther astray from the path" (وَ سَوْفَ يَعْلَمُونَ حِينَ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ مَنْ أَضَلُّ سَبِيلًا).

This punishment may refer to the torment of the Resurrection, as some commentators like Tabarsi have stated in Majma' al-Bayan, or to worldly punishment such as the painful defeat on the day of "Badr" and the like, as Qurtubi mentioned in his famous commentary. It is also possible that it refers to both.

Interestingly, this misguided group committed two contradictory acts in their speech: on one hand, they mocked the Prophet and his call, implying his claim was so baseless it did not deserve a serious encounter; on the other hand, they believed that if they did not cling firmly to the religion of their ancestors, the Prophet's words might turn them away from it. This shows they considered his words exceptionally serious, effective, calculated, and systematic. Such incoherent speech is not unexpected from confused and stubborn individuals.

Furthermore, it is often seen that deniers of the truth, when faced with the surging waves of the logic of divine leaders, sometimes choose mockery as a tactic to belittle and erase it, while inwardly they do not hold such a view, and at other times they take it seriously and fight it with their entire being.

[^1]: The word "in" (إِنْ) in "in kada layudilluna" is "mukhaffafah" (shortened) and is for emphasis; it is implied as "innahu kada," where the pronoun refers to the situation (shan).

The second response the Quran gives to their words appears in the next verse. Addressing the Prophet as a means of comfort and consolation, and also to state the primary reason for their rejection of his call, He says:

"Have you seen the one who takes as his god his own desire?" (أَ رَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ).

"Then would you be responsible for him?" (أَ فَأَنْتَ تَكُونُ عَلَيْهِ وَكِيلًا).

That is to say, if they resorted to mockery, denial, and various forms of opposition against your call, it was not because your logic was weak, your proofs unconvincing, or your religion doubtful. Rather, it is because they do not follow the command of reason and logic; their deity is their own whim. Do you expect such people to accept you or for you to be able to influence them? Regarding the meaning of the phrase "Have you seen the one who takes as his god his own desire," great commentators have offered various explanations.

A group, as mentioned above, says it means they have one idol, and that is their carnal desire, from which all their actions spring.

Meanwhile, another group believes it means they do not observe any logic even in choosing their idols; rather, whenever their eyes fell upon a beautiful piece of stone or tree, or anything else that stirred their fancy, they would choose it as a "deity," kneel before it, offer sacrifices to it, and seek the solution to their problems from it!

Coincidentally, a narration regarding the occasion of revelation (shan al-nuzul) of the above verse supports this meaning: in one of the years, life became difficult for the Quraysh in Mecca and they scattered around. Some of them, as soon as they encountered a beautiful tree or an interesting stone, would worship it. If it was a stone, they named it the "Stone of Felicity," sacrificed for it, sprinkled the blood of the sacrifice upon it, and even sought healing for their animals' diseases from it! One day, an Arab came and wanted to rub his camels against that stone to seek blessings. The camels bolted and scattered in the desert. He composed a poem whose meaning was:

"I came to the Stone of Felicity to gather our scattered affairs, but it turned our gathering into scattering!" Then he said, "What is this stone of felicity but a piece of stone like the earth; it neither leads man astray nor to guidance!"

Another Arab man saw one of these stones while a fox was urinating on it. He composed this poem: "Is it a lord, that upon whose head the fox urinates? * Surely disgraced is he upon whom foxes urinate!" [1] These two interpretations do not contradict each other. The essence of idol worship, which is born of superstition, is a type of whim-worship, and choosing various idols without any logic is another branch of whim-worship.

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 20.

Regarding the "Idol of Whims and Desires," a detailed discussion will follow in the "Key Points" section, Allah willing.

Finally, the third response the Quran gives regarding this misguided group is: "Or do you think that most of them hear or understand?" (أَمْ تَحْسَبُ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ يَسْمَعُونَ أَوْ يَعْقِلُونَ).

"They are only like cattle; nay, they are even farther astray!" (إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا كَالْأَنْعامِ بَلْ هُمْ أَضَلُّ).

Meaning, their mockery and offensive, illogical words should never upset you, because a person must either possess reason and utilize it (becoming an instance of "ya'qilun") or, if they lack knowledge, listen to the words of the wise (becoming an instance of "yasma'un"). But this group is neither this nor that, and for this reason, they are no different from cattle. It is clear that one cannot expect anything from cattle except braying, kicking, and performing illogical acts.

In fact, they are more wretched and miserable than cattle, for cattle lack the capacity for reason and thought, while these people possess it and yet have fallen into such a state!

It is noteworthy that here again, the Quran uses the expression "most of them" (aktharahum) and does not generalize this judgment to all of them, because there may be individuals among them who are truly deceived, and when faced with the truth, the veils will gradually be removed from their eyes and they will accept the truth. This itself is evidence of the observance of fairness in Quranic discussions.

Key Points

1- Whim-Worship and Its Painful Consequences

Undoubtedly, within the human being exist various instincts and desires, all of which are necessary for the continuation of life: anger, self-love, love for wealth and material life, and the like. Without doubt, the system of creation produced all of these for the same evolutionary goal.

However, the important thing is that sometimes these exceed their limits, overstep their bounds, and instead of being obedient tools in the hand of reason, they begin to rebel and act lawlessly. They imprison reason, rule over the entire existence of the human being, and take the reins of his choice.

This is what is referred to as "whim-worship" (hawa parasti), which is more dangerous than all types of idol worship; in fact, idol worship itself stems from it.

It is not without reason that the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله considered the idol of "hawa" (desire) to be the greatest and worst of idols when he said:

"Under the shade of the sky, there is no deity worshipped besides Allah that is greater in the sight of Allah than a followed whim." [1]

And in another hadith from some of the leaders of Islam, we read:

"The most hated deity worshipped on the face of the earth is whim."

If we reflect deeply, we will well understand the depth of this statement, for whim-worship is the source of negligence and ignorance, as the Quran says:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur under the verse discussed, quoted from Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 15, p. 257.

"And do not obey one whose heart We have made heedless of Our remembrance and who follows his desire" (Al-Kahf, Verse 28).

On the other hand, whim-worship is the source of disbelief and lack of faith, as the Quran says: "So let not one who does not believe in it and follows his desire avert you from it" (Taha, Verse 16).

Thirdly, whim-worship is the worst misguidance; the Quran says: "And who is more astray than he who follows his desire without guidance from Allah?" (Al-Qasas, Verse 50).

Fourthly, whim-worship is the opposite of truth-seeking and leads man out of the way of Allah, as we read in the Quran: "So judge between the people in truth and do not follow desire, for it will mislead you from the way of Allah" (Sad, Verse 26).

Fifthly, whim-worship prevents justice and equity, as we read in the Quran: "So follow not [personal] inclination, lest you not be just" (An-Nisa, 135).

Finally, if the system of the heavens and the earth revolved around the whims and desires of people, corruption would encompass the entire expanse of existence: "And if the Truth had followed their desires, the heavens and the earth and whoever is in them would have been corrupted" (Al-Mu'minun, Verse 71).

In Islamic narrations, shocking expressions are also found in this regard:

In a narration from Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"The wretched is he who is deceived by his whim and his delusion." [1]

In another hadith from the same Imam, we read: "Whim-worship is the enemy of reason" (Al-hawa 'aduww al-'aql). [2]

We also read: "Whim-worship is the foundation of all tribulations" (Al-hawa uss al-mihan). [3]

And the same Imam says: "Neither religion nor reason can ever coexist with whim-worship" (La dina ma'a hawa) [4] and (La 'aqla ma'a hawa). [5]

In short, where there is whim-worship, there is neither religion nor reason; there is nothing but misery, suffering, and calamity; there will be nothing but helplessness, wretchedness, and corruption.

The events of our lives and the bitter experiences we have seen during our lifetimes regarding ourselves and others are living witnesses to all the points mentioned in the above verses and narrations regarding whim-worship.

We see individuals who suffer the consequences of one hour of whim-worship for the rest of their lives!

We know of youths who, by following their whims, have become so trapped in dangerous addictions and sexual and moral deviations that they have turned into base, weak, and worthless beings, losing all their strengths and assets.

In contemporary and past history, we encounter the names of those who, for the sake of their whim-worship, have dragged thousands and sometimes millions of innocent people into blood and dust, and their shameful names will be remembered with ugliness forever.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 86. [^2]: Ghurar al-Hikam, Saying 265. [^3]: Ghurar al-Hikam, Saying 1048. [^4]: Ghurar al-Hikam, Saying 10531. [^5]: Ghurar al-Hikam, Saying 10541.

This principle admits no exceptions; even long-standing scholars and worshippers like "Bal'am bin Ba'ura," by following carnal desires, fell so far from the peak of human greatness that the Quran likens them to a vile dog that constantly pants (Verse 176 of Surah Al-A'raf).

Therefore, it is no surprise that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام say:

"The most dangerous precipice I fear for you are two: following whims and long-reaching hopes. As for following whims, it averts from the truth, and as for long-reaching hopes, they make one forget the Hereafter." [1]

The expressions found in the opposite case—that is, abandoning whim-worship—in verses and narrations also clarify the depth of this issue from the Islamic perspective, to the point that the key to Paradise is considered to be the fear of God and struggling against the whims of the soul: "But as for he who feared the position of his Lord and prevented the soul from [unlawful] inclination, then indeed, Paradise will be [his] refuge" (Surah An-Nazi'at, Verse 40).

Ali عليه السلام says: "The bravest of people is he who overcomes his whim." [2]

In the lives of the friends of the Truth, the saints of the Lord, and the scholars and great figures, many stories are narrated of those who, by abandoning whim-worship, attained high stations that were impossible through ordinary means.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 728 (under the root hawa) and Nahj al-Balagha, Sermons 28 and 42. [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 689 (root shaja').

2- Why More Astray Than Cattle?!

In the above verses, to reflect the importance of the matter, it first says: those whose deity is their whim are like cattle; then it progresses and adds: rather, they are even farther astray! A similar expression appears in Verse 179 of Surah Al-A'raf regarding the people of Hell who, due to not using their ears, eyes, and reason, meet such a fate: "Those are like cattle; nay, they are more astray!"

Although their being more astray is generally clear, commentators have had interesting discussions on this issue, which can be analyzed and expanded as follows:

1- If cattle do not understand anything and lack hearing ears and seeing eyes, the reason is their lack of innate capacity. But more miserable than them is the human being in whose existence the essence of all felicities is hidden, and to whom God has given so much potential that he can become the representative and vicegerent of Allah on earth, yet his state reaches a point where he falls to the level of a beast. He wastes all his merits and falls from the height of being the one before whom angels prostrated to the wretched abyss of devils. This is truly painful and a manifest misguidance.

2- Cattle have almost no reckoning or book and are not subject to divine punishments, whereas misguided humans must carry the burden of all their deeds and face the penalty for their actions without reduction.

3- Cattle provide many services to humans and perform various tasks, but rebellious and lawless humans not only provide no service but also create thousands of calamities and misfortunes.

4- Cattle pose no danger to anyone, and if they do, their danger is limited. But woe to the faithless, arrogant, whim-worshipping human who sometimes ignites the fire of a war in which millions of people are turned to ash.

5- If cattle have no law or program, they follow the path that creation has determined for them in the form of "instincts" and move along that line. But the oppressive, rebellious human recognizes neither the laws of creation nor the laws of legislation and considers his own whims and desires as the ruler over everything.

6- Cattle never have justifications for their actions; if they do something wrong, it is wrong, and if they go their way, which they do, it is what it is. But a selfish, bloodthirsty, whim-worshipping human often justifies all his crimes as if he were performing his divine and human duties! In this way, no creature is more dangerous and harmful than a faithless, rebellious, whim-worshipping human, and for this reason, in Verse 22 of Surah Al-Anfal, the title "the worst of beasts" (sharr al-dawab) is given to him—and what an appropriate title it is!

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ ٱلظِّلَّ وَلَوْ شَآءَ لَجَعَلَهُۥ سَاكِنًا ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا ٱلشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا (٤٥)
Have you not regarded how your Lord spreads the twilight? (Had He wished He would have made it stand still.) Then We made the sun a beacon for it. (45)
ثُمَّ قَبَضْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْنَا قَبْضًا يَسِيرًا (٤٦)
Then We retract it toward Ourselves, with a gentle retracting. (46)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلَّيْلَ لِبَاسًا وَٱلنَّوْمَ سُبَاتًا وَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّهَارَ نُشُورًا (٤٧)
It is He who made for you the night as a covering and sleep for rest and He made the day a recall to life. (47)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً طَهُورًا (٤٨)
And it is He who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy, and We send down from the sky purifying water, (48)
