لِّنُحْـِۧىَ بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا وَنُسْقِيَهُۥ مِمَّا خَلَقْنَآ أَنْعَـٰمًا وَأَنَاسِىَّ كَثِيرًا (٤٩)
with which We revive a dead country and provide water to many of the cattle and humans We have created. (49)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَـٰهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا۟ فَأَبَىٰٓ أَكْثَرُ ٱلنَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُورًا (٥٠)
Certainly We distribute it among them so that they may take admonition. But most people are only intent on ingratitude. (50)

Commentary

The Movement of Shadows!

In these verses, the discourse pertains to significant portions of Divine blessings as a means of explaining the secrets of Monotheism and the knowledge of God—matters in which reflection makes us more acquainted with and closer to our Creator. Given that the preceding verses contained extensive dialogues with the polytheists, the connection and relationship of these verses to the past becomes clear.

In these verses, the discussion covers the blessing of "shadows," followed by the effects and bounties of "night," "sleep and rest," the "light" of day, the "blowing of winds," the "descent of rain," the "revival of dead lands," and the "quenching of thirst" for livestock and humans.

First, It says: "Have you not seen how your Lord spread the shadow?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ كَيْفَ مَدَّ الظِّلَّ‌).

"And if He willed, He could have made it still" (a permanent and eternal shadow) (وَ لَوْ شاءَ لَجَعَلَهُ ساكِناً).

Undoubtedly, this part of the verse is an allusion to the importance of the blessing of extended and moving shadows—shadows that do not remain uniform but are in motion and shift location. However, there is a discussion among commentators as to which specific shadow is intended here:

A group says this elongated and spread shadow is the very shadow that prevails over the earth after the break of dawn and before sunrise; this is the most pleasant of shadows and hours. This pale light and spreading shadow begin from the moment of dawn, and at sunrise, bright light replaces it.

Others have said it refers to the entire shadow of the night, which begins from the moment of sunset and ends at the moment of sunrise. For we know that night is, in reality, the shadow of the hemisphere of the Earth that is positioned away from the sun—a conical shadow that extends into space on the opposite side. This conical shadow is constantly in motion, ending in one region with the sunrise and forming in another.

Some have also said it refers to the shadow that appears for objects in the afternoon and gradually becomes longer and more extended.

Of course, were it not for the subsequent sentences, we would understand a broad meaning from this phrase encompassing all extended shadows. However, other indications that follow show that the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all.

For following it, He says: "Then We made the sun a guide for it" (ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَا الشَّمْسَ عَلَيْهِ دَلِيلًا).

This points to the fact that if there were no sun, the concept of shadow would not be clear. Fundamentally, a shadow is created by the rays of the sun, for "shadow" is usually applied to the faint darkness that appears for objects when light shines upon a body through which it cannot pass, causing the shadow to appear on the opposite side. Therefore, not only must a shadow be identified by light according to the law that "things are known by their opposites," but its very existence is also by the grace of light.

Then He says: "Then We withdraw it toward Us, a gradual withdrawal" (ثُمَّ قَبَضْناهُ إِلَيْنا قَبْضاً يَسِيراً).

We know that when the sun rises, shadows are gradually retracted until noon, when in some regions the shadow vanishes entirely because the sun is positioned directly above every object, and in other regions, it reaches its minimum. In this way, shadows neither appear all at once nor are they retracted all at once. This itself is one of the wisdoms of the Lord; for if the transition from light to darkness and vice versa occurred suddenly, it would be harmful to all living beings. However, this gradual system in this transitional state is such that it provides the maximum benefit to creatures without causing any harm.

The expression "yasīran" (gradual/easy) refers to the gradual gathering of the shadow, or it points to the fact that the specific system of light and darkness is a simple and easy matter before the power of the Lord. The word "ilaynā" (toward Us) also emphasizes this Divine power.

In any case, there is no doubt that just as man needs the radiance of "light" in his life, he also needs "shadow" to moderate and prevent the times of intense light. Uniform radiance of light would disrupt life, just as a uniform and static shadow would be lethal.

In the first case, all living beings would burn, and in the second, all would freeze. It is this alternating system of "light" and "shadow" that has made life possible and pleasant for man.

Therefore, other verses of the Quran count the existence of night and day following one another as among the great Divine blessings. In one place, He says: "Say: Have you considered, if Allah made the night perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you light? Will you not then hear?" (Al-Qasas: 71).

And immediately He adds: "Say: Have you considered, if Allah made the day perpetual for you until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you a night in which you may rest? Will you not then see?" (Al-Qasas: 72).

Following this, He concludes that it is out of His mercy that He made for you the night and the day, that you may rest therein and that you may seek of His bounty for your livelihood, and perhaps you will be grateful (Al-Qasas: 73).

For this same reason, the Quran counts "extended shadow" (ẓill mamdūd) as one of the blessings of Paradise—which is neither a dazzling and exhausting light nor a terrifying darkness.

After mentioning the blessing of shadows, He proceeds to describe two other blessings that are perfectly suited to it, clarifying another corner of the secrets of the system of existence that demonstrates the existence of God. He says: "And He it is who has made the night a garment for you" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ اللَّيْلَ لِباساً).

What a beautiful and interesting expression: "He made the night a garment"... This dark veil covers not only humans but all creatures on earth, protecting them like a garment and enveloping them like a covering that a person uses during sleep to create darkness and rest.

Then He points to the blessing of sleep: "And [He made] sleep a rest" (وَ النَّوْمَ سُباتاً).

"Subāt" linguistically comes from the root "sabt," meaning to cut off; subsequently, it came to mean ceasing work for the purpose of rest. The reason "Saturday" is called "Yawm al-Sabt" in the Arabic language is that its naming was taken from the Jewish tradition, as Saturday was their day of cessation from work.

In reality, this expression refers to the cessation of all physical activities during sleep. For we know that during sleep, a significant portion of the body's functions cease entirely, and others, such as the heart and respiratory system, greatly reduce their normal schedule and continue in a calmer manner so that fatigue is removed and strength is renewed.

Timely and sufficient sleep renews all the body's forces, creates vitality and strength, and is the best means for calming the nerves. Conversely, the deprivation of sleep, especially for a long period, is very harmful and even lethal. For this reason, one of the most significant tortures used by torturers is the interruption of the sleep schedule, which quickly breaks a person's resistance.

At the end of the verse, He points to the blessing of "day," saying: "And He made the day a rising" (وَ جَعَلَ النَّهارَ نُشُوراً).

The word "nushūr" originally comes from "nashr," meaning to spread out, as opposed to folding. This expression may refer to the spreading of the soul throughout the body upon awakening, which is not unlike being revived after death; or it may refer to the spreading of humans across the social scene and moving for various life tasks upon the earth.

In a hadith from the noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that every morning he would say: "Praise be to Allah who gave us life after He caused us to die, and to Him is the rising (nushūr)." [^1] Truly, the light of day is movement-generating for the human soul and body, just as darkness is sleep-inducing and calming.

In the world of nature as well, when the first rays of the sun shine, a great bustle encompasses all living creatures; a resurrection arises among them, and each moves toward its own program. Even plants, in the presence of light, rapidly breathe, feed, and grow, whereas with sunset, it is as if a "lights out" bugle is sounded throughout the world of nature—birds return to their nests and living creatures settle for rest and sleep; even plants fall into a kind of slumber.

After explaining these great gifts, which are among the most fundamental pillars of human life, He addresses another very important gift, saying: "And He it is who sends the winds as heralds of glad tidings before His mercy, and We send down from the sky pure water" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَ أَنْزَلْنا مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً طَهُوراً).

The role of the winds as the vanguard of the descent of Divine mercy is hidden from no one, for if they were not there, not a drop of rain would ever fall on dry land. It is true that the sun's radiance evaporates the water of the seas and sends it upward, and the concentration of these vapors in the cold upper layers of the air forms rain-bearing clouds; but if the winds do not drive these heavy clouds from above the oceans toward the dry lands, the clouds would simply turn back into rain and fall into the same sea.

In short, the existence of these heralds of mercy, which are constantly in motion across the earth, causes the irrigation of the dry lands, the descent of life-giving rain, the formation of rivers, springs, and water-filled wells, and the cultivation of various types of plants.

Always, a portion of these winds that move ahead of the cloud masses and are mixed with a mild moisture create a delightful breeze from which the scent of rain can be smelled. These are like a herald of glad tidings announcing the arrival of a beloved traveler.

The use of "riyāḥ" (winds) in the plural form perhaps refers to their various types—some northern, some southern, some blowing from east to west, and some from west to east—which naturally causes the spreading of clouds across all regions of the earth. [^1] It is noteworthy that here "mā’" (water) is described as "ṭahūr," which is an intensive form (ṣīghah al-mubālaghah) of purity and cleanliness. Because of this meaning, its concept is being pure and purifying; that is, water is both pure in itself and purifies contaminated things. Many things other than water are pure, but they can never purify something contaminated.

In any case, water, in addition to its life-giving property, has the exceptionally important property of being a purifier. If there were no water, in a single day our entire bodies, souls, and lives would become filthy and contaminated. Although water is not usually a germicide, because of its extraordinary solvent property, it can dissolve, wash away, and eliminate various types of germs, and in this regard, it is a very effective aid to human health and the fight against various diseases.

Furthermore, we know that the purification of the soul from impurities through ghusl (ritual bath) and wudu (ablution) is also performed with water. Thus, this life-giving liquid is a purifier of both the soul and the body.

However, this property of being a purifier, despite all its importance, is placed in the second degree. Therefore, in the next verse, He adds: "Our goal in sending down the rain is that We may revive a dead land thereby" (لِنُحْيِيَ بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً) [^1] and also "to give this life-giving water to drink to those We have created: livestock and many humans" (وَ نُسْقِيَهُ مِمَّا خَلَقْنا أَنْعاماً وَ أَناسِيَّ كَثِيراً).

Here, several points are noteworthy:

  1. In this verse, mention is made of livestock and "many humans," even though all animals and humans use rainwater. This is to point to the desert-dwellers and nomads who have absolutely no water at their disposal and use rainwater directly. This great blessing is more perceptible to them; when a patch of cloud appears in the sky, a downpour occurs, and the hollows fill with clear rainwater, their animals are quenched and they themselves drink from it, feeling the surge of life and vitality within themselves and their livestock.

  2. The phrase "nusqiyahu" is from the root "isqā’." The difference between it and "saqyā"—as Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat and some other commentators have noted—is that isqā’ means preparing water and making it available so that whenever a person wishes, he may drink from it, whereas the root saqyā means to hand a vessel of water to someone so they may drink. In other words, isqā’ has a broader and more extensive meaning.

  3. In this verse, mention is first made of dead lands, then livestock, and then humans. This expression may be because until the lands are revived by rain, livestock will have no food, and until the livestock gain life, humans cannot feed upon them.

  4. Presenting the issue of the life-giving nature of water after the issue of purification may point to the close connection between the two (we have discussed the life-giving effects of water in detail in Volume 13 under verse 30 of Surah Al-Anbiya).

In the last verse under discussion, He refers to the "Quran," saying: "And We have certainly distributed it among them in various ways so that they may remember and realize the power of the Lord through it, but most people refused [everything] except disbelief" (وَ لَدَقْ صَرَّفْناهُ بَيْنَهُمْ لِيَذَّكَّرُوا فَأَبى‌ أَكْثَرُ النَّاسِ إِلَّا كُفُوراً).

Although many commentators, such as the late Tabarsi, Sheikh Tusi in Al-Tibyan, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, and others, have returned the pronoun in the phrase "ṣarrafnāhu" to the rain—meaning that We send raindrops in different directions on the earth and various regions and distribute it among humans so they may remember this great blessing of God—the truth is that this pronoun returns to the Quran and its verses. This is because this expression (in the past and present tense) appears in ten places in the Holy Quran, and in nine of them, it explicitly refers to the verses of the Quran and its statements. In numerous instances, the phrase "li-yadhakkarū" or its like follows it. Therefore, it seems very unlikely that in this one instance, this expression would have a different meaning.

Fundamentally, the root "taṣrīf," which means to change and transform, does not have much affinity with the descent of rainwater, whereas it is more appropriate for the verses of the Quran, which come in various garbs—sometimes as a promise, sometimes as a threat, sometimes as a command, sometimes as a prohibition, and sometimes as the stories of the predecessors.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 7, p. 4455. [^1]: It should be noted that "bushran" with a sukūn on the shīn is a contraction of "bushuran" (with a dammah on the shīn), the plural of "bashūr," meaning a herald of glad tidings. [^1]: It should be noted that "baldatan" here means a desert or plain, and although this word is feminine, its adjective "maytan" appears in the masculine form because it refers to "makān" (place), and "makān" is masculine.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ شِئْنَا لَبَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَّذِيرًا (٥١)
Had We wished, We would have sent a warner to every town. (51)
فَلَا تُطِعِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَجَـٰهِدْهُم بِهِۦ جِهَادًا كَبِيرًا (٥٢)
So do not obey the faithless, but wage a great jihad against them with it. (52)
۞ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى مَرَجَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ هَـٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَهُمَا بَرْزَخًا وَحِجْرًا مَّحْجُورًا (٥٣)
It is He who merged the two seas: this one sweet and agreeable, and that one briny and bitter, and between the two He set a barrier and a forbidding hindrance. (53)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ بَشَرًا فَجَعَلَهُۥ نَسَبًا وَصِهْرًا ۗ وَكَانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيرًا (٥٤)
It is He who created the human being from water, then invested him with ties of blood and marriage, and your Lord is all-powerful. (54)
وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُهُمْ وَلَا يَضُرُّهُمْ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱلْكَافِرُ عَلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ ظَهِيرًا (٥٥)
They worship besides Allah that which neither brings them any benefit nor causes them any harm, and the faithless one is ever an abettor against his Lord. (55)

Commentary

Two Seas of Fresh and Salt Water Side by Side

The first verse under discussion refers to the greatness of the Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) status, stating:

"And if We had willed, We could have sent a warner into every city" (but We did not do so, and instead placed the responsibility of guiding the worlds upon your shoulders) (وَ لَوْ شِئْنا لَبَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ قَرْيَةٍ نَذِيراً).

In fact, just as Allah—according to the previous verses—has the power to send life-giving raindrops to all dead lands, He also has the ability to descend revelation and prophethood upon the heart of a prophet in every city and land, and to send a warner and admonisher to every nation. However, Allah chooses what is most appropriate for His servants, because the concentration of prophethood in a single individual brings about unity and cohesion among humans and prevents any form of division and scattering.

There is also the possibility that some polytheists, alongside other excuses, raised this one as well, saying: "Would it not have been better if God had appointed a prophet in every city and settlement?" But the Quran answers them by saying: If God willed, He could have done so, but this scattering was certainly not in the best interest of the nations and peoples.

In any case, this verse is a proof of the greatness of the Prophet's status, the necessity of unified leadership, and the heavy weight of his responsibility.

For this reason, in the next verse, it mentions two important commands that constitute two fundamental programs of the prophets. First, addressing the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "So do not obey the disbelievers" (فَلا تُطِعِ الْكافِرِينَ‌).

Do not take a single step toward compromising with their deviations, for compromising with the deviant is the bane of the call toward God. Stand firm against them and strive for their reformation, but be careful never to surrender to their whims and superstitions.

And as for the second command: "And strive against them with it [the Quran] a great striving" (وَ جاهِدْهُمْ بِهِ جِهاداً كَبِيراً).

A striving as great as the greatness of your mission, and as great as the striving of all previous prophets; a striving that encompasses all dimensions of people's souls and thoughts and includes both material and spiritual aspects.

Undoubtedly, the meaning of "striving" (Jihad) in this context is intellectual, cultural, and promotional striving, not armed combat, because this Surah is Meccan, and we know that the command for armed Jihad was not revealed in Mecca.

According to the late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, this verse is clear evidence that intellectual and promotional striving against the whispers of the misguided and the enemies of truth is among the greatest forms of Jihad. It is even possible that the famous Hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"We have returned from the Lesser Jihad to the Greater Jihad" (رجعنا من الجهاد الاصغر الى الجهاد الاكبر)

refers to this very Jihad and the greatness of the work of scholars and divines in propagating the religion.

This expression also speaks to the greatness of the Quran's status, as it is the instrument for this "Great Striving" and a sharp weapon whose power of expression, reasoning, deep impact, and attraction are beyond human imagination and power.

It is an effective means, as radiant as the sun and the light of day, as tranquil as the veils of night, as motivating as the winds, as grand as the clouds, and as life-giving as the drops of rain mentioned in the previous verses.

After a brief interval, it returns to arguing for the greatness of Allah through the description of His blessings in the system of creation. In connection with the mention of the descent of life-giving raindrops in the previous verses, it refers to the non-mixing of fresh and salt waters, saying: "And He it is who has released the two seas, one palatable and sweet and the other salty and bitter, and He placed between them a barrier and a forbidden partition" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي مَرَجَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ وَ هذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ وَ جَعَلَ بَيْنَهُما بَرْزَخاً وَ حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً).

"Maraja" (مَرَجَ) comes from the root "marj" (on the weight of falj), meaning to mix or to release and let loose, and here it means the placing of fresh and salt water side by side.

"Adhb" (عَذْبٌ) means palatable, pure, and cool, and "Furat" (فُراتٌ) means tasty and sweet. "Milh" (مِلْحٌ) means salty, and "Ujaj" (أُجاجٌ) means bitter and hot (thus, milh and ujaj are the opposites of adhb and furat).

"Barzakh" (بَرْزَخاً) means a veil or a barrier between two things.

The phrase "Hijran Mahjura" (حِجْراً مَحْجُوراً), as we previously noted (under verse 22 of this same Surah), was a phrase used among Arabs when they encountered someone they feared; they would say this to seek safety, meaning: "Grant us immunity and stay away from us."

In any case, this verse depicts another of the wonderful manifestations of the Lord's power in the world of creation: how an invisible veil and an unseen barrier are placed between the salt and fresh seas, not allowing them to mix.

Today, we know that this invisible veil is the "difference in the density of salt and fresh water"—or what is called the difference in their "specific gravity"—which prevents them from mixing for a long period.

Although some commentators have struggled to find such two seas on the Earth where fresh water sits beside salt water without mixing, this problem is solved for us. We know that all great rivers of fresh water that flow into the seas form a "sea" of fresh water along the coast, pushing back the salt waters. This state continues for a long time, and due to the difference in their density, they resist mixing with one another, and each says to the other: "Hijran Mahjura!"

Interestingly, due to the tides of the seas, which occur twice every twenty-four hours because of the moon's gravity, the sea level rises and falls significantly. These fresh waters, which have formed a sea at the mouths of these rivers and surrounding points, advance onto the land. Since ancient times, humans have utilized this phenomenon by digging many canals in such coastal areas, bringing vast lands under the cultivation of trees, the irrigation of which is provided by this very fresh water that spreads over wide areas due to the tides.

Currently, in southern Iran, there are perhaps millions of date palms—some of which we have observed closely—that are irrigated solely by this means, even though they are located at a considerable distance from the sea coast. In years when rainfall is low and the volume of the great rivers flowing into the sea decreases, the salt water sometimes prevails, which deeply worries the farmers of those regions as it damages their crops.

But usually, this is not the case, and this "palatable and sweet" water, which sits beside the "salty and bitter" water without mixing, is considered a great asset for them.

It goes without saying that the existence of natural causes in such matters never diminishes their value. For what is nature? It is nothing but the act of God and the will and decree of the Lord who bestowed these properties upon these entities. Interestingly, when one passes over these regions by airplane, the sight of these two waters, which have different colors and do not mix, is clearly visible, reminding one of this Quranic point.

Furthermore, the placement of this verse among verses related to "disbelief" and "faith" may be an allusion and a metaphor for the fact that sometimes in a society, in a city, or even in a single house, believers—who are like palatable and sweet water—exist alongside disbelievers—who are like salty and bitter water—with two ways of thinking, two types of belief, and two types of pure and impure actions, without being mixed together.

In the next verse, following the discussion on the descent of rain and the salt and fresh seas side by side, it speaks of the creation of man from water, saying: "And He it is who has created from water a human being" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ مِنَ الْماءِ بَشَراً).

Truly, fashioning within water and striking such an original design upon water is proof of the ultimate power of the Lord. In fact, the previous verses spoke of the nurturing of plants by rain, and here it speaks of a higher stage: the creation of man from water.

There is discussion among commentators as to what "water" refers to here:

Some believe that "human being" (Bashar) refers to the first human, Adam (عليه السلام), because his creation was from "teen" (clay), which is a mixture of water and soil. Additionally, according to some Islamic narrations, the first thing God created was water, and He created man from that water. The indefinite form of "Basharan" (a human being) supports this meaning.

However, others believe that "Ma'" (water) refers to the fluid of the zygote (sperm/semen), through which all humans come into existence by the power of the Lord. With the mixing of the male's "sperm" swimming in the fluid with the female's "ovum," the first bud of human life—the first living human cell—is formed.

If one were to carefully study the stages of conception from the beginning to the end of the fetal period, one would observe so many signs of the Truth's greatness and the Creator's power that they alone would suffice to recognize His Holy Essence.

The evidence for this interpretation is the phrase that follows in the verse, which we will explain: "and made him [a relative by] blood and marriage" (فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَ صِهْراً).

Beyond this, undoubtedly, the largest part of the human body is composed of water, such that it can be said the primary substance of every human's existence is water. For this reason, human resistance to water deficiency is very low, whereas a human can resist the lack of food for days or weeks.

Of course, it is also possible that all these meanings are gathered within the concept of the verse: that the first human was created from water, that the origin of all individuals is from the fluid of the zygote, and that water constitutes the most important building material of the human body. How has water—which is considered one of the simplest entities in this world—become the origin of such a marvelous creation?! This is a clear proof of His power.

Following the creation of man, it speaks of the expansion of generations, saying:

"God expanded this human through two branches: the branch of lineage (blood) and marriage" (فَجَعَلَهُ نَسَباً وَ صِهْراً).

"Nasab" (lineage) refers to the bond that arises among humans through procreation, such as the relationship between father and child or brothers to one another. But "Sihr," which originally means "son-in-law," refers to the bonds established through this means between two clans, such as a person's bond with their spouse's relatives. These two are what jurists (Fuqaha) refer to as "Nasab" (lineage) and "Sabab" (cause/affinity) in the discussions of marriage.

In the Holy Quran, in Surah An-Nisa, seven categories of Mahram (prohibited for marriage) through lineage are mentioned (mother, daughter, sister, paternal aunt, maternal aunt, brother's daughter, and sister's daughter) and four categories through affinity or marriage (spouse's daughter, spouse's mother, son's spouse, and father's spouse).

Of course, other opinions have been expressed by commentators regarding this phrase, but what we have stated is the clearest and strongest of all.

Among them: some have considered "Nasab" to mean male children and "Sihr" to mean female children, because lineage ties are calculated through fathers, not mothers.

However, as we explained in detail in Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 2, under verse 61 of Surah Al-Imran, this is a major mistake originating from pre-Islamic traditions that recognized lineage only through the father, while the mother had no role. In Islamic jurisprudence and among all Islamic scholars, it is certain that the rulings of lineage-based Mahram status apply to both the father's side and the mother's side. (For more explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 2, p. 437).

It is noteworthy that we have a famous Hadith here, narrated in both Shiite and Sunni books, stating that the above verse was revealed concerning the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and Ali (عليه السلام), because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) gave his daughter Fatimah (عليه السلام) in marriage to Ali (عليه السلام). Thus, Ali (عليه السلام) was both the cousin of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the husband of his daughter, and this is the meaning of "Nasaban wa Sihran" [1]. However, as we have said many times, such narrations explain the clearest instances and do not prevent the generality of the verse's concept. The verse includes every kind of bond created through lineage and marriage, one of the clearest examples of which was the bond of Ali (عليه السلام) with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) from two directions.

At the end of the verse, as an emphasis on the preceding matters, it says: "And your Lord has always been All-Powerful" (وَ كانَ رَبُّكَ قَدِيراً).

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, it clarifies the deviation of the polytheists from the principle of monotheism by comparing the power of idols with the power of the Lord—examples of which were given in the previous verses—saying: "And they worship besides Allah that which neither benefits them nor harms them" (وَ يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ما لا يَنْفَعُهُمْ وَ لا يَضُرُّهُمْ‌).

It is certain that the mere existence of benefit or harm cannot be the criterion for worship, but the Quran, with this expression, conveys the point that they have no excuse for this worship, for idols are entities that are completely useless and lack any value or positive or negative influence.

And at the end of the verse, it adds: "And the disbeliever is an assistant (in the path of disbelief) against his Lord" (وَ كانَ الْكافِرُ عَلى‌ رَبِّهِ ظَهِيراً).

They are not alone in their deviant path; they decisively reinforce one another. The forces they ought to mobilize in the path of "Allah," they mobilize against the religion of God, His Prophet, and the true believers.

And if we see that some commentators have interpreted "disbeliever" here exclusively as "Abu Jahl," it is by way of mentioning a clear example; otherwise, "disbeliever" everywhere has a broad meaning that includes all disbelievers.

Key Points

1- Unity of Leadership

In the first verse under discussion, we read the word of the Lord: "If We had willed, We could have sent a warning prophet in every city, but We did not do so."

This is certainly because prophets are the leaders of nations, and we know that division in the matter of leadership causes the weakening of any nation or people. Especially where the Finality of Prophethood is concerned and leadership must continue until the end of the world, the importance of concentration and unity becomes more apparent.

A single leader can unite all forces and give them cohesion and unity of program. In truth, the issue of unity of leadership is a reflection of the reality of Monotheism (Tawhid) in human society, the opposite of which is a kind of polytheism, division, and hypocrisy.

And if we read in verse 24 of Surah Fatir: "And there was no nation but that there had passed within it a warner" (وَ إِنْ مِنْ أُمَّةٍ إِلَّا خَلا فِيها نَذِيرٌ), it does not contradict the above discussion at all, because it speaks of a "nation" (Ummah), not the people of every single city and land.

Moving beyond the status of prophets, this same principle governs lower levels of leadership as well. Nations that have suffered fragmentation in leadership have, in addition to weakness and humiliation, ended up with fragmentation in all their other affairs.

2- The Quran as a Means of Great Striving

"Great Striving" (Jihad-e Kabir) is an expressive term for the importance of the program of a constructive divine struggle.

Interestingly, in the above verses, this title is given to the Quran, or in other words, to the action of those who struggle against crookedness, deviations, and impurities by means of the Quran.

This expression, on one hand, clarifies the importance of logical and ideological struggles, and on the other hand, the greatness of the Quran's status.

It is mentioned in some narrations that Abu Sufyan, Abu Jahl, and some other leaders of polytheism came separately one night to secretly listen to the verses of the Quran while the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was engaged in prayer. Each of them, without the others knowing, hid in a place and listened to the Quranic verses from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) for a long time that night. When they dispersed, the white of dawn had appeared, and they happened to return by the same path. Their secret was revealed, and they began to reproach one another, emphasizing that such a thing must not be repeated, for if some of the ignorant were to see this scene, it would cause them doubt and hesitation!

However, on the second night, the same program was repeated, and again they encountered each other upon returning, becoming upset and worried, repeating and emphasizing the same words as before.

On the third night, the same program was renewed, and when they saw each other, they said: "We must not move from here until we make a covenant and a pact that this will not be repeated." They made a pact and separated.

The next morning, one of the polytheists named Akhnas ibn Shariq took his staff and went to the house of Abu Sufyan, saying to him: "Tell me, what is your opinion about what you heard from Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)?" Abu Sufyan said: "By God, I heard things whose meaning I understood well, but I also heard matters whose meaning and intent I did not grasp!"

Akhnas left Abu Sufyan and went to the house of Abu Jahl, saying to him: "What do you say about what you heard from Muhammad?" Abu Jahl said: "What did I hear? The matter is that we and the children of Abu Abd Manaf are in competition for honors. They fed the hungry, and so did we; they provided mounts for the riderless, and so did we; they gave and spent, and so did we; and we were coming forward neck and neck. But then they claimed that a prophet has risen from among them to whom heavenly revelation comes. How can we compete with them in this matter? Now that it is so, by God, we will not believe in him and we will never testify to his truth!" Akhnas stood up and left him [1].

Yes, the attraction of the Quran drew them toward itself for consecutive nights, and until the white of dawn, they were immersed in this divine attraction. However, selfishness, prejudice, and the preservation of material interests dominated them to such an extent that it prevented them from accepting the truth.

Undoubtedly, this divine light has the power to attract any prepared heart wherever it may be, and for this reason, in the verses under discussion, the Quran is introduced as the means of "Great Striving."

[1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, p. 337; Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 172.

Ayahs

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا (٥٦)
We did not send you except as a bearer of good news and as a warner. (56)
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ أَن يَتَّخِذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا (٥٧)
Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord.’ (57)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْحَىِّ ٱلَّذِى لَا يَمُوتُ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِۦ ۚ وَكَفَىٰ بِهِۦ بِذُنُوبِ عِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرًا (٥٨)
Put your trust in the Living One who does not die, and celebrate His praise. He suffices as one all-aware of the sins of His servants. (58)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فِى سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ ثُمَّ ٱسْتَوَىٰ عَلَى ٱلْعَرْشِ ۚ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ فَسْـَٔلْ بِهِۦ خَبِيرًا (٥٩)
He, who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days, and then settled on the Throne, the All-beneficent; so ask someone who is well aware about Him. (59)

Commentary

My Reward is Your Guidance!

Since the preceding verses spoke of the idolaters' insistence on worshipping idols that possess absolutely no power to benefit or harm, the first of the verses under discussion points to the duty of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the face of these stubborn bigots, stating: "We have not sent you except as a giver of glad tidings and a warner" (وَ ما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّراً وَ نَذِيراً) [1]. If they do not accept your call, there is no blame upon you; you have fulfilled your duty, which is to bring glad tidings and warnings, and you have invited ready hearts toward Allah.

This statement both defines the Prophet's duty and serves as a consolation for him, while also acting as a form of threat and indifference toward this misguided group.

Then, He commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to tell them: "Say: I do not ask of you any reward for this [Quran and the conveyance of this divine religion]" (قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ). He then adds: "Except that whoever wills may take a path to his Lord" (إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ سَبِيلًا).

This means: "My only reward and wage is your guidance, and that too out of your own will and choice, not through compulsion or force." This is a beautiful expression that clarifies the ultimate grace and love of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) toward his followers, for He considers their prosperity and happiness to be His own wage and reward [2]. It is obvious that the guidance of the Ummah brings an extraordinary spiritual reward for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), for "The one who points toward good is like the one who performs it."

[^1]: According to some, "Nadhir" (warner) is an intensive form (mubalaghah), while "Mubashir" (giver of glad tidings) is simply an active participle. This difference in expression might be because the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was facing a group of disbelievers who were stubbornly persistent in their deviation; naturally, He had to warn them extensively (Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse discussed).

[^2]: Therefore, the exception in the above verse is a "connected exception" (istithna' muttasil), even though at first glance it may appear "disconnected" (munqati').

Other possibilities have also been suggested in the commentary of this verse, including:

A group of commentators believe the meaning of the verse is: "I ask no reward from you, unless you yourselves wish to spend wealth in the way of Allah for the needy, which depends on your own desire" [1]. However, the first commentary is much closer to the meaning of the verse.

From what was mentioned above, it becomes clear that the pronoun in "alayhi" (upon it) refers to the Quran and the propagation of the religion of Islam, as the discussion is about not demanding a reward for this call.

In addition to cutting off the excuses of the polytheists, this sentence clarifies that accepting this divine call is very easy, simple, and possible for everyone without any hardship or cost.

This itself is a testimony to the truthfulness of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call and the purity of His thought and program, for false claimants certainly engage in such work for a reward, whether direct or indirect.

The next verse clarifies the Prophet's primary support and says: "And put your trust in the Living One who does not die" (وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْحَيِّ الَّذِي لا يَمُوتُ). By having a support, refuge, and guardian who is always alive and will always remain alive, you have no need for their reward, nor any fear of their harm, loss, or conspiracies.

And now that it is so, "Celebrate His praise and glorify Him" (وَ سَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِهِ); declare Him free from all defects and

[^1]: In this case, the exception would be a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati').

praise Him for all His perfections.

In reality, this phrase can be considered a justification for the previous sentence; for when He is pure from every fault and defect and adorned with every perfection and beauty, such a Being is worthy of trust.

Then He adds: Do not be concerned about the sabotage and conspiracies of the enemies; "Sufficient is He as Aware of the sins of His servants" (وَ كَفى‌ بِهِ بِذُنُوبِ عِبادِهِ خَبِيراً), and He will settle their account in due time.

The next verse explains the power of the Lord across the expanse of existence and is another description of this reliable support. It says: "He is the One who created the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in six days (six periods)" (الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما فِي سِتَّةِ أَيَّامٍ). "Then He established Himself upon the Throne" of power and proceeded to regulate the universe (ثُمَّ اسْتَوى‌ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ).

One who possesses such vast power can protect those who trust in Him against every danger and incident, for both the creation of the world was by Him and its administration, leadership, and regulation are in the hands of His Pure Essence.

Furthermore, the gradual creation of the world is an allusion to the fact that Allah does not hurry in any matter. If He does not punish your enemies immediately, it is because He gives them space and respite to reform themselves. Moreover, one who hurries is one who fears losing an opportunity, and this is inconceivable regarding Allah, the Almighty.

Regarding the creation of the universe in six days and the fact that "day" in such

cases means a "period"—a period that might last millions or billions of years—and the evidence for this meaning from Arabic literature and the literature of other languages, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 6 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 54 of Surah Al-A'raf (page 200 onwards), and we have also identified these six periods.

Similarly, the meaning of "Arsh" (Throne) and the phrase "established Himself upon the Throne" was mentioned there.

At the end of the verse, He adds: "He is the Most Merciful" (الرَّحْمنُ). The One whose general mercy encompasses all beings, and the obedient and the rebellious, the believer and the disbeliever, all benefit from His unstinting table of blessings.

Now that you have a God who is Gracious, All-Powerful, and Capable, "If you want something, ask Him, for He is Aware of the needs of all His servants" (فَسْئَلْ بِهِ خَبِيراً).

In reality, this sentence is the result of the sum of the previous discussions, saying: announce to them that I do not want a reward from you, and put your trust in the God who possesses all these attributes; He is All-Powerful, Most Merciful, and All-Aware. Since it is so, whatever you want, ask of Him.

Commentators have other statements regarding the interpretation of this sentence, and they have mostly interpreted "su'al" (asking) here as "inquiring" (not requesting), saying the concept of the sentence is: if you want to inquire about the creation of existence and the power of the Lord, ask Him Himself, for He is aware of everything.

Some, in addition to interpreting "su'al" as "inquiring," have said that "Khabir" (the Aware) refers to Gabriel or the Prophet; meaning, ask them about the attributes of Allah.

Of course, the latter interpretation seems very unlikely, and the one before it is not very consistent with the preceding verses. What we said regarding the meaning of the verse—that "su'al" means requesting and asking from Allah—seems closer [1].

Key Points

1. The Reward of the Message

In many verses of the Quran, we read that the Divine Prophets explicitly stated this fact: "We do not seek any reward or wage from anyone; rather, our reward is only with the Great Allah."

Verses 109, 127, 145, 164, and 180 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, as well as verses 29 and 51 of Surah Hud, verse 72 of Surah Yunus, and 47 of Surah Saba, indicate this meaning. Undoubtedly, this non-demand for reward refutes any accusation against the true Prophets; furthermore, they can continue their work with complete freedom, and the locks and chains that might be placed on their tongues due to material relationships are removed.

However, it is interesting that regarding the Prophet of Islam, three different expressions are seen:

  1. The expression found in the above verses which says: "My reward and wage is only that you be guided!" This is an exceptionally meaningful and attractive expression.
  2. The expression found in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 23: "Say: I do not ask of you any reward for it except love for my kinsfolk" (قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى‌).
  3. The expression found in Surah Saba, verse 47: "Say: Whatever reward I have asked of you, it is for yourselves; my reward is only upon Allah" (قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهو لَكُمْ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ).

[^1]: According to this interpretation, the "ba" in "bihi" is redundant (za'idah), but according to other interpretations, "ba" means "an" (about).

By combining these three expressions, we conclude that: if in the case of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the reward of the message is considered to be "love for the kinsfolk," this love is, on one hand, for the benefit of the believers themselves, not for the Prophet's benefit, and on the other hand, this love is a means for being guided to the path of Allah.

Therefore, the sum of these verses shows that love for the kinsfolk of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) is the continuation of the program of the message and the leadership of that Prophet. In other words, to continue the path of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and His guidance and leadership, one must hold onto the kinsfolk and seek help from their leadership. This is exactly what the Shia advocate in the issue of Imamate, believing that the line of leadership after the Prophet will continue forever, not in the form of Prophethood, but in the form of Imamate.

It is also necessary to note that love is an effective factor for following, just as we read in Surah Al-Imran, verse 31: "Say: If you love Allah, then follow me" (قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللَّهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي), for I am the conveyer of His command.

Fundamentally, the bond of love with someone draws a person toward the beloved and their desires, and the stronger the bond of love, the stronger this attraction. Especially love whose motive is the "perfection" of the beloved; the perception of this perfection causes a person to try to bring themselves closer to that source of perfection and the fulfillment of his desires [1].

2. In Whom Should One Trust?

In the above verses, when He commands the Prophet to trust—to turn His gaze away from all creatures

[^1]: For further explanation in this regard, please refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 384 (under verse 31 of Surah Al-Imran).

and look only toward the Pure Essence of Allah—He enumerates attributes for this Pure Essence that are, in fact, the essential conditions for one who can become a real and reliable refuge for humanity.

First, He must be alive, for a dead and useless being like idols can never be a support for anyone.

Second, His life must be eternal, such that the possibility of His death does not create instability in the minds of those who trust in Him.

Third, His knowledge and awareness must encompass everything; He must be aware of both the needs of those who trust Him and the conspiracies and plans of the enemies.

Fourth, power over everything must set aside any kind of weakness or inability that would cause this support to falter.

Fifth, sovereignty, regulation, and the administration of all affairs must be in His capable hand. We know that these attributes are gathered only in the Pure Essence of Allah, and for this reason, the only reassuring and unwavering support in the face of all events is His Essence.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ قَالُوا۟ وَمَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ أَنَسْجُدُ لِمَا تَأْمُرُنَا وَزَادَهُمْ نُفُورًا ۩ (٦٠)
When they are told: ‘Prostrate yourselves before the All-beneficent,’ they say, ‘What is ‘‘the All-beneficent’’? Shall we prostrate ourselves before whatever you bid us?’ And it increases their aversion. (60)
تَبَارَكَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ بُرُوجًا وَجَعَلَ فِيهَا سِرَٰجًا وَقَمَرًا مُّنِيرًا (٦١)
Blessed is He who appointed houses in the sky and set in it a lamp and a shining moon. (61)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ خِلْفَةً لِّمَنْ أَرَادَ أَن يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرَادَ شُكُورًا (٦٢)
It is He who made the night and the day alternate for someone who desires to take admonition, or desires to give thanks. (62)

Commentary

The Celestial Constellations

Since the preceding verses spoke of the grandeur and power of Allah, as well as the vastness of His mercy, the first verse under discussion adds: "And when it is said to them, 'Prostrate to the All-Beneficent (al-Rahman),' whose mercy encompasses your entire existence, they say out of pride and arrogance, or perhaps out of mockery: 'And what is the All-Beneficent?'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمُ اسْجُدُوا لِلرَّحْمنِ قالُوا وَ مَا الرَّحْمنُ‌).

We do not know "al-Rahman" at all, and this word has no clear meaning for us.

"Shall we prostrate to whatever you command us?" (أَ نَسْجُدُ لِما تَأْمُرُنا).

We shall not submit to anyone, nor shall we follow the command of this person or that!

"This statement they utter, and it only increases them in aversion and distance from God" (وَ زادَهُمْ نُفُوراً).

Undoubtedly, the most appropriate of Allah's names to invite one to humility and prostration before Him is that highly attractive name, al-Rahman, with its concept of all-encompassing mercy. However, due to their spiritual blindness and obstinacy, they not only failed to show any flexibility toward this invitation but instead resorted to ridicule and mockery, asking contemptuously, "What is al-Rahman?" Just as Pharaoh said in response to the invitation of Moses عليه السلام: "And what is the Lord of the worlds?" (Surah Ash-Shu'ara, Verse 23). They were not even willing to say "Who is He?" Although a group of commentators believe that the name "al-Rahman" for God was unfamiliar to the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance, and when they heard this description from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they asked this question out of genuine surprise. Some of them even said: "We know no one by the name of Rahman; the only one we know by this name is the one in the land of 'Yamama'!" (referring to Musaylimah the Liar, who falsely claimed prophethood and whom the people knew by the name Rahman).

However, this view seems very unlikely, because the root of this name and its form are both Arabic, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله always recited this name to them at the beginning of the Surahs of the Quran in the verse: بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ. Therefore, their aim was nothing but excuse-making and mockery.

The subsequent sentence also testifies to this fact, as they say: "Shall we obey your command and prostrate at your order?" (أَ نَسْجُدُ لِما تَأْمُرُنا).

But since the propagation of divine leaders only affects ready hearts, the obstinate blind-hearted not only fail to benefit but are increased in their aversion. For the verses of the Quran are like life-giving drops of rain that grow greenery and flowers in a garden, but weeds in a salt marsh! Thus, it is no surprise that He says: "And it increased them in aversion" (وَ زادَهُمْ نُفُوراً). [1]

The next verse is, in fact, an answer to their question when they asked, "What is al-Rahman?"—even though they said it mockingly, the Quran gives them a serious answer—stating: "Blessed and Magnificent is He who has placed constellations in the heaven" (تَبارَكَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ فِي السَّماءِ بُرُوجاً). "Buruj" is the plural of "Burj," which originally means "manifestation" or "appearance." For this reason, that part of the wall around a city or a military gathering place that is higher and more visible is called a "Burj" (tower). Similarly, when a woman displays her adornments, they say "tabarrajat al-mar'ah."

[1]: Therefore, the subject of "increased" (zada) is the command to prostrate itself, which had a reverse effect on those diseased hearts. Although some commentators have narrated that the Prophet (ص) prostrated after this statement and the believers prostrated as well, and this caused them to distance themselves further; thus, the subject of "increased" would be the prostration. However, the first meaning seems more correct.

This word is also applied to high palaces.

In any case, the "celestial constellations" refer to specific astronomical configurations (zodiac signs) that the sun and moon face during each season and time of the year. For example, it is said the sun is in the constellation of "Aries" (Hamal), meaning it is aligned with the "constellation of Aries," or the "moon" is in "Scorpio" (Aqrab), meaning the lunar sphere is positioned opposite the "constellation of Scorpio" (constellations are groups of stars that appear to us in a specific shape).

In this way, the verse refers to the celestial stations of the sun and moon, and subsequently adds: "and placed therein a lamp and a luminous moon" (وَ جَعَلَ فِيها سِراجاً وَ قَمَراً مُنِيراً). [1] In reality, this verse describes the precise order of the sun and moon's movement in the sky (as it appears to us, though these changes are actually due to the earth's rotation around the sun) and the extraordinarily precise system that has governed them for millions of years without the slightest flaw, such that knowledgeable astronomers can predict the position of the sun and moon's movement on a specific day and hour hundreds of years in advance. This system governing these massive celestial spheres is a vocal witness to the existence of a Lord who is the Designer and Administrator of the great universe of existence.

With all these clear signs, with these exquisite and precise stations of the sun and moon, do you still not recognize Him and say: "What is al-Rahman"? But as for why He named the sun a "lamp" (siraj) and accompanied the moon with the attribute "luminous" (munir)?

[1]: According to the above commentary, the pronoun "therein" (fiha) refers to the constellations (buruj), not to the heaven (sama'), and it must be so, because the important subject is the movement of the sun and moon with a specific system within the constellations, not merely the existence of constellations in the sky.

The reason may be that "siraj" means a lamp whose light originates from within itself, and this is consistent with the state of the sun, which, according to definitive scientific research, has its own light. This is unlike the moon, whose light is from the reflection of the sun, and therefore He described it as "munir," meaning "giving light," even if its light is taken from another. (We have discussed this in detail in Volume 8 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verses 5 and 6 of Surah Yunus—page 226 onwards).

In the last verse under discussion, He continues to introduce the Lord of the worlds and recounts another part of the system of existence, saying: "And He is the One who made the night and the day to follow each other, for whoever desires to remember or desires gratitude" (وَ هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ اللَّيْلَ وَ النَّهارَ خِلْفَةً لِمَنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يَذَّكَّرَ أَوْ أَرادَ شُكُوراً). This exquisite system governing the night and day, where they alternately succeed one another and this order has continued for millions of years—an order without which human life would have perished due to intense light and heat or darkness and gloom—is a fascinating proof for those who wish to know God.

We know that the emergence of the "night" and "day" system is due to the earth's rotation around itself, and its gradual and regular changes, where one is constantly decreased and the other increased, are due to the tilt of its axis relative to its orbit, which causes the four seasons.

If our planet Earth rotated faster or slower than it does today, in one case the nights would be so long that everything would freeze, and the days so long that the sunlight would burn everything. In the other case, the short interval between night and day would neutralize their effects. Furthermore, the centrifugal force would increase so much that it would fling terrestrial beings off the planet! In short, the study of this system, on one hand, awakens the innate nature of God-consciousness in man (and perhaps the expression "to remember" refers to this very fact), and on the other hand, it revives the spirit of gratitude in him, as indicated by the phrase "or desires gratitude."

It is noteworthy that in some narrations quoted from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the Infallible Imams عليهم السلام in the commentary of this verse, we read: "The succession of night and day is so that if a person falls short in one of his duties in the worship of the Lord, he may compensate or make it up in the other." This meaning could be a second interpretation for the verse, and since the verses of the Quran have inner layers (batn), it does not contradict the first meaning.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Any worship or obedience that you missed during the night, make it up during the day; Allah, Blessed and Exalted, says: 'And He is the One who made the night and the day to follow each other for whoever desires to remember or desires gratitude'—meaning that man should make up the duties missed at night during the day, and the duties missed during the day at night." [1] Fakhr al-Razi has narrated a similar meaning from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

[1]: "Man La Yahduruhu al-Faqih," as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, under the verse.

Ayahs

وَعِبَادُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ قَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا (٦٣)
The servants of the All-beneficent are those who walk humbly on the earth, and when the ignorant address them, say, ‘Peace!’ (63)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَـٰمًا (٦٤)
Those who spend the night for their Lord, prostrating and standing [in worship]. (64)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا ٱصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا (٦٥)
Those who say, ‘Our Lord! Turn away from us the punishment of hell. Indeed its punishment is enduring. (65)
إِنَّهَا سَآءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا (٦٦)
Indeed it is an evil station and abode.’ (66)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَآ أَنفَقُوا۟ لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا۟ وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا۟ وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا (٦٧)
Those who are neither wasteful nor tightfisted when spending, but balanced between these [two extremes]. (67)

Commentary

The Distinctive Qualities of the Special Servants of Allah

From these verses onward, a comprehensive and fascinating discussion is presented regarding the specific characteristics of the special servants of the Lord, referred to under the title "Ibad al-Rahman" (the Servants of the Most Compassionate). In reality, this serves as a completion to the previous verses where the stubborn polytheists, upon hearing the name of the Compassionate God (al-Rahman), would ask out of mockery and pride: "What is the Compassionate?" We saw that the Quran introduced the "Compassionate" God to them within two verses; now it is time to introduce the special servants of this Compassionate God. When His servants possess such high status and character, the greatness of the Compassionate God can be better understood.

These verses outline twelve distinctive qualities of these servants, some relating to matters of belief, some to ethics, and others to social conduct. Some aspects are individual, while others are collective; together, they form a collection of the highest human values.

First, it states: "The special servants of the Compassionate God are those who walk upon the earth with tranquility and without arrogance" (وَعِبَادُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا) [^1]. Indeed, the first description given of the "Servants of the Most Compassionate" is the negation of pride, arrogance, and selfishness, which manifests in all of a person's actions and even in the manner of their walking. This is because moral traits always reveal themselves through a person's deeds, speech, and movements, to the extent that by carefully observing how a person walks, one can discern a significant portion of their character.

Yes, they are humble, and humility is the key to faith, whereas pride and arrogance are considered the keys to disbelief. In daily life, we have seen with our own eyes, and in the verses of the Quran we have repeatedly read, that the arrogant were not even willing to listen to the words of divine leaders; they mocked truths, and their vision did not extend beyond the tips of their noses. Is it possible to believe with such a state of pride? Indeed, these believers are servants of the Compassionate God, and the first sign of servitude is humility—a humility that has permeated every atom of their being and is apparent even in their gait.

And if we see that one of the most important commands Allah gives to His Prophet is: وَلَا تَمْشِ فِي الْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّكَ لَن تَخْرِقَ الْأَرْضَ وَلَن تَبْلُغَ الْجِبَالَ طُولًا ("And do not walk upon the earth exultantly. Indeed, you will never tear the earth [apart], and you will never reach the mountains in height" - Surah Al-Isra, Verse 37), it is because the spirit of faith is humility.

Truly, if a person has the slightest understanding of themselves and the universe, they would know how small they are in the face of this vast world. Even if their neck were as high as the mountains, the tallest mountains on earth compared to the greatness of the earth are less than the bumps on the skin of an orange, and that very earth is but a tiny speck in these massive galaxies.

In such a state, are pride and arrogance not evidence of absolute ignorance? In a fascinating Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that one day he was passing through an alley and saw a group of people gathered at one spot. He asked for the reason.

They replied that there was a madman whose insane and laughable actions had drawn people's attention. The Prophet called them toward himself and said: "Shall I introduce the real madman to you?" Everyone remained silent and listened with all their being.

He said: "The one who walks with arrogance and pride, constantly looking at his sides, moving his flanks with his shoulders (seeing no one but himself, and his thoughts not extending beyond himself); the one from whom people expect no good and from whose evil they are not safe—that is the real madman. But this one you saw is merely a sick person."

Their second quality is forbearance and patience, as the Quran continues in the same verse: "And when the ignorant address them [with harsh words], they reply with 'Peace'" (وَإِذَا خَاطَبَهُمُ الْجَاهِلُونَ قَالُوا سَلَامًا).

This "Peace" (Salam) is a sign of indifference coupled with nobility, not a result of weakness. It is a "Peace" that signifies a refusal to retaliate against the ignorant and the foolish. it is a "Peace" of bidding farewell to their irrational talk, not a "Peace" of greeting that signifies affection and friendship. In short, it is a "Peace" that is a sign of forbearance, patience, and magnanimity.

Indeed, another of their great spiritual phenomena is endurance and patience, without which no human can traverse the difficult and winding path of servitude and worship of God, especially in societies where corrupt, mischievous, and ignorant individuals are numerous.

In the second verse, it addresses their third characteristic, which is the sincere worship of the Lord:

It says: "They are those who spend the night before their Lord prostrating and standing" (وَالَّذِينَ يَبِيتُونَ لِرَبِّهِمْ سُجَّدًا وَقِيَامًا).

In the darkness of the night, when the eyes of the heedless are asleep and there is no room for showing off or hypocrisy, they forbid themselves sweet sleep and engage in something sweeter: the remembrance of God and standing and prostrating before His majestic presence. They spend a portion of the night in intimate conversation with the Beloved, illuminating their hearts and souls with His remembrance and name.

Although the phrase "يَبِيتُونَ" (spend the night) suggests they spend the entire night until morning in prostration and standing, it is understood that this refers to a significant portion of the night, or it may be the case at certain times.

Furthermore, mentioning "prostration" (سُجُود) before "standing" (قِيَام) is due to its supreme importance, even though in the actual performance of prayer, standing precedes prostration. [^1]

Their fourth distinctive quality is the fear of divine punishment and retribution: "They are those who constantly say: 'Our Lord, avert from us the punishment of Hell, for its punishment is indeed a persistent affliction'" (وَالَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا اصْرِفْ عَنَّا عَذَابَ جَهَنَّمَ ۖ إِنَّ عَذَابَهَا كَانَ غَرَامًا).

"For indeed, Hell is an evil settlement and an evil residence" (إِنَّهَا سَاءَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا). Despite the fact that they remember God at night and are busy with His worship, and during the day they walk the path of fulfilling their duties, their hearts are still filled with the fear of responsibilities. This is the same fear that serves as a powerful motivator to move toward performing duties better and more extensively.

[^1]: It should be noted that "سُجَّد" is the plural of "سَاجِد" (one who prostrates) and "قِيَام" is the plural of "قَائِم" (one who stands).

It is the same fear that, like a powerful internal policeman, controls a person, ensuring they perform their duties in the best possible way without needing an external supervisor, while still considering themselves deficient before God.

The word "غَرَام" (gharam) originally means a severe calamity or distress that does not leave a person. If a creditor is called a "غَرِيم" [^1], it is because he constantly stays with the person to claim his right. The burning love and passion that makes a person insistently pursue a task or something is also called "غَرَام". Applying this word to Hell is because its punishment is severe, relentless, and long-lasting.

The difference between "مُسْتَقَرّ" (settlement) and "مُقَام" (residence) might be that Hell is a permanent residence (مُقَام) for the disbelievers, while for the [sinful] believers, it is a temporary settlement (مُسْتَقَرّ). In this way, both groups entering Hell are referenced.

It is clear that Hell is an evil settlement and residence. Where is the burning fire, and where is settling? Where are the lethal flames, and where are residence and comfort?

It is also possible that "مُسْتَقَرّ" and "مُقَام" carry the same meaning, serving as an emphasis on the permanence of the punishment of Hell, directly contrasting with Paradise, about which we read later in these verses: "خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا" ("Abiding therein eternally. Excellent is the settlement and residence") (Al-Furqan, 76).

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 15, p. 151

  • In the final verse under discussion, it points to the fifth distinguished quality of the "Servants of the Most Compassionate," which is moderation

[^1]: "غَرِيم" refers to both the "creditor" and the "debtor" (Lisan al-Arab, root gha-ra-ma).

and the avoidance of any kind of excess or deficiency in affairs, especially in the matter of spending (Infaq). It says: "They are those who, when they spend, are neither extravagant nor stingy, but maintain a middle way between those [extremes]" (وَالَّذِينَ إِذَا أَنفَقُوا لَمْ يُسْرِفُوا وَلَمْ يَقْتُرُوا وَكَانَ بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ قَوَامًا).

It is noteworthy that the verse takes the act of spending for granted, such that it does not need mentioning, because spending is an inevitable duty of every human being. Therefore, it focuses on the quality of their spending, stating that they have a just spending habit, far from any extravagance or stinginess. They do not give so much that their wives and children go hungry, nor are they so tight-fisted that others cannot benefit from their blessings.

In the commentary on "إِسْرَاف" (extravagance) and "إِقْتَار" (stinginess), which are opposites, commentators have various views, all of which return to one core idea: "إِسْرَاف" is to consume beyond the limit, in what is not right, or out of place; and "إِقْتَار" is to consume less than what is right and necessary.

In one of the Islamic narrations, a fascinating analogy is made for extravagance, stinginess, and the middle path. When Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام recited this verse, he picked up a handful of pebbles from the ground and gripped them tightly in his hand, saying: "This is 'إِقْتَار' and stinginess." Then he picked up another handful and opened his hand so wide that all of them fell to the ground, saying: "This is 'إِسْرَاف'." The third time, he picked up another handful and opened his hand slightly so that some fell and some remained in his palm, and he said: "This is 'قَوَام' (the middle way)." [^1] The word "قَوَام" (pronounced qawām) linguistically means justice, uprightness, and the middle point between two things.

[^1]: Al-Kafi—as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 29.

Key Points

1- The Manner of the Believers' Gait

In the verses above, we read that one of the signs of the special servants of God is humility—a humility that governs their spirit and is even visible in their walk; a humility that compels them to submit to the truth. However, some might mistake humility for weakness, lethargy, or laziness, which is a dangerous way of thinking.

Humility in walking does not mean taking weak and lifeless steps; rather, while being humble, they should take firm steps that indicate seriousness and strength. Regarding the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله state, one of the companions says:

"I never saw anyone faster in walking than the Messenger of Allah (ص); it was as if the earth was being folded for him, and we would exert ourselves while he seemed unbothered." [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, regarding the interpretation of the verse "الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا", he said:

"It means a person walks according to his natural disposition upon which he was created, without affectation or arrogance." [^2]

[^1]: Fi Zilal, under the verse; Tafsir al-Qurtubi also narrates another report in this regard very similar to what was mentioned above. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

In another Hadith describing the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله state, it is said:

"When he walked, he would lean forward as if he were descending a slope." [^1] In any case, as we mentioned, the quality of walking is not the only thing intended; rather, it is a window into understanding the spiritual state of a person. In reality, the verse refers to the permeation of the spirit of humility and modesty into the soul and spirit of the Servants of the Most Compassionate.

2- Stinginess and Extravagance

Undoubtedly, extravagance (Israf) is one of the most condemned acts from the perspective of the Quran and Islam, and it has been heavily rebuked in verses and narrations. Extravagance is a Pharaonic program: "وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعَالٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ" ("And indeed, Pharaoh was a tyrant in the land, and indeed, he was of the extravagant" - Yunus, 83).

The extravagant are the companions of the Fire and Hell: "وَأَنَّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ هُمْ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ" ("And that the extravagant are the companions of the Fire" - Ghafir, 43).

Considering what has been proven today—that the resources on earth, relative to the human population, are not so vast that one can afford to be extravagant—every act of extravagance causes the deprivation of innocent people. Furthermore, the spirit of extravagance is usually coupled with selfishness, conceit, and alienation from God's creation.

At the same time, stinginess, parsimony, and being miserly are equally ugly, unpleasant, and condemned. Fundamentally, from the perspective of monotheistic insight, the real owner is Allah, and we are all His trustees. Any disposal of wealth without His permission and satisfaction is ugly and unpleasant, and we know He permits neither extravagance nor stinginess and narrow-mindedness.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ وَلَا يَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّفْسَ ٱلَّتِى حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا يَزْنُونَ ۚ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثَامًا (٦٨)
Those who do not invoke another deity besides Allah, and do not kill a soul [whose life] Allah has made inviolable, except with due cause, and do not commit fornication. (Whoever does that shall encounter its retribution, (68)
يُضَـٰعَفْ لَهُ ٱلْعَذَابُ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَيَخْلُدْ فِيهِۦ مُهَانًا (٦٩)
the punishment being doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection. In it he will abide in humiliation forever, (69)
إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ ٱللَّهُ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ حَسَنَـٰتٍ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا (٧٠)
except those who repent, attain faith, and act righteously. For such, Allah will replace their misdeeds with good deeds, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (70)
وَمَن تَابَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَإِنَّهُۥ يَتُوبُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَتَابًا (٧١)
And whoever repents and acts righteously indeed turns to Allah with due penitence). (71)

Commentary

Another Discussion on the Attributes of the "Servants of the Most Compassionate"

The sixth characteristic of the "Servants of the Most Compassionate" ( عباد الرحمن) mentioned in the verses under discussion is pure monotheism (Tawhid), which distances them from any form of polytheism, dualism, or polytheism. It says: "And those who do not invoke another deity with Allah" (وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ).

The light of Tawhid illuminates their entire hearts and their individual and social lives, and the gloom and darkness of polytheism have completely departed and vanished from the horizon of their thoughts and souls.

The seventh attribute is their purity from the stain of the blood of the innocent: "And they do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden, except by right" (وَ لا يَقْتُلُونَ النَّفْسَ الَّتِي حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ‌) [1]. From the above verse, it is clearly understood that all human souls are inherently respected and the shedding of their blood is forbidden, unless factors arise that overshadow this inherent sanctity and provide a justification for shedding blood.

Their eighth description is that the garment of their chastity is never stained: "and they do not commit adultery" (وَ لا يَزْنُونَ‌).

At the crossroads of disbelief and faith, they choose faith; at the crossroads of security and insecurity of lives, they choose security; and at the crossroads of purity and pollution, they choose purity. Through their efforts, they provide an environment free from any kind of polytheism, insecurity, unchastity, and impurity.

[1]: The exception in the above sentence is what is technically called "Istithna' Mufarragh" (an elliptical exception), and the implied meaning is: "They do not kill the soul which Allah has forbidden for any reason, except by right."

At the end of this verse, to emphasize even further, it adds: "and whoever does that shall meet the penalty" (وَ مَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثاماً).

"Ithm" and "Atham" originally mean actions that distance a person from reaching a reward, and subsequently, it has been applied to any kind of sin; however, here it means the recompense for sin.

Some have also said that "Ithm" means sin and "Atham" means the punishment for sin [1]. If we see that some commentators have mentioned it as the name of a valley, a mountain, or a well in Hell, it is actually by way of providing an example of its manifestation.

Regarding the philosophy of the prohibition of adultery, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 12, page 103 onwards (under verse 33 of Surah Al-Isra).

It is noteworthy that in the above verse, the issue of polytheism is mentioned first, then the killing of a soul, and after that, adultery. It is understood from some narrations that these three sins are ranked in order of importance exactly as they appear in the verse.

Ibn Mas'ud narrates from the noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: I asked: "Which sin is the greatest?" He said: "That you set up a rival to Allah while He created you." I said: "Then which?" He said: "That you kill your child for fear that he may eat with you." I said: "Then which?" He said: "That you commit adultery with your neighbor's wife." Then Allah revealed the confirmation of this in: وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَدْعُونَ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ... until the end of the verse.

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse being discussed.

Although this hadith speaks of specific types of murder and adultery, considering the absolute nature of the verse's concept, this ruling applies to all types, and the case in the narration is a clearer manifestation of it.

Since these three sins are of the utmost importance, the next verse emphasizes them again, saying: "The punishment will be doubled for him on the Day of Resurrection, and he will abide therein disgraced" (يُضاعَفْ لَهُ الْعَذابُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَ يَخْلُدْ فِيهِ مُهاناً).

Two questions arise here: First, why is the punishment for such people doubled? Why are they not punished according to the measure of their sin? Is this consistent with the principles of justice?

Second, here there is talk of "Khulud" (immortality) and eternal punishment, while we know that immortality is only for the disbelievers; and of the three sins mentioned in this verse, only the first sin is disbelief. How can killing a soul or adultery cause immortality?

Commentators have discussed the answer to the first question extensively. What seems most correct is that the meaning of the punishment being doubled is that for each of these three sins mentioned in this verse, there will be a separate punishment, which collectively constitutes a doubled punishment.

Furthermore, sometimes one sin becomes the source of other sins, such as disbelief which leads to the abandonment of obligations and the commission of prohibitions, and this itself causes the doubling of the divine punishment.

For this reason, some commentators have taken this verse as evidence for the well-known principle that "the disbelievers are obligated to the branches of religion just as they are obligated to the roots" (الكفار مكلفون بالفروع كما انهم مكلفون بالاصول).

As for the answer to the second question, it can be said that some sins are so severe that they cause one to leave this world without faith, as we have stated regarding the killing of a soul under verse 93 of Surah An-Nisa [1]. In the case of adultery, especially if it is adultery with a married person, it may also be so.
