There is also the possibility that "Khulud" in the above verse refers to those who commit all three sins together: polytheism, murder, and adultery. Evidence for this meaning is the following verse which says: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ عَمَلًا صالِحاً "Except for those who repent, believe, and do righteous work." In this way, no problem remains.

Some commentators have also taken "Khulud" here to mean a long duration, not eternity, but the first and second interpretations seem more correct.

It is noteworthy that here, in addition to the issue of ordinary punishment, another penalty is mentioned, which is humiliation and disgrace (مهانت), which has a psychological aspect. This itself can be an interpretation of the doubling of the punishment, as they suffer both physical and spiritual torment.

But since the Holy Quran never closes the way of return to criminals and encourages and invites sinners to repent, in the next verse it says:

[1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 4, p. 68.

"Except for those who repent, believe, and do righteous work; for those, Allah will replace their evil deeds with good deeds, and Allah is Ever-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ عَمَلًا صالِحاً فَأُوْلئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ وَ كانَ اللَّهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً).

As we saw in the previous verse, three of the greatest sins were mentioned. The fact that the door of repentance is left open for such individuals is proof that every repentant sinner can return to Allah, provided that their repentance is sincere, the sign of which is the compensatory righteous work mentioned in the verse; otherwise, mere verbal seeking of forgiveness with a fleeting regret in the heart is never proof of repentance.

The important issue regarding the above verse is: how does Allah replace their "evil deeds" (سيئات) with "good deeds" (حسنات)?

The Transformation of Evil Deeds into Good Deeds

There are several interpretations here, all of which can be acceptable:

  1. When a person repents and believes in Allah, a profound transformation occurs throughout his entire being. Because of this internal revolution and change, his evil deeds in the future are replaced by good deeds. If in the past he committed murder, in the future he replaces it with defending the oppressed and fighting the oppressors; and if he was an adulterer, he later becomes chaste and virtuous. He finds this divine success in the shadow of faith and repentance.

  2. Another is that Allah, by His grace, generosity, favor, and bounty, erases his evil deeds after he repents and places good deeds in their stead. As we read in a narration from Abu Dharr from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: On the Day of Resurrection, some individuals are brought forth. Allah commands: "Present his minor sins to him and hide the major ones." It is said to him: "You did such-and-such minor sin on such-and-such day," and he confesses to it, while his heart is trembling and quaking because of the major sins.

At this point, whenever Allah wishes to show him grace, He commands: "Give him a good deed in place of every evil deed." He says: "O my Lord! I had significant sins that I do not see here."

Abu Dharr says: At this moment, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله smiled such that his teeth became visible, then he recited this verse: "فَأُوْلئِكَ يُبَدِّلُ اللَّهُ سَيِّئاتِهِمْ حَسَناتٍ" [1].

  1. The third interpretation is that "evil deeds" does not refer to the physical acts the person performed, but rather the negative effects that settled upon the person's soul and spirit. When he repents and believes, those negative effects are removed from his soul and spirit and are transformed into effects of goodness. This is the meaning of replacing evil deeds with good deeds.

Of course, these three interpretations are not mutually exclusive and it is possible for all three to be combined in the concept of the verse.

The next verse actually describes the nature of correct repentance and says: "And whoever repents and does righteous work, he indeed turns to Allah with a [true] conversion" (and receives his reward from Him) (وَ مَنْ تابَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَإِنَّهُ يَتُوبُ إِلَى اللَّهِ مَتاباً) [2]. That is, repentance and the abandonment of sin should not be solely because of the ugliness of the sin, but rather, in addition to that, the motive should be sincerity of intention and returning to the Lord.

Therefore, for example, leaving wine or lying because of the harms they have, although good, only has its true value if it stems from a divine motive.

Some commentators have mentioned another interpretation for the above verse, which is: This sentence is an answer to the wonder that might occasionally arise in some minds from the previous verse, which is: how is it possible for Allah to replace evil deeds with good deeds? This verse answers that when a person returns to the Great Allah, this matter is not surprising.

A third interpretation has been mentioned for the verse: whoever repents from sin returns to Allah and His countless rewards.

Although these three interpretations are not contradictory, the first interpretation seems closer, especially since it is consistent with a narration quoted in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim under the verse being discussed.

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 33. [2]: "Matab" is a mim-infinitive meaning repentance, and since it is a cognate accusative (maf'ul mutlaq) here, it expresses emphasis.

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَشْهَدُونَ ٱلزُّورَ وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِٱللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا۟ كِرَامًا (٧٢)
Those who do not give false testimony, and when they come upon frivolity, pass by with dignity. (72)
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا۟ عَلَيْهَا صُمًّا وَعُمْيَانًا (٧٣)
Those who, when reminded of the signs of their Lord, do not turn a deaf ear and a blind eye to them. (73)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَا هَبْ لَنَا مِنْ أَزْوَٰجِنَا وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِنَا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ وَٱجْعَلْنَا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِمَامًا (٧٤)
And those who say, ‘Our Lord! Give us joy and comfort in our spouses and offspring, and make us imams of the Godwary.’ (74)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ ٱلْغُرْفَةَ بِمَا صَبَرُوا۟ وَيُلَقَّوْنَ فِيهَا تَحِيَّةً وَسَلَـٰمًا (٧٥)
Those shall be rewarded with sublime abodes for their patience and steadfastness, and they shall be met there with greetings and ‘Peace,’ (75)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَمُقَامًا (٧٦)
to abide in them [forever], an excellent station and abode. (76)

Commentary

The Reward of the "Servants of the All-Merciful" (The Special Servants of Allah)

Following the previous verses that recounted some of the characteristics of the "Servants of the All-Merciful" (Ibad al-Rahman), the verses under discussion explain the remainder of these attributes.

Their ninth prominent attribute is the respect for and preservation of the rights of others: "And those who do not bear witness to falsehood" (وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَشْهَدُونَ الزُّورَ).

Great commentators have interpreted this verse in two ways:

Some, as mentioned above, consider "witnessing zur" to mean "bearing false witness," because zur linguistically means inclination and deviation. Since lies, falsehood, and injustice are matters of deviation, they are called zur.

This expression (shahadat al-zur) is discussed under this very title in the Book of Testimony (Shahadat) in our jurisprudence, and it has been prohibited in numerous narrations, although we have not seen a direct citation of the above verse in those specific narrations.

The second interpretation is that shuhud means "presence"; that is, the special servants of Allah do not attend gatherings of falsehood.

In some narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it has been interpreted as gatherings of ghina (sinful singing)—those gatherings where vain singing is performed, accompanied by musical instruments or without them.

Undoubtedly, the purpose of such narrations is not to limit the broad concept of zur to ghina alone; rather, ghina is one of its clearest examples, and it also encompasses other gatherings of vanity, play, wine-drinking, lying, backbiting, and the like.

It does not seem unlikely that both interpretations are combined in the meaning of the verse. Thus, the Ibad al-Rahman and special servants of Allah neither give false testimony nor attend gatherings of vanity, falsehood, and sin; for attending such gatherings, in addition to endorsing the sin, is a prelude to the contamination of the heart and soul.

Then, in the continuation of the verse, it refers to another of their prominent traits, which is having a positive goal in life, saying: "And when they pass by vanity, they pass by with dignity" (وَ إِذا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِراماً).

In truth, they neither attend gatherings of falsehood nor do they become tainted by vanity and futility.

Given that laghw (vanity) includes any action that lacks a rational purpose, it shows that they always pursue a reasonable, useful, and constructive goal in life. They detest those who are futile and those who perform futile acts. If such matters happen to cross their path in life, they pass by them in such a way that their indifference itself is proof of their inner dissatisfaction with these actions. They are so noble that contaminated environments never affect them or change their character.

Undoubtedly, ignoring such scenes is only when there is no better way to combat corruption and "enjoin the good and forbid the evil"; otherwise, they would certainly stand firm and fulfill their duty to the final stage.

The tenth description of this group of special servants of Allah is having seeing eyes and hearing ears when encountering the signs of their Lord. He says: "And those who, when they are reminded of the signs of their Lord, do not fall upon them deaf and blind" (وَ الَّذِينَ إِذا ذُكِّرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ لَمْ يَخِرُّوا عَلَيْها صُمًّا وَ عُمْياناً).

Certainly, this is not referring to the actions of the disbelievers, for they pay no attention to Divine signs at all. Rather, it refers to the group of hypocrites or those superficial Muslims who fall upon the signs of Allah with closed eyes and ears, without understanding their reality, reaching their depth, knowing Allah's intent, reflecting upon them, or being inspired by them in practice.

The path of Allah cannot be traversed with closed eyes and ears; before anything else, hearing ears and seeing eyes are necessary to walk this path—an eye that looks at the inner reality and sees deeply, and an ear that is sensitive and discerning of nuances.

If we reflect correctly, the harm caused by this group—who, with closed eyes and ears, imagine they are following Divine signs—is no less than the harm of enemies who knowingly strike at the foundations of the true religion; in fact, it is sometimes far greater.

Fundamentally, a conscious understanding of religion is the primary source of resistance, persistence, and steadfastness. Those with closed eyes and ears can easily be deceived, and by distorting religion, they can be diverted from the original path and led into the wilderness of disbelief, lack of faith, and misguidance.

Such individuals are tools in the hands of enemies and good prey for Satan. Only the deep-thinking, insightful, and hearing believers remain firm like a mountain and do not become playthings in the hands of others.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said: "They are insightful and not doubters" (Mustabsirin laysu bi-shakkak). "It means they take steps based on awareness, not out of doubt and hesitation!" [1]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 43.

The eleventh characteristic of these true believers is that they pay special attention to the upbringing of their children and families, and they feel an extraordinary responsibility toward them. "They constantly supplicate at the threshold of Allah, saying: 'Our Lord, grant us from among our wives and offspring those who will be the comfort of our eyes!'" (وَ الَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا هَبْ لَنا مِنْ أَزْواجِنا وَ ذُرِّيَّاتِنا قُرَّةَ أَعْيُنٍ).

Obviously, the intention is not that they merely sit in a corner and pray; rather, prayer is evidence of their inner longing and love for this matter, and the secret to their effort and striving.

Certainly, such individuals do not neglect whatever is in their power regarding the upbringing of their children and spouses and familiarizing them with the principles and branches of Islam and the paths of truth and justice. Where their reach falls short, they cling to the grace of the Lord and pray. Fundamentally, every correct prayer must be like this: striving to the extent of one's ability and praying for what lies beyond the boundaries of that ability.

"Qurrat 'ayn" is equivalent to "light of the eye" as we say in Persian, an allusion to someone who is a source of joy and happiness. This expression is originally derived from the word qurr (rhyming with hurr), which means coldness and coolness. Since it is well-known (and many commentators have explicitly stated) that tears of joy are always cool, while tears of grief and sorrow are hot and burning, qurrat 'ayn means something that causes a person's eye to become cool—that is, tears of joy flow from his eyes. This is a beautiful metaphor for joy and gladness.

"How many a cool eye was hot yesterday, and today eyes are cooled whose tears are flowing!" [1]

[^1]: The evidence for this is a poem that al-Qurtubi quoted in his commentary from one of the Arab poets: "How many a cool eye was hot yesterday * But today eyes are cooled whose tears are flowing!"

The issue of child-rearing, guiding the spouse, and the duty of fathers and mothers toward their children is among the most important issues emphasized by the Quran, and God willing, we will explain it under verse 6 of Surah al-Tahrim.

Finally, the twelfth prominent description of these sincere servants of Allah—which, from one perspective, is the most important of these attributes—is that they are never content with merely walking the path of truth themselves. Rather, their ambition is so high that they want to be leaders and pioneers for the community of believers and to invite others to this path as well.

Unlike reclusive and isolated ascetics, they do not merely pull their own rug out of the water; rather, their effort is to save the drowning! Therefore, at the end of the verse, it says: they are those who say: "Our Lord, make us leaders for the righteous" (وَ اجْعَلْنا لِلْمُتَّقِينَ إِماماً).

Again, it is necessary to note that they do not just pray to boastfully occupy the seats of the great; rather, they provide the means for greatness and leadership such that the worthy qualities of a true leader are gathered within them. This is a very difficult task with rigorous and heavy conditions.

Surely we have not forgotten that these verses do not describe the attributes of all believers, but rather explain the qualities of the elite group of believers who stand in the front rank under the title of "Ibad al-Rahman." Yes, they are the special servants of the All-Merciful, and just as Allah's general mercy encompasses everyone, the mercy of these servants of Allah is also general in some respects. Their knowledge, thought, speech, pen, wealth, and power always work in the path of guiding Allah's creation.

They are the models and examples for human society. They are considered patterns for the righteous. They are like guiding lights in the seas and deserts, calling the lost toward themselves and delivering them from sinking into whirlpools and falling into precipices.

In numerous narrations, we read that this verse was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. In another narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "This verse refers to us." [1] Without doubt, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام are among the clearest instances of the verse, but this does not prevent the expansion of the verse's concept so that other believers may also be leaders and pioneers for others in various spheres.

Some commentators have inferred from this verse that requesting spiritual, religious, and divine leadership is not only not blameworthy but is also desirable and sought after. [2] Additionally, it should be noted that the word "Imam," although singular, sometimes carries a plural meaning, and such is the case in the verse under discussion.

After completing these twelve attributes, the Quran refers to these special servants of Allah with all these characteristics and, in a brief summary, states their Divine reward as follows: "Those are the ones who will be rewarded with the high chambers [of Paradise] for what they patiently endured" (أُوْلئِكَ يُجْزَوْنَ الْغُرْفَةَ بِما صَبَرُوا).

"Ghurfah" is from the root gharf (rhyming with harf), meaning to pick up something and consume it. Ghurfah refers to something that is picked up and consumed (like water a person takes from a spring to drink). Later, it was applied to the upper parts of a building and the top floors of houses. Here, it is a metaphor for the highest dwellings of Paradise.

Since the "Ibad al-Rahman," possessing these attributes, are in the first rank of believers, their heavenly degree must also be the highest of degrees.

It is noteworthy that it says: this high station is given to them because they exercised patience and steadfastness in the way of Allah. It might be imagined that this is another of their attributes, but in reality, it is not a new attribute but rather the guarantor for the implementation of all the previous attributes. Is the servitude of the Lord, the struggle against the rebellion of desires, the abandonment of false witness, the acceptance of humility, and other such traits possible without patience and steadfastness?

This statement reminds one of the famous narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who said: "Patience and steadfastness are to faith as the head is to the body" (Wa al-sabru min al-imani ka al-ra'si min al-jasad), for the survival of the body depends on the survival of the head, as the command center for all limbs is located in the human brain.

Therefore, patience here has a broad meaning that includes endurance and steadfastness against the difficulties of the path of obedience to the Lord, jihad and struggle against rebellious whims, and standing firm against the factors of sin. If in some reports it is interpreted only as poverty and financial deprivation, it is certainly by way of mentioning an example.

Then it adds: "And they will be met therein with greetings and peace" (وَ يُلَقَّوْنَ فِيها تَحِيَّةً وَ سَلاماً).

The inhabitants of Paradise will offer greetings and peace to one another, the angels will offer them to them, and above all, Allah will offer them peace and greetings, as we read in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 58: "Peace!—a word from a Merciful Lord" (سَلامٌ قَوْلًا مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ). And in Surah Yunus, verses 23 and 24, we read: "And the angels will enter upon them from every gate, [saying], 'Peace be upon you'" (وَ الْمَلائِكَةُ يَدْخُلُونَ عَلَيْهمْ مِنْ كُلِّ بابٍ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ).

There is discussion among commentators as to whether "tahiyyah" and "salam" here have two different meanings or one. However, considering that "tahiyyah" originally means a prayer for the life and vitality of another, and "salam" is from the root of salamah (safety/health) and means a prayer for someone's well-being, we conclude that the first word is a request for life and the second is for that life to be coupled with safety—though sometimes these two words may carry the same meaning.

Of course, in common usage, "tahiyyah" has acquired a broader meaning, referring to any words spoken upon meeting someone that cause joy, respect, and an expression of affection toward them.

Then, for further emphasis, He says: "Abiding eternally therein. How excellent a settlement and residence!" (خالِدِينَ فِيها حَسُنَتْ مُسْتَقَرًّا وَ مُقاماً).

[^1]: These narrations have been brought in detail by Ali ibn Ibrahim and the author of Nur al-Thaqalayn in their commentaries under the verse in question. [^2]: Refer to the commentaries of al-Qurtubi and Fakhr al-Razi.

Ayah

قُلْ مَا يَعْبَؤُا۟ بِكُمْ رَبِّى لَوْلَا دُعَآؤُكُمْ ۖ فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزَامًۢا (٧٧)
Say, ‘Were it not for the sake of summoning you [to faith], what store my Lord would have set by you? But you impugned [me and my summons], so it will soon follow as a result.’ (77)

Commentary

If it were not for your prayer, you would have no value!

This verse, which is the final verse of Surah al-Furqan, is in fact a conclusion for the entire Surah, as well as for the discussions regarding the characteristics of the "Servants of the All-Merciful" (Ibad al-Rahman) mentioned in the preceding verses. Addressing the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it says: "Say, my Lord would not care for you were it not for your prayer" (قُلْ ما يَعْبَؤُا بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْ لا دُعاؤُكُمْ).

"Ya'ba'u" (يَعْبَؤُا) is derived from the root "aba" (matching the weight of 'abd), meaning weight or gravity. Therefore, the phrase "la ya'ba'u" means He does not grant weight [to someone], or in other words, He pays no heed.

Although many possibilities have been suggested regarding the meaning of "dua" (prayer/supplication) here, the roots of all of them essentially return to one principle.

Some have said "dua" carries its well-known meaning of supplicating.

Others have interpreted it as faith (iman), some as worship (ibadah) and monotheism (tawhid), some as gratitude (shukr), and some as calling upon God during hardships and calamities. However, the root of all these is faith and turning one's attention toward the Lord.

Thus, the concept of the verse becomes: That which gives you weight, value, and worth in the presence of Allah is your faith, your attention to the Lord, and your servitude to Him.

Then it adds: "But you have denied [the verses of the Lord and the Messengers of God], so [the consequence of] this denial will be inevitable and will never leave you" (فَقَدْ كَذَّبْتُمْ فَسَوْفَ يَكُونُ لِزاماً).

It might be imagined that there is a contradiction between the beginning and the end of the verse, or at least a lack of necessary connection and cohesion. However, with a little reflection, it becomes clear that the main point is this: In the past, you denied the signs of Allah and His Messengers; if you do not return to Allah and adopt the path of faith and servitude to Him, you will have no value or status before Him, and the punishments for your denial will certainly seize you [^1]. Among the clear evidences supporting this interpretation is a tradition narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام.

[^1]: The above verse is among those much discussed by commentators. What we have stated in its interpretation seems to be the clearest explanation. However, a group of famous commentators have mentioned another interpretation, summarized as follows: Allah pays no heed to you because you denied His signs, except for the fact that He invites you toward faith (according to this interpretation, "du'aukum" is an instance of adding the infinitive to the object, and its subject is a pronoun referring back to "my Lord." However, according to the interpretation we chose, "du'aukum" is an instance of adding the infinitive to the subject, and the apparent rule of adding an infinitive to a pronoun is that it refers to the subject unless evidence to the contrary is found). A third interpretation has also been mentioned here: the goal is to state that you, the human race—who have mostly chosen the path of denial—have no weight or value before Allah except for the sake of that minority like the "Servants of the All-Merciful" who come toward Allah and call upon Him with sincerity. (This interpretation, though correct in meaning and content, does not align well with the literal wording of the verse, as the pronouns in "du'aukum" and "kadhdhabtum" apparently refer to one group, not two) (Reflect on this).

He was asked: "Is reciting the Quran extensively superior, or supplicating (dua) extensively?" (كثرة القراءة افضل او كثرة الدعاء؟).

The Imam replied: "Supplicating extensively is superior," and then he recited the above verse (كثرة الدعا افضل و قرء هذه الاية) [^1].

Key Point: Prayer as a Path to Self-Building and Knowing God

We know that in the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, great importance is given to the matter of prayer (dua), an example of which is the verse above. However, accepting this might initially seem difficult for some, thinking that praying is a very easy task achievable by everyone, or they might go further and say prayer is the work of helpless individuals! That it holds no importance!

But the mistake arises from looking at prayer stripped of its conditions. If the specific conditions of prayer are taken into account, this truth is clearly proven: that prayer is an effective means for self-building and a close bond between man and Allah.

The first condition of prayer is knowing the One whom man calls upon.

Another condition is the washing of the heart and soul, and preparing the spirit to request from Him, for when a person goes to someone, they must be prepared for the meeting.

The third condition of prayer is seeking the pleasure and satisfaction of the One from whom man makes a request, for without it, the probability of an effect is very slim.

And finally, the fourth condition for the fulfillment of prayer is that man employs all his power, strength, and ability, exerting maximum effort and endeavor, and then raises his hands in prayer regarding what lies beyond that, turning his heart toward the Creator.

For it is explicitly stated in Islamic narrations that if a person neglects a task they can perform themselves and resorts to prayer, their prayer will not be answered! In this way, prayer is a means for knowing the Lord and His attributes of Beauty and Majesty, a means for repentance from sin and purification of the soul, a factor for performing good deeds, and a cause for struggle, effort, and striving to the utmost limit of one's ability.

For this reason, significant expressions regarding prayer are seen that cannot be understood except through what we have said. For example:

In a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Prayer is the weapon of the believer, the pillar of religion, and the light of the heavens and the earth" (الدعاء سلاح المؤمن، و عمود الدين، و نور السماوات و الارض) [^1]. In another tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "Prayer is the key to success and the tool of prosperity, and the best prayer is that which arises from a pure chest and a God-fearing heart" (الدعاء مفاتيح النجاح، و مقاليد الفلاح، و خير الدعاء ما صدر عن صدر نقى و قلب تقى) [^2]. And in a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Prayer is more piercing than the point of a spear!" (الدعاء انفذ من السنان) [^3].

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Safi" under the verse discussed—other commentaries have also narrated this tradition with slight variations. Other narrations mentioned in some of the Amali of the Sheikh and some from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim under the verse discussed also support the above interpretation. [^1], [^2], [^3]: Usul al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapters on Prayer—Chapter: Prayer is the Weapon of the Believer.

Beyond all this, events fundamentally occur in human life that plunge one into despair regarding apparent causes; prayer can be a window toward the hope of victory and an effective means for fighting despair and hopelessness.

For this reason, prayer during difficult and grueling events grants man power, strength, hope, and tranquility, and it has an undeniable psychological effect.

Regarding the issue of prayer, its philosophy, conditions, and results, we have discussed it in detail in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 186 of Surah al-Baqarah; please refer there for further explanation.

O Lord! Place us among Your special servants and grant us the success to acquire the characteristics of the "Servants of the All-Merciful." O Allah! Open the doors of prayer to us and make it the cause of our value in Your presence. O God! Grant us the success of prayers that are pleasing in Your sight and do not deprive us of their fulfillment; indeed, You are Powerful over all things and Worthy of responding.
